verses.explainer: His winnowing fork is in his hand to clear his threshing floor. He will gather the wheat into his barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire. The harvest metaphor makes clear that the coming one will separate the genuine from the false.

verses.text: Then with many other exhortations he preached good news to the people,
verses.explainer: With many other exhortations, John preaches good news to the people. Luke insists that even John's sharp, confrontational message is 'good news' -- because the truth that demands change is better than comfortable lies.

verses.text:
but Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him for Herodias, his brother’s
 wife, and for all the evil things which Herod had done,
verses.explainer: But Herod the tetrarch, rebuked by John for his marriage to Herodias and for all his evil deeds, responds with hostility.

verses.text: added this also to them all, that he shut up John in prison.
verses.explainer: Herod adds the worst deed of all: he locks John in prison. Luke mentions this early to clear John off the stage before Jesus' baptism -- in Luke's narrative, John and Jesus' active ministries do not overlap.

verses.text: Now when all the people were baptized, Jesus also had been baptized, and was praying. The sky was opened,
verses.explainer: When all the people are baptized, Jesus also is baptized. And while he is praying, heaven opens. Luke emphasizes that Jesus is praying at his baptism -- prayer at every key moment is a distinctive Lukan theme.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus' baptism marked his public commissioning for ministry. The heavens opening signaled a direct connection between God and earth being restored. In Jewish thought, heaven had been 'closed' -- there had been no prophetic voice for centuries.
thenVsNow.now: Christian baptism draws on this event as its model and source. The practice continues in virtually every Christian tradition as the initiation rite into the community of faith.

verses.text: and the Holy Spirit descended in a bodily form like a dove on him; and a voice came out of the sky, saying “You are my beloved Son. In you I am well pleased.”
verses.explainer: The Holy Spirit descends on him in bodily form like a dove, and a voice from heaven says: 'You are my beloved Son; in you I am well pleased.' All three persons of the Trinity are present: Father speaks, Spirit descends, Son is identified.

verses.text: Jesus himself, when he began to teach, was about thirty years old, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli,
verses.explainer: Jesus is about thirty years of age when he begins his ministry. Luke now presents Jesus' genealogy, tracing his lineage backward through history.

verses.text: the son of Matthat, the son of Levi, the son of Melchi, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph,
verses.explainer: The genealogy runs through Heli (or Eli), and continues backward. Unlike Matthew's forward-moving genealogy, Luke traces Jesus' line from present to past.

verses.text: the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Esli, the son of Naggai,
verses.explainer: The line continues through Mattathias, Amos, Nahum, Esli, and Naggai -- names largely unknown outside this genealogy.

verses.text: the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Joseph, the son of Judah,
verses.explainer: The genealogy passes through Maath, Mattathias, Semein, Josech, and Joda, continuing the backward progression through Israel's history.

verses.text: the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri,
verses.explainer: The line reaches Joanan, Rhesa, Zerubbabel, Shealtiel, and Neri. Zerubbabel was a post-exilic leader who helped rebuild the Temple after the Babylonian exile.

verses.text: the son of Melchi, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmodam, the son of Er,
verses.explainer: The genealogy continues through Melchi, Addi, Cosam, Elmadam, and Er.

verses.text: the son of Jose, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Matthat, the son of Levi,
verses.explainer: The line passes through Jesus (a different Jesus), Eliezer, Jorim, Matthat, and Levi.

verses.text: the son of Simeon, the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonan, the son of Eliakim,
verses.explainer: It continues through Simeon, Judah, Joseph, Jonam, and Eliakim.

verses.text: the son of Melea, the son of Menan, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David,
verses.explainer: The line reaches Melea, Menna, Mattatha, Nathan, and David. Luke traces the line through Nathan, David's son, rather than through Solomon as Matthew does. This may reflect Mary's lineage rather than Joseph's royal line.

verses.text: the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Salmon, the son of Nahshon,
verses.explainer: The genealogy continues through Jesse, Obed, Boaz, Salmon, and Nahshon -- figures from the book of Ruth and Israel's tribal history.

verses.text: the son of Amminadab, the son of Aram, the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah,
verses.explainer: The line passes through Amminadab, Admin, Arni, Hezron, and Perez, tracing back toward the patriarchal period.

verses.text: the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor,
verses.explainer: The genealogy reaches Judah, Jacob, Isaac, and Abraham -- the great patriarchs of Israel. At this point, Matthew's genealogy stops. Luke keeps going.

verses.text: the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah,
verses.explainer: Beyond Abraham, the line continues through Terah, Nahor, Serug, Reu, and Peleg, moving through the genealogies of Genesis 11.

verses.text: the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech,
verses.explainer: The genealogy passes through Eber, Shelah, Cainan, Arphaxad, and Shem -- figures from the post-flood genealogies of Genesis.

verses.text: the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalaleel, the son of Cainan,
verses.explainer: The line reaches Methuselah, Enoch, Jared, Mahalaleel, and Cainan -- the pre-flood patriarchs of Genesis 5.

verses.text: the son of Enos, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.
verses.explainer: The genealogy ends: 'the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.' By tracing Jesus all the way back to Adam, Luke makes a theological statement: Jesus is not just Israel's Messiah but the representative of all humanity. Adam was called 'son of God' -- Jesus is the true Son of God who will succeed where Adam failed.

thenVsNow.then: Matthew's genealogy starting with Abraham established Jesus' Jewish credentials. Luke's genealogy going back to Adam established Jesus' solidarity with the entire human race. For Luke's Gentile audience, this was essential: Jesus belongs to everyone.
thenVsNow.now: The two genealogies (Matthew and Luke) have generated centuries of scholarly discussion about their differences. The theological point transcends the historical puzzles: Jesus is connected to all of humanity, not just one ethnic group.

explainer.summary: Fresh from his baptism, Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness for forty days of temptation. He resists Satan with Scripture and returns to Galilee in the power of the Spirit. His first public act in Luke is reading from Isaiah in the Nazareth synagogue and declaring 'Today this Scripture is fulfilled.' The hometown crowd initially marvels, then turns violent when Jesus implies God's grace extends to Gentiles. He escapes, moves to Capernaum, and begins healing and casting out demons.
themes: Jesus overcomes temptation where Adam and Israel failed
The Spirit's power driving Jesus' ministry
Jesus' mission statement from Isaiah 61
Rejection by his own people
Authority over demons and disease
explainer.contextInBook: The temptation narrative shows Jesus succeeding where Israel failed during their wilderness wandering. The Nazareth sermon functions as a programmatic statement -- a preview of the entire Gospel in miniature. Jesus' rejection at Nazareth foreshadows the larger rejection that will lead to the cross.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Luke mentions the Holy Spirit more than any other
wordStudies.meaning: Luke mentions the Holy Spirit more than any other Synoptic Gospel. In chapter 4 alone, Jesus is 'full of the Spirit' (v.1), 'led by the Spirit' (v.1), returns 'in the power of the Spirit' (v.14), and reads that 'the Spirit of the Lord is upon me' (v.18). The Spirit is the driving force of everything Jesus does.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A key word in Luke
wordStudies.meaning: A key word in Luke. 'Today this Scripture is fulfilled' (4:21), 'Today salvation has come to this house' (19:9), 'Today you will be with me in paradise' (23:43). Luke emphasizes the present reality of salvation -- not just a future hope but a current experience.

verses.text: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returned from the Jordan, and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness
verses.explainer: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returns from the Jordan and is led by the Spirit in the wilderness for forty days, being tempted by the devil. The Spirit that descended at baptism now drives him into testing. Divine empowerment leads to immediate trial.

verses.text: for forty days, being tempted by the devil. He ate nothing in those days. Afterward, when they were completed, he was hungry.
verses.explainer: He eats nothing during those forty days, and when they are ended, he is hungry. The physical deprivation makes the temptations that follow more acute. Satan attacks when Jesus is at his weakest.

verses.text: The devil said to him, “If you are the Son of God, command this stone to become bread.”
verses.explainer: The devil says: 'If you are the Son of God, command this stone to become bread.' The temptation is to use divine power for personal comfort. It also subtly questions Jesus' identity -- 'if you are the Son of God.'

verses.text:
Jesus answered him, saying,
“It is written, ‘Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God.’”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 8:3: 'Man shall not live by bread alone.' Each of Jesus' responses comes from Deuteronomy, the book that records Israel's wilderness failures. Jesus fights with the same Scripture Israel should have relied on.

verses.text: The devil, leading him up on a high mountain, showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time.
verses.explainer: The devil takes Jesus up and shows him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time. The visual spectacle is designed to overwhelm -- every empire, every throne, every source of power displayed at once.

verses.text: The devil said to him, “I will give you all this authority, and their glory, for it has been delivered to me; and I give it to whomever I want.
verses.explainer: The devil offers all this authority and glory, claiming it has been delivered to him and he can give it to whomever he wishes. The claim that Satan has authority over the world's kingdoms is a sobering theological statement that Jesus does not dispute.

verses.text: If you therefore will worship before me, it will all be yours.”
verses.explainer: The price: worship the devil. All the power in the world in exchange for bowing to evil. It is the shortcut to the kingdom without the cross.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“Get behind me Satan! For it is written, ‘You shall worship the Lord your God, and you shall serve him only.’”
verses.explainer: Jesus responds with Deuteronomy 6:13: 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve.' The answer is absolute -- there is no negotiating with evil, even for a good outcome.

verses.text: He led him to Jerusalem, and set him on the pinnacle of the temple, and said to him, “If you are the Son of God, cast yourself down from here,
verses.explainer: The devil takes Jesus to the pinnacle of the Temple in Jerusalem and says: 'If you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here.' Now Satan quotes Scripture himself -- Psalm 91:11-12, about God's angels protecting the righteous.

verses.text:
for it is written,
‘He will put his angels in charge of you, to guard you;’
verses.explainer: Satan quotes: 'He will command his angels concerning you, to guard you.' The devil can cite Scripture accurately while distorting its meaning. Knowing the Bible is not the same as understanding it.

verses.text:
and,
‘On their hands they will bear you up,
lest perhaps you dash your foot against a stone.’”
verses.explainer: 'On their hands they will bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone.' Satan omits a key phrase from the psalm and weaponizes a promise of protection into a dare to test God.

verses.text:
Jesus answering, said to him,
“It has been said, ‘You shall not tempt the Lord your God.’”
verses.explainer: Jesus responds with Deuteronomy 6:16: 'You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.' Manufacturing a crisis to force God to act is not faith but presumption.

verses.text: When the devil had completed every temptation, he departed from him until another time.
verses.explainer: The devil departs from him 'until an opportune time.' The temptation is over but not finished permanently. Satan will return -- at Gethsemane, through Peter's denial, and supremely at the cross.

thenVsNow.then: Luke's note that Satan departed temporarily suggests an ongoing spiritual battle, not a one-time victory. Jesus' entire ministry takes place between this initial victory and the final confrontation at the cross.
thenVsNow.now: The idea that temptation is recurrent rather than once-and-done resonates with human experience. Spiritual victory in one moment does not guarantee freedom from future testing.

verses.text: Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee, and news about him spread through all the surrounding area.
verses.explainer: Jesus returns to Galilee in the power of the Spirit. News about him spreads through the whole region. The Spirit that sustained him in temptation now empowers him for ministry.

verses.text: He taught in their synagogues, being glorified by all.
verses.explainer: He teaches in their synagogues, being glorified by everyone. The initial reception is universally positive. That will not last.

verses.text: He came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up. He entered, as was his custom, into the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and stood up to read.
verses.explainer: Jesus comes to Nazareth, where he has been brought up. On the Sabbath, he goes to the synagogue 'as was his custom.' Regular synagogue attendance is part of his normal practice.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Synagogue worship', 'explanation': 'First-century synagogue services included prayers, readings from the Torah and the Prophets, and exposition. Any qualified Jewish male could be invited to read and comment. As a respected teacher, Jesus would naturally be asked to participate.'}

verses.text: The book of the prophet Isaiah was handed to him. He opened the book, and found the place where it was written,
verses.explainer: He is handed the scroll of the prophet Isaiah. He unrolls it and finds the passage he wants -- Isaiah 61:1-2. This is a deliberate choice, not a random reading.

verses.text:
“The Spirit of the Lord is on me,
because he has anointed me to preach good news to the poor.
He has sent me to heal the broken hearted,
to proclaim release to the captives,
recovering of sight to the blind,
to deliver those who are crushed,
verses.explainer: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor. He has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed.' This is Jesus' mission statement. Every element will be demonstrated in the Gospel that follows.

verses.text: and to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord.”
verses.explainer: 'To proclaim the year of the Lord's favor.' Jesus stops reading mid-sentence. Isaiah 61:2 continues with 'and the day of vengeance of our God.' Jesus reads the mercy part and stops before the judgment part. His mission is grace, not retribution.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Year of the Lord's favor", 'explanation': 'This alludes to the Year of Jubilee (Leviticus 25), when debts were cancelled, slaves freed, and land returned to original owners. It was a radical economic reset that was probably never fully practiced. Jesus claims to inaugurate the ultimate Jubilee.'}

verses.text: He closed the book, gave it back to the attendant, and sat down. The eyes of all in the synagogue were fastened on him.
verses.explainer: He rolls up the scroll, gives it back to the attendant, and sits down. The eyes of everyone in the synagogue are fixed on him. Sitting down was the posture for authoritative teaching. The tension is palpable.

verses.text:
He began to tell them,
“Today, this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.”
verses.explainer: 'Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.' One of the most dramatic declarations in the Gospels. Jesus does not say this text will be fulfilled someday -- it is happening right now, in this room, through him.

verses.text: All testified about him, and wondered at the gracious words which proceeded out of his mouth, and they said, “Isn’t this Joseph’s son?”
verses.explainer: All speak well of him and marvel at the gracious words coming from his mouth. Then the mood shifts: 'Is this not Joseph's son?' Familiarity breeds contempt. They cannot reconcile these extraordinary claims with the boy they watched grow up.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Doubtless you will tell me this parable, ‘Physician, heal yourself! Whatever we have heard done at Capernaum, do also here in your hometown.’”
verses.explainer: Jesus anticipates their objection: 'Physician, heal yourself. Do here in your hometown what we heard you did in Capernaum.' They want miracles as proof, not just words.

verses.text:
He said,
“Most certainly I tell you, no prophet is acceptable in his hometown.
verses.explainer: 'No prophet is accepted in his hometown.' Jesus states the principle plainly: the people who know you best are often the last to accept your calling.

verses.text: But truly I tell you, there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah, when the sky was shut up three years and six months, when a great famine came over all the land.
verses.explainer: Jesus cites Elijah, who was sent not to any Israelite widow during the famine but to a Gentile widow in Zarephath of Sidon. God sometimes bypasses his own people to bless outsiders.

verses.text: Elijah was sent to none of them, except to Zarephath, in the land of Sidon, to a woman who was a widow.
verses.explainer: Elijah was not sent to any of the many widows in Israel. The exclusion is pointed. God is not obligated to favor those who take him for granted.

verses.text: There were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha the prophet, yet not one of them was cleansed, except Naaman, the Syrian.”
verses.explainer: Similarly, Elisha healed Naaman the Syrian, not any of the many lepers in Israel. Again, a Gentile receives what Israel does not. Jesus is preparing his audience for the reality that God's grace extends beyond ethnic boundaries.

thenVsNow.then: By citing Gentile recipients of God's grace, Jesus challenged the assumption that Israel had an exclusive claim on God's favor. For a Nazareth synagogue audience, this was provocative and offensive.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God's grace is not limited to any one group -- ethnic, denominational, or cultural -- remains both foundational to Christianity and a source of tension whenever any group claims special access to God.

verses.text: They were all filled with wrath in the synagogue, as they heard these things.
verses.explainer: When they hear this, everyone in the synagogue is filled with rage. The same people who marveled at his words moments ago now want to kill him. The trigger is his suggestion that Gentiles can receive what Israel rejects.

verses.text: They rose up, threw him out of the city, and led him to the brow of the hill that their city was built on, that they might throw him off the cliff.
verses.explainer: They drive him out of the city and bring him to the brow of the hill on which their city is built, intending to throw him over the cliff. This is an attempted mob execution.

verses.text: But he, passing through the middle of them, went his way.
verses.explainer: But Jesus passes through the midst of them and goes away. Luke gives no explanation for how Jesus escapes. The implication is that his time has not yet come -- he cannot be killed before the appointed hour.

verses.text: He came down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee. He was teaching them on the Sabbath day,
verses.explainer: He goes down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and teaches them on the Sabbath. Rejected at home, Jesus establishes his base elsewhere.

verses.text: and they were astonished at his teaching, for his word was with authority.
verses.explainer: They are astonished at his teaching, because his word is with authority. Unlike Nazareth, Capernaum is receptive. The same message produces different responses depending on the audience.

verses.text: In the synagogue there was a man who had a spirit of an unclean demon, and he cried out with a loud voice,
verses.explainer: In the synagogue, a man has an unclean demon who cries out with a loud voice. Demons in Luke appear in sacred spaces -- the synagogue is not immune to evil.

verses.text: saying, “Ah! what have we to do with you, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know you who you are: the Holy One of God!”
verses.explainer: The demon says: 'What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are -- the Holy One of God!' The demon knows Jesus' identity and fears his power.

verses.text:
Jesus rebuked him, saying,
“Be silent, and come out of him!”
 When the demon had thrown him down in the middle of them, he came out of him, having done him no harm.
verses.explainer: Jesus rebukes the demon: 'Be silent and come out of him!' The demon throws the man down in their midst and comes out without hurting him. Jesus' authority is immediate and complete.

verses.text: Amazement came on all, and they spoke together, one with another, saying, “What is this word? For with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out!”
verses.explainer: Amazement seizes them all, and they discuss among themselves: 'What is this word? With authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out!' Jesus' authority is over both ideas (teaching) and realities (demons).

verses.text: News about him went out into every place of the surrounding region.
verses.explainer: Reports about him go out into every place in the surrounding region. The news network of the ancient world -- word of mouth -- spreads rapidly.

verses.text: He rose up from the synagogue, and entered into Simon’s house. Simon’s mother-in-law was afflicted with a great fever, and they begged him for her.
verses.explainer: He leaves the synagogue and enters Simon's house. Simon's mother-in-law is suffering from a high fever, and they ask Jesus about her.

verses.text: He stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her. Immediately she rose up and served them.
verses.explainer: Jesus stands over her and rebukes the fever, and it leaves her. Immediately she rises and serves them. Luke the physician notes the severity ('high fever') and the completeness of the cure.

verses.text: When the sun was setting, all those who had any sick with various diseases brought them to him; and he laid his hands on every one of them, and healed them.
verses.explainer: At sunset, all who have any sick with various diseases bring them to Jesus. He lays his hands on each one and heals them. The individual touch -- 'each one' -- shows personal care, not assembly-line healing.

verses.text: Demons also came out of many, crying out, and saying, “You are the Christ, the Son of God!” Rebuking them, he didn’t allow them to speak, because they knew that he was the Christ.
verses.explainer: Demons also come out of many, crying: 'You are the Son of God!' Jesus rebukes them and does not allow them to speak, because they know he is the Christ. Jesus controls the narrative about his identity.

verses.text: When it was day, he departed and went into an uninhabited place, and the multitudes looked for him, and came to him, and held on to him, so that he wouldn’t go away from them.
verses.explainer: At daybreak, Jesus departs to a desolate place. The crowds search for him and try to keep him from leaving. But Jesus will not be confined to one location.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus consistently withdrew from crowds to pray and seek God's direction. His popularity was never his goal; his mission was.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of withdrawal from public pressure for private renewal is widely recognized as essential for sustainable leadership. Jesus modeled what modern psychology calls the need for solitude and reflection.

verses.text:
But he said to them,
“I must preach the good news of God’s Kingdom to the other cities also. For this reason I have been sent.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'I must preach the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns as well, for I was sent for this purpose.' Jesus defines his mission: preaching the kingdom, not staying put.

verses.text: He was preaching in the synagogues of Galilee.
verses.explainer: He continues preaching in the synagogues of Judea. The mission expands outward from Capernaum to the broader region.

explainer.summary: Luke narrates the calling of Peter through a miraculous catch of fish, then records a series of healings and conflicts. Jesus cleanses a leper, heals a paralytic lowered through a roof (claiming authority to forgive sins), calls the tax collector Levi, and defends eating with sinners. He teaches that new wine requires new wineskins. The chapter establishes Jesus' authority over disease, sin, and social boundaries.
themes: The call to discipleship through overwhelming grace
Authority to forgive sins
Eating with sinners
New wine in new wineskins
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 5 parallels Mark 1-2 but with Luke's distinctive additions: the miraculous catch of fish and Peter's response of unworthiness. The conflict with religious leaders begins here and will intensify throughout the Gospel.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A title used only in Luke, meaning 'boss' or 'supe
wordStudies.meaning: A title used only in Luke, meaning 'boss' or 'supervisor.' It is less reverent than 'Lord' (kyrios) and more practical than 'Teacher' (didaskalos). Peter uses it when he still sees Jesus primarily as a respected authority figure, not yet as divine.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A Jewish movement committed to extending Temple ho
wordStudies.meaning: A Jewish movement committed to extending Temple holiness into everyday life through strict interpretation of Torah. They believed in resurrection, angels, and oral tradition. Luke portrays them as more complex than other Gospels -- sometimes hostile, sometimes interested, occasionally sympathetic.

verses.text: Now while the multitude pressed on him and heard the word of God, he was standing by the lake of Gennesaret.
verses.explainer: The crowd presses in on Jesus to hear the word of God as he stands by the lake of Gennesaret (the Sea of Galilee). His popularity is now so intense that the crowd physically pushes him toward the water.

verses.text: He saw two boats standing by the lake, but the fishermen had gone out of them, and were washing their nets.
verses.explainer: He sees two boats by the lake. The fishermen have left them and are washing their nets after a night of unsuccessful fishing.

verses.text: He entered into one of the boats, which was Simon’s, and asked him to put out a little from the land. He sat down and taught the multitudes from the boat.
verses.explainer: Jesus gets into Simon's boat and asks him to put out a little from shore. He sits down and teaches the crowds from the boat. The water provides distance and acts as a natural amplifier.

verses.text:
When he had finished speaking, he said to Simon,
“Put out into the deep, and let down your nets for a catch.”
verses.explainer: When he finishes speaking, Jesus tells Simon: 'Put out into the deep and let down your nets for a catch.' A carpenter's son telling a professional fisherman where to fish -- after the fisherman has already worked all night and caught nothing.

verses.text: Simon answered him, “Master, we worked all night, and took nothing; but at your word I will let down the net.”
verses.explainer: Simon answers: 'Master, we worked hard all night and caught nothing. But at your word I will let down the nets.' Peter's obedience despite his professional skepticism is the seed of discipleship.

thenVsNow.then: Night was the best time for fishing on the Sea of Galilee -- fish came to the surface to feed. If they caught nothing at night, daytime would be even less productive. Jesus' instruction defied fisherman's wisdom.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's response -- 'but at your word' -- has become a model for faith that acts despite experience or logic. The willingness to trust God's instruction over one's own expertise is central to Christian discipleship.

verses.text: When they had done this, they caught a great multitude of fish, and their net was breaking.
verses.explainer: When they do this, they catch so many fish that their nets begin to break. The abundance is overwhelming -- far beyond what any normal catch would produce.

verses.text: They beckoned to their partners in the other boat, that they should come and help them. They came, and filled both boats, so that they began to sink.
verses.explainer: They signal to their partners in the other boat to come help. Both boats fill with fish until they begin to sink. This is not an incremental blessing but an absurd, over-the-top abundance.

verses.text: But Simon Peter, when he saw it, fell down at Jesus’ knees, saying, “Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord.”
verses.explainer: When Simon Peter sees this, he falls at Jesus' knees saying: 'Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord!' Peter's reaction is not joy but terror. The miraculous catch reveals Jesus' power, and that power exposes Peter's unworthiness.

verses.text: For he was amazed, and all who were with him, at the catch of fish which they had caught;
verses.explainer: Amazement has seized Simon and all who are with him at the catch of fish. The awe is universal -- everyone in both boats recognizes this is beyond natural explanation.

verses.text:
and so also were James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon.
Jesus said to Simon,
“Don’t be afraid. From now on you will be catching people alive.”
verses.explainer: James and John, sons of Zebedee, are Simon's partners. Jesus says to Simon: 'Do not be afraid; from now on you will be catching people.' The fishing metaphor transforms their occupation into a calling.

annotations.word: James and John, sons of Zebedee

verses.text: When they had brought their boats to land, they left everything, and followed him.
verses.explainer: They bring the boats to land, leave everything, and follow him. They walk away from the biggest catch of their careers. The abundance that just filled their boats is left behind for something greater.

verses.text: While he was in one of the cities, behold, there was a man full of leprosy. When he saw Jesus, he fell on his face, and begged him, saying, “Lord, if you want to, you can make me clean.”
verses.explainer: In one of the cities, a man full of leprosy sees Jesus, falls on his face, and begs: 'Lord, if you will, you can make me clean.' He does not doubt Jesus' ability, only his willingness.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Full of leprosy', 'explanation': "Luke the physician specifies the severity -- 'full of' leprosy suggests an advanced case. The man would have been covered in lesions, completely excluded from society, and considered ritually defiling to anyone who touched him."}

verses.text:
He stretched out his hand, and touched him, saying,
“I want to. Be made clean.”
Immediately the leprosy left him.
verses.explainer: Jesus stretches out his hand and touches him, saying: 'I will; be clean.' Immediately the leprosy leaves him. Jesus reverses the expected contamination -- instead of becoming unclean through touch, Jesus makes the unclean man clean.

verses.text:
He commanded him to tell no one,
“But go your way, and show yourself to the priest, and offer for your cleansing according to what Moses commanded, for a testimony to them.”
verses.explainer: Jesus orders him to tell no one but to go show himself to the priest and offer the sacrifice Moses commanded, as a testimony to them. Jesus respects the existing religious system while transcending it.

verses.text: But the report concerning him spread much more, and great multitudes came together to hear, and to be healed by him of their infirmities.
verses.explainer: The news about Jesus spreads even more, and great crowds gather to hear him and to be healed of their infirmities. Despite the instruction to silence, the word gets out.

verses.text: But he withdrew himself into the desert, and prayed.
verses.explainer: But he withdraws to desolate places and prays. Luke again emphasizes Jesus' pattern of solitary prayer. The greater the public demand, the more Jesus seeks private communion with God.

verses.text: On one of those days, he was teaching; and there were Pharisees and teachers of the law sitting by, who had come out of every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem. The power of the Lord was with him to heal them.
verses.explainer: Pharisees and teachers of the law are sitting there, having come from every village of Galilee and Judea and from Jerusalem. The power of the Lord is present for him to heal. The gathering of religious authorities from across the land suggests an informal investigation.

annotations.word: Pharisees and teachers of the law

verses.text: Behold, men brought a paralyzed man on a cot, and they sought to bring him in to lay before Jesus.
verses.explainer: Men bring a paralyzed man on a bed, trying to bring him in and lay him before Jesus. They are determined to get their friend to the healer.

verses.text: Not finding a way to bring him in because of the multitude, they went up to the housetop, and let him down through the tiles with his cot into the middle before Jesus.
verses.explainer: Unable to find a way through the crowd, they go up on the roof and lower him through the tiles with his bed into the middle of the crowd, right in front of Jesus. Creative desperation overcomes every obstacle.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Lowered through the tiles', 'explanation': "Luke describes a tiled roof, reflecting a more Hellenistic architectural style than Mark's mud-and-branch roof. Luke may be adapting the detail for his Greek-speaking audience, or the house may have actually had a tiled roof."}

verses.text:
Seeing their faith, he said to him,
“Man, your sins are forgiven you.”
verses.explainer: When Jesus sees their faith, he says: 'Man, your sins are forgiven you.' He addresses the deeper need before the visible one. The friends' faith benefits the paralyzed man.

verses.text: The scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, “Who is this that speaks blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?”
verses.explainer: The scribes and Pharisees begin to question: 'Who is this who speaks blasphemy? Who can forgive sins but God alone?' Their theology is correct -- only God can forgive sins. The question is whether they are willing to follow that logic to its conclusion.

verses.text:
But Jesus, perceiving their thoughts, answered them,
“Why are you reasoning so in your hearts?
verses.explainer: Jesus perceives their thoughts and confronts them directly: 'Why do you question in your hearts?' He reads their internal objections before they voice them.

verses.text: Which is easier to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven you;’ or to say, ‘Arise and walk?’
verses.explainer: 'Which is easier, to say, Your sins are forgiven you, or to say, Rise up and walk?' Forgiveness is invisible and unverifiable; healing is immediately testable. Jesus will do the harder thing to prove the easier.

verses.text:
But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins” (he said to the paralyzed man),
“I tell you, arise, and take up your cot, and go to your house.”
verses.explainer: 'But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins...' He tells the paralyzed man to rise, take up his bed, and go home. The healing is evidence for the forgiveness.

verses.text: Immediately he rose up before them, and took up that which he was laying on, and departed to his house, glorifying God.
verses.explainer: Immediately the man rises before them, picks up what he was lying on, and goes home glorifying God. The healing is instantaneous and complete.

verses.text: Amazement took hold on all, and they glorified God. They were filled with fear, saying, “We have seen strange things today.”
verses.explainer: Amazement seizes them all, and they glorify God. They are filled with awe, saying: 'We have seen extraordinary things today.' Even the skeptical authorities cannot deny what they have witnessed.

verses.text:
After these things he went out, and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax office, and said to him,
“Follow me!”
verses.explainer: After this, Jesus goes out and sees a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax booth. He says: 'Follow me.' The call is identical to the call given to Peter, James, and John.

verses.text: He left everything, and rose up and followed him.
verses.explainer: Levi leaves everything, rises, and follows Jesus. He walks away from a lucrative but despised career. Unlike fishermen who might return to their boats, a tax collector who abandoned his post would not get it back.

verses.text: Levi made a great feast for him in his house. There was a great crowd of tax collectors and others who were reclining with them.
verses.explainer: Levi makes a great feast for Jesus at his house, and a large company of tax collectors and others are reclining at table with them. Levi introduces his social network to Jesus -- the only people who would socialize with a tax collector.

thenVsNow.then: Table fellowship was a powerful social statement. Sharing a meal meant accepting someone as an equal. For Jesus to recline at a tax collector's banquet was a public declaration of acceptance that scandalized the religious community.
thenVsNow.now: The question of who is welcome at the table -- literally and figuratively -- remains one of the most divisive issues in religious communities. Jesus' practice of eating with outcasts continues to challenge exclusionary boundaries.

verses.text: Their scribes and the Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, “Why do you eat and drink with the tax collectors and sinners?”
verses.explainer: The Pharisees and their scribes grumble against the disciples: 'Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?' The objection is about social contamination -- associating with sinners makes you unclean.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Those who are healthy have no need for a physician, but those who are sick do.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick.' Luke the physician records the most fitting metaphor for his author. Jesus defines his mission as healing, not policing.

verses.text: I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.”
verses.explainer: 'I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance.' The mission statement is clear: Jesus came for the broken, not the self-satisfied.

verses.text: They said to him, “Why do John’s disciples often fast and pray, likewise also the disciples of the Pharisees, but yours eat and drink?”
verses.explainer: They challenge: 'John's disciples fast often and pray, and so do the Pharisees' disciples, but yours eat and drink.' Jesus' group looks unserious compared to the ascetic practices of John and the Pharisees.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Can you make the friends of the bridegroom fast, while the bridegroom is with them?
verses.explainer: Jesus answers: 'Can you make wedding guests fast while the bridegroom is with them?' His presence is a celebration, not a mourning. Fasting would be inappropriate.

verses.text: But the days will come when the bridegroom will be taken away from them. Then they will fast in those days.”
verses.explainer: 'But the days will come when the bridegroom is taken away, and then they will fast in those days.' The first hint of Jesus' death in Luke's Gospel. The celebration will not last forever.

verses.text:
He also told a parable to them.
“No one puts a piece from a new garment on an old garment, or else he will tear the new, and also the piece from the new will not match the old.
verses.explainer: He tells a parable: no one tears a piece from a new garment to patch an old one. Otherwise, the new is ruined and the patch from the new does not match the old. What Jesus brings cannot simply be patched onto the existing religious system.

verses.text: No one puts new wine into old wine skins, or else the new wine will burst the skins, and it will be spilled, and the skins will be destroyed.
verses.explainer: No one puts new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the new wine will burst the skins and be spilled, and the skins will be ruined. New wine needs new containers.

verses.text: But new wine must be put into fresh wine skins, and both are preserved.
verses.explainer: New wine must be put into fresh wineskins. Jesus' message requires new structures, not old ones forced to accommodate something they cannot contain.

verses.text: No man having drunk old wine immediately desires new, for he says, ‘The old is better.’”
verses.explainer: Luke alone adds: 'No one after drinking old wine desires the new, for he says, The old is good.' This acknowledges the comfort of tradition. People naturally prefer what they are used to. Change is hard, even when the new is better.

thenVsNow.then: This verse may be Luke's honest assessment of why many Jews did not accept Jesus' message: the old religion was familiar and comfortable. Why change when the old seems perfectly fine?
thenVsNow.now: This verse resonates with any situation where tradition and innovation collide. Institutional inertia, the preference for the familiar, and resistance to change are not just religious phenomena -- they are deeply human.

explainer.summary: Two Sabbath controversies (plucking grain and healing a man's withered hand) escalate conflict with religious leaders. Jesus then spends a night in prayer before choosing twelve apostles. He descends to a level place and delivers the Sermon on the Plain -- Luke's parallel to Matthew's Sermon on the Mount. It includes blessings on the poor and woes on the rich, commands to love enemies, and the call not to judge. The sermon ends with the parable of building on rock versus sand.
themes: Jesus as Lord of the Sabbath
The choosing of the Twelve
Blessings and woes -- God's reversal of human values
Love of enemies as the distinctive Christian ethic
Judging others versus self-examination
explainer.contextInBook: The Sabbath conflicts bring opposition to a boiling point. Jesus' response is to solidify his movement: he appoints twelve apostles (symbolizing a new Israel) and lays out the ethical foundation of his kingdom in the Sermon on the Plain.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One sent with authority, an authorized representat
wordStudies.meaning: One sent with authority, an authorized representative. In Greco-Roman culture, an apostolos acted with the full authority of the sender. Jesus' twelve apostles are his authorized agents, commissioned to act on his behalf.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Not 'happy' in the modern sense but 'fortunate,' '
wordStudies.meaning: Not 'happy' in the modern sense but 'fortunate,' 'to be envied,' or 'in the right place before God.' The beatitudes are not descriptions of pleasant feelings but declarations of divine favor that contradict worldly measures of success.

verses.text: Now on the second Sabbath after the first, he was going through the grain fields. His disciples plucked the heads of grain and ate, rubbing them in their hands.
verses.explainer: On a Sabbath, Jesus is going through grain fields. His disciples pluck and eat heads of grain, rubbing them in their hands. The rubbing is the detail that the Pharisees latch onto -- it could be classified as 'threshing,' a form of work forbidden on the Sabbath.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Plucking grain on the Sabbath', 'explanation': "Deuteronomy 23:25 allowed travelers to pluck grain by hand from another's field. The Pharisees' issue was not theft but Sabbath violation. Their oral tradition classified plucking as 'reaping' and rubbing as 'threshing' -- two of the 39 prohibited categories of work."}

verses.text: But some of the Pharisees said to them, “Why do you do that which is not lawful to do on the Sabbath day?”
verses.explainer: Some of the Pharisees say: 'Why are you doing what is not lawful on the Sabbath?' The challenge is a legal accusation, not a casual question.

verses.text:
Jesus, answering them, said,
“Haven’t you read what David did when he was hungry, he, and those who were with him;
verses.explainer: Jesus responds by citing David, who entered the house of God and ate the bread of the Presence, which was lawful only for priests, and gave some to his companions. If David could override ritual law in time of need, so can Jesus' disciples.

verses.text: how he entered into God’s house, and took and ate the show bread, and gave also to those who were with him, which is not lawful to eat except for the priests alone?”
verses.explainer: He took and ate the bread of the Presence, and gave it to those with him -- bread that is not lawful for any but the priests to eat. The parallel is clear: if Israel's king could override the rules, so can Israel's greater king.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.”
verses.explainer: 'The Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.' Jesus claims authority over the institution God established at creation. This is not a modest claim -- it places Jesus above the Torah itself.

thenVsNow.then: Claiming lordship over the Sabbath was effectively claiming to be God, since God instituted the Sabbath. For the Pharisees, whose entire program was organized around Sabbath observance, this was an existential challenge.
thenVsNow.now: The relationship between rest, work, and spiritual practice continues to evolve. The principle that institutions exist to serve people, not the reverse, remains widely applicable beyond religious contexts.

verses.text: It also happened on another Sabbath that he entered into the synagogue and taught. There was a man there, and his right hand was withered.
verses.explainer: On another Sabbath, Jesus enters the synagogue and teaches. A man is there whose right hand is withered. Luke the physician specifies which hand -- a detail the other Gospels omit.

verses.text: The scribes and the Pharisees watched him, to see whether he would heal on the Sabbath, that they might find an accusation against him.
verses.explainer: The scribes and Pharisees watch Jesus to see whether he will heal on the Sabbath, so they can find a reason to accuse him. They are not there to worship; they are there to gather evidence.

verses.text:
But he knew their thoughts; and he said to the man who had the withered hand,
“Rise up, and stand in the middle.” He arose and stood.
verses.explainer: Jesus knows their thoughts and tells the man with the withered hand: 'Rise and stand in the middle.' He forces the confrontation into public view. Nothing will be hidden.

verses.text:
Then Jesus said to them,
“I will ask you something: Is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good, or to do harm? To save a life, or to kill?”
verses.explainer: 'I ask you, is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm, to save life or to destroy it?' Jesus reframes the debate. The question is not whether healing counts as work but whether refusing to heal is itself a violation of the Sabbath's purpose.

verses.text:
He looked around at them all, and said to the man,
“Stretch out your hand.” He did, and his hand was restored as sound as the other.
verses.explainer: He looks around at them all and says to the man: 'Stretch out your hand.' He does, and his hand is restored. The healing involves no physical act of 'work' -- just a word. There is nothing to prosecute.

verses.text: But they were filled with rage, and talked with one another about what they might do to Jesus.
verses.explainer: They are filled with fury and discuss what they might do to Jesus. The religious leaders' rage at a healing on the Sabbath reveals how badly their priorities have become distorted.

verses.text: In these days, he went out to the mountain to pray, and he continued all night in prayer to God.
verses.explainer: In these days, Jesus goes out to the mountain to pray, and he spends all night in prayer to God. Before the most important decision of his ministry -- choosing the Twelve -- Jesus prays through the entire night. Luke highlights prayer at every critical juncture.

thenVsNow.then: All-night prayer was not standard Jewish practice. It indicates the gravity of the decision and Jesus' total dependence on God's guidance for choosing the foundation of his movement.
thenVsNow.now: Extended prayer before major decisions is a practice many Christians emulate, drawn from this example. The principle that consequential choices deserve proportional preparation applies broadly.

verses.text: When it was day, he called his disciples, and from them he chose twelve, whom he also named apostles:
verses.explainer: When day comes, he calls his disciples and chooses twelve of them, whom he names 'apostles.' The term 'apostle' means 'sent one' -- these are not just students but authorized representatives.

verses.text: Simon, whom he also named Peter; Andrew, his brother; James; John; Philip; Bartholomew;
verses.explainer: Simon, whom he names Peter, and Andrew his brother; James and John; Philip and Bartholomew. Peter is named first in every New Testament list of apostles, reflecting his leadership role.

verses.text: Matthew; Thomas; James, the son of Alphaeus; Simon, who was called the Zealot;
verses.explainer: Matthew and Thomas; James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot. The group includes a tax collector (Matthew) and a Zealot (Simon) -- a Roman collaborator and a revolutionary nationalist sitting side by side.

verses.text: Judas the son of James; and Judas Iscariot, who also became a traitor.
verses.explainer: Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor. The list ends ominously. Even in the founding act, betrayal is foreshadowed.

verses.text: He came down with them, and stood on a level place, with a crowd of his disciples, and a great number of the people from all Judea and Jerusalem, and the sea coast of Tyre and Sidon, who came to hear him and to be healed of their diseases;
verses.explainer: Jesus comes down with them and stands on a level place, with a great crowd of disciples and a great multitude of people from all Judea, Jerusalem, and the coast of Tyre and Sidon. Luke's sermon happens on a plain, not a mountain.

verses.text: as well as those who were troubled by unclean spirits, and they were being healed.
verses.explainer: They come to hear him and to be healed. Those troubled by unclean spirits are cured. Teaching and healing go together -- the gospel is for the whole person.

verses.text: All the multitude sought to touch him, for power came out of him and healed them all.
verses.explainer: The whole crowd tries to touch him, because power comes out from him and heals them all. Jesus' healing power is described almost as a physical force emanating from his person.

verses.text:
He lifted up his eyes to his disciples, and said,
“Blessed are you who are poor,
God’s Kingdom is yours.
verses.explainer: He lifts up his eyes on his disciples and says: 'Blessed are you who are poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.' Luke's version of the beatitudes is directed at real, material poverty -- not Matthew's 'poor in spirit.' This is a declaration that God's kingdom belongs to those who have nothing.

thenVsNow.then: In a world where wealth was considered a sign of God's blessing and poverty was seen as divine punishment, Jesus' declaration inverted everything. The poor are blessed not because poverty is good but because God's kingdom operates by opposite rules.
thenVsNow.now: Luke's beatitudes continue to challenge prosperity theology and any religious framework that equates wealth with divine favor. They are central to liberation theology and Christian social ethics.

verses.text:
Blessed are you who hunger now,
for you will be filled.
Blessed are you who weep now,
for you will laugh.
verses.explainer: 'Blessed are you who are hungry now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh.' The reversals are temporal -- what is now will not always be. Present suffering will give way to future fulfillment.

verses.text: Blessed are you when men shall hate you, and when they shall exclude and mock you, and throw out your name as evil, for the Son of Man’s sake.
verses.explainer: 'Blessed are you when people hate you, exclude you, revile you, and cast out your name as evil on account of the Son of Man.' Persecution for following Jesus is not a curse but a badge of honor.

verses.text: Rejoice in that day, and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven, for their fathers did the same thing to the prophets.
verses.explainer: 'Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, for your reward is great in heaven. Their fathers did the same things to the prophets.' The disciples join a long line of persecuted prophets. Being rejected for faithfulness is a tradition.

verses.text:
“But woe to you who are rich!
For you have received your consolation.
verses.explainer: 'But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation.' Luke includes woes that Matthew omits. The rich have already received their reward -- comfort in this life is their only comfort.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Woes on the rich', 'explanation': "Luke's woes are unique among the Gospels and reflect his consistent concern with economic justice. They do not say wealth is sinful in itself but that those who find their security and satisfaction in wealth have nothing further to expect from God."}

verses.text:
Woe to you, you who are full now,
for you will be hungry.
Woe to you who laugh now,
for you will mourn and weep.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you who are full now, for you will be hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.' The comfortable and the complacent face future reversal.

verses.text:
Woe,
when
men speak well of you,
for their fathers did the same thing to the false prophets.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you when all people speak well of you, for their fathers did the same to the false prophets.' Universal popularity is a warning sign, not a badge of honor. The false prophets were the ones everybody liked.

verses.text: “But I tell you who hear: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,
verses.explainer: 'But I say to you who hear: love your enemies, do good to those who hate you.' The most radical command in the sermon. Enemy-love is not a feeling but an action -- doing good to those who actively oppose you.

thenVsNow.then: In a culture governed by honor and shame, reciprocity, and revenge, loving enemies was not merely difficult but socially incomprehensible. It overturned the entire honor-revenge system that governed social relations.
thenVsNow.now: Enemy-love remains the most distinctive and challenging ethical teaching of Christianity. It has inspired movements from Franciscan poverty to Gandhian nonviolence to Martin Luther King Jr.'s civil rights campaigns.

verses.text: bless those who curse you, and pray for those who mistreat you.
verses.explainer: 'Bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you.' The response to hostility is not retaliation but blessing. This requires supernatural transformation, not natural capacity.

verses.text: To him who strikes you on the cheek, offer also the other; and from him who takes away your cloak, don’t withhold your coat also.
verses.explainer: 'To one who strikes you on the cheek, offer the other also. From one who takes away your cloak, do not withhold your tunic either.' Nonresistance to violence and generosity beyond what is demanded. Jesus is describing a life freed from the cycle of retaliation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Turning the other cheek', 'explanation': 'A slap on the cheek was an insult, not an attempt to injure. Offering the other cheek was not passive submission but a refusal to be degraded -- it forced the aggressor to treat you as an equal. Some scholars see this as creative nonviolent resistance rather than passive acceptance of abuse.'}

verses.text: Give to everyone who asks you, and don’t ask him who takes away your goods to give them back again.
verses.explainer: 'Give to everyone who begs from you, and from one who takes away your goods, do not demand them back.' Radical generosity with no expectation of return. The kingdom ethic is one of open-handedness.

verses.text: “As you would like people to do to you, do exactly so to them.
verses.explainer: 'As you wish that others would do to you, do so to them.' The Golden Rule in its positive formulation. Most ancient ethical systems stated it negatively ('do not do to others what you would not want done to you'). Jesus puts it in active terms -- initiative, not just restraint.

verses.text: If you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.
verses.explainer: 'If you love those who love you, what benefit is that? Even sinners love those who love them.' Loving people who love you back is not virtue -- it is nature. Any human being does that.

verses.text: If you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.
verses.explainer: 'If you do good to those who do good to you, what benefit? Even sinners do the same.' Reciprocal goodness is the baseline of human society, not the height of divine ethics.

verses.text: If you lend to those from whom you hope to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, to receive back as much.
verses.explainer: 'If you lend to those from whom you expect to receive, what credit is that? Even sinners lend to sinners to receive as much back.' Kingdom economics goes beyond reciprocity.

verses.text: But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing back; and your reward will be great, and you will be children of the Most High; for he is kind toward the unthankful and evil.
verses.explainer: 'Love your enemies, do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return. Your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and the evil.' The reason for enemy-love: it is what God does. Loving enemies makes you like God, who is kind even to those who are ungrateful and evil.

verses.text:
“Therefore be merciful,
even as your Father is also merciful.
verses.explainer: 'Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful.' Where Matthew says 'Be perfect,' Luke says 'Be merciful.' Mercy is Luke's summary of the divine character and the standard for human behavior.

verses.text:
Don’t judge,
and you won’t be judged.
Don’t condemn,
and you won’t be condemned.
Set free,
and you will be set free.
verses.explainer: 'Judge not, and you will not be judged; condemn not, and you will not be condemned; forgive, and you will be forgiven.' A chain of reciprocity: what you give is what you get.

verses.text:
“Give, and it will be given to you: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, and running over, will be given to you.
For with the same measure you measure it will be measured back to you.”
verses.explainer: 'Give, and it will be given to you. Good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, will be put into your lap.' The image is of grain measured so generously that it overflows. God's response to generosity is even greater generosity.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Pressed down, shaken together, running over', 'explanation': 'This describes how grain was measured in the marketplace. An honest (or generous) merchant would press the grain down and shake the container to fit more, then heap it until it overflowed. It is an image of extravagant, unmistakable generosity.'}

verses.text:
He spoke a parable to them.
“Can the blind guide the blind? Won’t they both fall into a pit?
verses.explainer: He tells them a parable: 'Can a blind man lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?' The parable targets those who presume to guide others without seeing clearly themselves.

verses.text: A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when he is fully trained will be like his teacher.
verses.explainer: 'A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone when fully trained will be like his teacher.' The goal of discipleship is to become like the master. The quality of the teacher determines the quality of the student.

verses.text: Why do you see the speck of chaff that is in your brother’s eye, but don’t consider the beam that is in your own eye?
verses.explainer: 'Why do you see the speck that is in your brother's eye, but do not notice the log that is in your own eye?' The absurd image of someone with a log in their eye trying to remove a speck from someone else's. Self-deception about one's own faults is universal.

verses.text: Or how can you tell your brother, ‘Brother, let me remove the speck of chaff that is in your eye,’ when you yourself don’t see the beam that is in your own eye? You hypocrite! First remove the beam from your own eye, and then you can see clearly to remove the speck of chaff that is in your brother’s eye.
verses.explainer: 'How can you say to your brother, Let me take out the speck in your eye, when you yourself do not see the log in your own eye? Hypocrite! First remove the log from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck in your brother's eye.' Self-examination must precede correction of others.

thenVsNow.then: The label 'hypocrite' (hypokrites) originally meant an actor wearing a mask on stage. Jesus uses it for people who perform righteousness while hiding their own flaws.
thenVsNow.now: The speck-and-log image remains one of the most widely quoted teachings of Jesus. It is used across cultures to challenge judgmentalism and promote self-awareness.

verses.text: For there is no good tree that produces rotten fruit; nor again a rotten tree that produces good fruit.
verses.explainer: 'No good tree bears bad fruit, nor does a bad tree bear good fruit.' Character is revealed by output. The external life reveals the internal reality.

verses.text: For each tree is known by its own fruit. For people don’t gather figs from thorns, nor do they gather grapes from a bramble bush.
verses.explainer: 'Each tree is known by its own fruit. Figs are not gathered from thornbushes, nor are grapes picked from brambles.' You identify a plant by what it produces. You identify a person the same way.

verses.text: The good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings out that which is good, and the evil man out of the evil treasure of his heart brings out that which is evil, for out of the abundance of the heart, his mouth speaks.
verses.explainer: 'The good person out of the good treasure of his heart produces good, and the evil person out of his evil treasure produces evil, for out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.' Words reveal the heart. Speech is the overflow of character.

verses.text: “Why do you call me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ and don’t do the things which I say?
verses.explainer: 'Why do you call me Lord, Lord, and not do what I tell you?' The gap between verbal confession and actual obedience is the central challenge of discipleship.

verses.text: Everyone who comes to me, and hears my words, and does them, I will show you who he is like.
verses.explainer: 'Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and does them, I will show you what he is like.' Jesus is about to give the definitive image of genuine versus superficial discipleship.

verses.text: He is like a man building a house, who dug and went deep, and laid a foundation on the rock. When a flood arose, the stream broke against that house, and could not shake it, because it was founded on the rock.
verses.explainer: 'He is like a man building a house, who dug deep and laid the foundation on the rock. And when a flood arose, the stream broke against that house and could not shake it, because it had been well built.' Obedience to Jesus' teaching creates a life that withstands crisis.

verses.text: But he who hears, and doesn’t do, is like a man who built a house on the earth without a foundation, against which the stream broke, and immediately it fell, and the ruin of that house was great.”
verses.explainer: 'But the one who hears and does not act is like a man who built a house on the ground without a foundation. When the stream broke against it, immediately it collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great.' Hearing without doing creates the illusion of security that catastrophe will expose. The sermon ends with a stark choice: build on obedience or face ruin.

thenVsNow.then: In the arid climate of Palestine, building in a dry riverbed seemed safe most of the year. But when the rains came, flash floods could destroy anything in their path. The builder on sand chose short-term convenience over long-term security.
thenVsNow.now: The parable applies to any situation where people hear good advice, even agree with it, but fail to act on it. The difference between knowing what is right and doing what is right is the difference between a foundation of rock and no foundation at all.

explainer.summary: Luke 7 showcases Jesus' compassion reaching across every social boundary. A Roman centurion's extraordinary faith leads to healing without Jesus even entering his house. A widow's dead son is raised at the gate of Nain. John the Baptist, now imprisoned, sends messengers to ask if Jesus is really the one. Jesus defends John's ministry while distinguishing the new era of the kingdom. The chapter climaxes with a sinful woman weeping at Jesus' feet in a Pharisee's house, demonstrating that those who have been forgiven much love much.
themes: Faith that transcends ethnic boundaries
Compassion for the grieving and marginalized
Jesus as the fulfillment of prophetic expectations
Forgiveness and love as inseparable realities
Table fellowship as theological drama
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 7 develops Luke's portrait of Jesus as the universal Savior. A Gentile soldier, a widow, a disgraced woman -- the people Jesus helps are those the religious system cannot or will not reach. The chapter also addresses the relationship between Jesus and John the Baptist, clarifying that Jesus' ministry looks different from what John expected.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A Roman military officer commanding approximately
wordStudies.meaning: A Roman military officer commanding approximately 100 soldiers. Centurions in the New Testament are consistently portrayed positively -- this one, the centurion at the cross, and Cornelius in Acts. They represent the best of Roman discipline and honor.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One who speaks on behalf of God
wordStudies.meaning: One who speaks on behalf of God. In this chapter, Jesus is recognized as a prophet by the crowd at Nain (7:16) and the question is raised whether he is a true prophet by Simon the Pharisee (7:39). Luke presents Jesus as the ultimate prophet -- greater than John, greater than any before him.

verses.text: After he had finished speaking in the hearing of the people, he entered into Capernaum.
verses.explainer: After finishing the Sermon on the Plain, Jesus enters Capernaum. The teaching is complete; now comes the demonstration of its principles in action.

verses.text: A certain centurion’s servant, who was dear to him, was sick and at the point of death.
verses.explainer: A centurion's servant, whom the centurion values highly, is sick and near death. Luke notes the centurion's affection for his servant -- unusual in a world where slaves were considered property.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Roman centurion in Capernaum', 'explanation': "Capernaum sat on the Via Maris trade route and had a Roman military presence. This centurion had apparently built the local synagogue (v.5), indicating both wealth and unusual respect for Jewish religion. He represents a category of Gentiles called 'God-fearers' who worshiped Israel's God without full conversion."}

verses.text: When he heard about Jesus, he sent to him elders of the Jews, asking him to come and save his servant.
verses.explainer: The centurion sends Jewish elders to Jesus, asking him to come heal his servant. He approaches through intermediaries -- a sign of humility and cultural sensitivity.

verses.text: When they came to Jesus, they begged him earnestly, saying, “He is worthy for you to do this for him,
verses.explainer: The elders plead earnestly: 'He is worthy of having you do this for him.' They advocate for a Gentile, which was unusual. His generosity has earned genuine Jewish respect.

verses.text: for he loves our nation, and he built our synagogue for us.”
verses.explainer: 'He loves our nation, and he is the one who built us our synagogue.' A Roman officer who loves Israel and invests in its religious institutions. He defies every stereotype of the occupying enemy.

thenVsNow.then: For a Roman soldier to build a Jewish synagogue was extraordinary. It demonstrated genuine religious conviction, not mere political expediency. The Jewish elders' willingness to intercede for him shows that personal relationships could transcend the hostility between occupier and occupied.
thenVsNow.now: The centurion models a faith that crosses cultural and political divides. His story challenges assumptions about who can have genuine faith and who remains outside God's reach.

verses.text: Jesus went with them. When he was now not far from the house, the centurion sent friends to him, saying to him, “Lord, don’t trouble yourself, for I am not worthy for you to come under my roof.
verses.explainer: Jesus goes with them, but when he is not far from the house, the centurion sends friends with a message: 'Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy to have you come under my roof.'

verses.text: Therefore I didn’t even think myself worthy to come to you; but say the word, and my servant will be healed.
verses.explainer: 'Therefore I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and let my servant be healed.' The centurion believes Jesus can heal remotely, with just a command. This level of faith in Jesus' authority is unprecedented in Luke's Gospel.

verses.text: For I also am a man placed under authority, having under myself soldiers. I tell this one, ‘Go!’ and he goes; and to another, ‘Come!’ and he comes; and to my servant, ‘Do this,’ and he does it.”
verses.explainer: 'For I too am a man set under authority, with soldiers under me. I say to one, Go, and he goes; and to another, Come, and he comes; and to my servant, Do this, and he does it.' The centurion reasons from his own experience of authority: if his word commands obedience, how much more does Jesus' word command reality itself.

thenVsNow.then: The centurion's logic is military: authority works through chains of command. He perceives that Jesus has authority over illness and death in the same way a commander has authority over soldiers. The word is enough.
thenVsNow.now: The centurion's words have been adapted into Christian liturgy worldwide. In the Catholic Mass, communicants pray: 'Lord, I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof, but only say the word and my soul shall be healed.'

verses.text:
When Jesus heard these things, he marveled at him, and turned and said to the multitude who followed him,
“I tell you, I have not found such great faith, no, not in Israel.”
verses.explainer: Jesus marvels at the centurion and says to the crowd: 'I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such faith.' A Gentile soldier has outstripped all of Israel in faith. This is a stunning and provocative declaration.

verses.text: Those who were sent, returning to the house, found that the servant who had been sick was well.
verses.explainer: When the messengers return to the house, they find the servant well. The healing happened at a distance, through a word, without Jesus entering the house. Authority needs no proximity.

verses.text: Soon afterwards, he went to a city called Nain. Many of his disciples, along with a great multitude, went with him.
verses.explainer: Soon afterward, Jesus goes to a town called Nain, and his disciples and a great crowd go with him. Nain is a small village about 25 miles southwest of Capernaum.

verses.text: Now when he came near to the gate of the city, behold, one who was dead was carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow. Many people of the city were with her.
verses.explainer: As he approaches the gate of the town, a dead man is being carried out -- the only son of his mother, and she is a widow. A considerable crowd from the town is with her.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "A widow's only son", 'explanation': 'In a society with no social safety net, a widow who lost her only son lost everything -- her provider, her legal standing, her future. She would be among the most destitute and vulnerable people in the community. She faced not just grief but economic ruin.'}

verses.text:
When the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her, and said to her,
“Don’t cry.”
verses.explainer: When the Lord sees her, he has compassion on her and says: 'Do not weep.' No one asks Jesus to intervene. He acts purely out of compassion when he sees her grief.

verses.text:
He came near and touched the coffin, and the bearers stood still. He said,
“Young man, I tell you, arise!”
verses.explainer: He comes forward and touches the bier, and the bearers stand still. He says: 'Young man, I say to you, arise.' Touching a bier made one ritually unclean, but Jesus' touch reverses death rather than contracting impurity.

verses.text: He who was dead sat up, and began to speak. And he gave him to his mother.
verses.explainer: The dead man sits up and begins to speak, and Jesus gives him to his mother. The phrase 'gave him to his mother' deliberately echoes the Elijah story in 1 Kings 17:23, where Elijah 'gave him to his mother.'

verses.text: Fear took hold of all, and they glorified God, saying, “A great prophet has arisen among us!” and, “God has visited his people!”
verses.explainer: Fear seizes them all, and they glorify God, saying: 'A great prophet has arisen among us!' and 'God has visited his people!' The crowd recognizes prophetic power and divine presence but has not yet grasped who Jesus truly is.

verses.text: This report went out concerning him in the whole of Judea, and in all the surrounding region.
verses.explainer: This report about him spreads through all Judea and the surrounding country. The raising of the dead becomes front-page news across the region.

verses.text: The disciples of John told him about all these things.
verses.explainer: John's disciples report all these things to him in prison. John has been hearing about Jesus' ministry from his cell and wants to know if his own mission was fulfilled.

verses.text: John, calling to himself two of his disciples, sent them to Jesus, saying, “Are you the one who is coming, or should we look for another?”
verses.explainer: John sends two of his disciples to ask Jesus: 'Are you the one who is to come, or shall we look for another?' This is not necessarily doubt -- it may be John wanting confirmation for his own disciples, or it may reflect John's expectation of a more fiery, judgmental Messiah.

verses.text: When the men had come to him, they said, “John the Baptizer has sent us to you, saying, ‘Are you he who comes, or should we look for another?’”
verses.explainer: The men arrive and deliver John's question. They identify themselves as John the Baptist's messengers.

verses.text: In that hour he cured many of diseases and plagues and evil spirits; and to many who were blind he gave sight.
verses.explainer: At that very hour, Jesus heals many diseases, afflictions, and evil spirits, and gives sight to many who are blind. Jesus does not answer with words first -- he answers with actions.

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Go and tell John the things which you have seen and heard: that the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised up, and the poor have good news preached to them.
verses.explainer: Jesus tells them: 'Go and tell John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news preached to them.' Every item on this list comes from Isaiah's messianic prophecies.

verses.text: Blessed is he who finds no occasion for stumbling in me.”
verses.explainer: 'Blessed is the one who is not offended by me.' A gentle warning: Jesus' ministry does not look like what John (or anyone) expected. The Messiah is healing and teaching, not wielding an axe of judgment.

verses.text:
When John’s messengers had departed, he began to tell the multitudes about John,
“What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?
verses.explainer: After John's messengers leave, Jesus speaks to the crowds about John: 'What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken by the wind?' Jesus defends John's courage -- he is no spineless figure bending to popular opinion.

verses.text: But what did you go out to see? A man clothed in soft clothing? Behold, those who are gorgeously dressed, and live delicately, are in kings’ courts.
verses.explainer: 'What then did you go out to see? A man dressed in soft clothing? Those who wear fine clothes and live in luxury are in palaces.' John's rough wilderness lifestyle was deliberate -- he was not seeking comfort.

verses.text: But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and much more than a prophet.
verses.explainer: 'What then? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet.' Jesus elevates John above the prophetic category. He is the forerunner, the one who prepared the way.

verses.text:
This is he of whom it is written,
‘Behold, I send my messenger before your face,
who will prepare your way before you.’
