verses.explainer: 'This is the one of whom it is written: Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who will prepare your way before you.' Jesus applies Malachi 3:1 to John, confirming his role as the prophesied forerunner.

verses.text: “For I tell you, among those who are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptizer, yet he who is least in God’s Kingdom is greater than he.”
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, among those born of women none is greater than John. Yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he.' John is the greatest figure of the old era, but the new era of the kingdom surpasses even John's greatness.

verses.text: When all the people and the tax collectors heard this, they declared God to be just, having been baptized with John’s baptism.
verses.explainer: All the people and the tax collectors, having been baptized with John's baptism, declared God's justice. Those who accepted John's message recognized that Jesus' assessment of John was right.

verses.text: But the Pharisees and the lawyers rejected the counsel of God, not being baptized by him themselves.
verses.explainer: But the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the purpose of God for themselves, not having been baptized by John. By refusing John's baptism, they cut themselves off from the sequence of God's plan.

verses.text: “To what then will I liken the people of this generation? What are they like?
verses.explainer: 'To what then shall I compare the people of this generation, and what are they like?' Jesus prepares to diagnose the fundamental problem with his contemporaries.

verses.text: They are like children who sit in the marketplace, and call to one another, saying, ‘We piped to you, and you didn’t dance. We mourned, and you didn’t weep.’
verses.explainer: 'They are like children sitting in the marketplace calling to one another: We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not weep.' Nothing satisfies them. They are impossible to please.

verses.text: For John the Baptizer came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, ‘He has a demon.’
verses.explainer: 'John the Baptist came eating no bread and drinking no wine, and you say, He has a demon.' John's asceticism was dismissed as madness.

verses.text: The Son of Man has come eating and drinking, and you say, ‘Behold, a gluttonous man, and a drunkard; a friend of tax collectors and sinners!’
verses.explainer: 'The Son of Man has come eating and drinking, and you say, Look at him! A glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!' Jesus' sociability is dismissed as debauchery. The title 'friend of sinners,' meant as an insult, becomes one of the most beautiful descriptions of Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: The accusation of being a 'glutton and drunkard' echoed the charge against a rebellious son in Deuteronomy 21:20, which carried the death penalty. Jesus' critics were using dangerous language.
thenVsNow.now: The phrase 'friend of sinners' has been reclaimed as a positive description of Jesus' character. It encapsulates the Gospel's insistence that Jesus' primary relationships were with those whom respectable society rejected.

verses.text: Wisdom is justified by all her children.”
verses.explainer: 'Yet wisdom is justified by all her children.' The proof of wisdom is in its results. Time will vindicate both John's austere approach and Jesus' joyful one.

verses.text: One of the Pharisees invited him to eat with him. He entered into the Pharisee’s house, and sat at the table.
verses.explainer: A Pharisee invites Jesus to eat with him. Jesus enters the Pharisee's house and reclines at table. Luke records three occasions when Pharisees invite Jesus to dinner (7:36, 11:37, 14:1) -- a detail unique to Luke showing that not all Pharisees are hostile.

verses.text: Behold, a woman in the city who was a sinner, when she knew that he was reclining in the Pharisee’s house, brought an alabaster jar of ointment.
verses.explainer: A woman of the city, who is a sinner, learns that Jesus is reclining at table in the Pharisee's house. She brings an alabaster flask of ointment. The description 'a sinner' likely means she was a prostitute or had a similarly notorious reputation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Reclining at table', 'explanation': 'Formal meals were eaten while reclining on couches arranged around a low table, with feet extending outward away from the table. Houses were often open during meals, allowing uninvited people to enter and observe -- which is how this woman gains access.'}

verses.text: Standing behind at his feet weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears, and she wiped them with the hair of her head, kissed his feet, and anointed them with the ointment.
verses.explainer: Standing behind Jesus at his feet, weeping, she begins to wet his feet with her tears and wipe them with her hair. She kisses his feet and anoints them with the ointment. Every action is extravagant, intimate, and socially scandalous.

thenVsNow.then: For a woman to let down her hair in public was considered shameful -- a sign of sexual availability. For a known sinner to touch a rabbi was ritually contaminating. Every element of this scene violated social and religious propriety.
thenVsNow.now: This scene has profoundly shaped Christian understanding of worship as an act of extravagant, uninhibited gratitude. The woman becomes a model of devotion that is unconcerned with appearances.

verses.text: Now when the Pharisee who had invited him saw it, he said to himself, “This man, if he were a prophet, would have perceived who and what kind of woman this is who touches him, that she is a sinner.”
verses.explainer: The Pharisee, Simon, says to himself: 'If this man were a prophet, he would have known who and what sort of woman this is who is touching him, for she is a sinner.' Simon assumes Jesus is either ignorant or indifferent to the woman's sin.

verses.text:
Jesus answered him,
“Simon, I have something to tell you.”

He said, “Teacher, say on.”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers Simon's unspoken thought: 'Simon, I have something to say to you.' He demonstrates that he knows exactly what Simon is thinking -- and what kind of woman this is.

verses.text: “A certain lender had two debtors. The one owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty.
verses.explainer: 'A certain moneylender had two debtors. One owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty.' Jesus teaches through a parable. A denarius was roughly a day's wage, so the debts are vastly different -- about two years' wages versus two months.

verses.text: When they couldn’t pay, he forgave them both. Which of them therefore will love him most?”
verses.explainer: 'When they could not pay, he cancelled the debt of both. Now which of them will love him more?' The trap is set. The answer is obvious.

verses.text:
Simon answered, “He, I suppose, to whom he forgave the most.”
He said to him,
“You have judged correctly.”
verses.explainer: Simon answers: 'The one, I suppose, for whom he cancelled the larger debt.' Jesus says: 'You have judged rightly.' Simon has pronounced the verdict on himself without realizing it.

verses.text:
Turning to the woman, he said to Simon,
“Do you see this woman? I entered into your house, and you gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears, and wiped them with the hair of her head.
verses.explainer: Turning to the woman, Jesus says to Simon: 'Do you see this woman? I entered your house; you gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.' Jesus contrasts Simon's minimal hospitality with the woman's extravagant devotion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hospitality customs', 'explanation': 'A proper host provided water for foot-washing, a kiss of greeting, and oil for anointing the head. Simon provided none of these basic courtesies. His invitation was socially correct but personally cold.'}

verses.text: You gave me no kiss, but she, since the time I came in, has not ceased to kiss my feet.
verses.explainer: 'You gave me no kiss, but from the time I came in she has not ceased to kiss my feet.' The contrast sharpens: Simon withheld the standard greeting; the woman has been continuously showing devotion.

verses.text: You didn’t anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with ointment.
verses.explainer: 'You did not anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with ointment.' Oil for the head was basic hospitality; expensive ointment for the feet is lavish worship.

verses.text: Therefore I tell you, her sins, which are many, are forgiven, for she loved much. But to whom little is forgiven, the same loves little.”
verses.explainer: 'Therefore I tell you, her sins, which are many, are forgiven -- for she loved much. But he who is forgiven little, loves little.' The parable's point: those who know the depth of their sin and the greatness of their forgiveness love most deeply.

verses.text:
He said to her,
“Your sins are forgiven.”
verses.explainer: Then he says to her: 'Your sins are forgiven.' Jesus pronounces forgiveness directly -- the same authority he claimed in chapter 5 when he healed the paralytic.

verses.text: Those who sat at the table with him began to say to themselves, “Who is this who even forgives sins?”
verses.explainer: Those at table begin to say among themselves: 'Who is this, who even forgives sins?' The question echoes 5:21. Jesus keeps doing what only God can do.

verses.text:
He said to the woman,
“Your faith has saved you. Go in peace.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says to the woman: 'Your faith has saved you; go in peace.' Salvation is linked to faith, and it results in peace. The woman entered weeping; she leaves in peace.

explainer.summary: Chapter 8 opens with the remarkable notice that women financially supported Jesus' ministry -- including Joanna, wife of Herod's steward. Jesus teaches the parable of the sower, explaining how different hearts receive God's word. He then demonstrates authority over nature (calming a storm), the demonic realm (the Gerasene demoniac), disease (the woman with a hemorrhage), and death (raising Jairus' daughter). Luke arranges these miracles to show Jesus' comprehensive sovereignty.
themes: Women as disciples and supporters of Jesus
The parable of the sower and hearing God's word
Jesus' authority over nature, demons, disease, and death
Faith amid fear and desperation
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 8 transitions from teaching to demonstration. After the parables about hearing the word, Jesus performs four mighty acts that test whether his disciples have truly heard and believed.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'thrown alongside' -- a comparison or an
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'thrown alongside' -- a comparison or analogy. Jesus' parables are not mere illustrations but riddles that reveal truth to those with ears to hear and conceal it from those who resist. They demand active engagement from the listener.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Trust, confidence, reliance
wordStudies.meaning: Trust, confidence, reliance. In Luke 8, faith is tested repeatedly: the disciples lack it in the storm (v.25), the hemorrhaging woman possesses it (v.48), and Jairus must maintain it despite devastating news (v.50).

verses.text: Soon afterwards, he went about through cities and villages, preaching and bringing the good news of God’s Kingdom. With him were the twelve,
verses.explainer: Jesus travels through cities and villages, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom of God. The twelve are with him. His itinerant ministry covers the whole region.

verses.text: and certain women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary who was called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out;
verses.explainer: Also with him are certain women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities: Mary called Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out.

verses.text: and Joanna, the wife of Chuzas, Herod’s steward; Susanna; and many others; who served them from their possessions.
verses.explainer: Joanna, the wife of Chuza, Herod's household manager; Susanna; and many others who provided for them out of their means. Women of considerable wealth and social standing financed Jesus' ministry.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Women patrons', 'explanation': "Women financing a religious teacher's movement was unusual but not unprecedented in the Greco-Roman world. Joanna's connection to Herod's court means Jesus' movement had supporters inside the political establishment. These women are disciples in the fullest sense -- traveling with, supporting, and learning from Jesus."}

thenVsNow.then: Listing women as traveling companions and financial supporters of a rabbi was remarkable. Women did not typically travel with teachers, and their financial independence implied high social status. Luke highlights their role where other Gospels do not.
thenVsNow.now: This passage has been central to discussions about women's roles in ministry. It demonstrates that from the earliest days, women were integral to Jesus' mission -- not as passive observers but as active participants and supporters.

verses.text: When a great multitude came together, and people from every city were coming to him, he spoke by a parable.
verses.explainer: When a great crowd gathers and people from town after town come to him, he tells a parable. The sower parable is the first extended parable in Luke and functions as a key to understanding all the others.

verses.text: “The farmer went out to sow his seed. As he sowed, some fell along the road, and it was trampled under foot, and the birds of the sky devoured it.
verses.explainer: 'A sower went out to sow his seed. As he sowed, some fell along the path and was trampled underfoot, and the birds of the air devoured it.' The path represents hardened ground where seed cannot penetrate.

verses.text: Other seed fell on the rock, and as soon as it grew, it withered away, because it had no moisture.
verses.explainer: 'Some fell on the rock, and as it grew up, it withered away because it had no moisture.' Thin soil over rock -- initial growth but no depth to sustain it.

verses.text: Other fell amid the thorns, and the thorns grew with it, and choked it.
verses.explainer: 'Some fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up with it and choked it.' The thorns represent competing concerns that strangle spiritual growth.

verses.text:
Other fell into the good ground, and grew, and produced one hundred times as much fruit.” As he said these things, he called out,
“He who has ears to hear, let him hear!”
verses.explainer: 'Some fell into good soil and grew and yielded a hundredfold.' Jesus calls out: 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear.' The yield is extraordinary -- normal harvests produced five to fifteenfold. A hundredfold is miraculous abundance.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient Palestinian agriculture averaged a seven to tenfold yield. A hundredfold was almost unimaginable. Jesus is saying that when the word truly takes root, the results far exceed normal expectations.
thenVsNow.now: The sower parable is one of the most widely taught passages in Christianity. Its analysis of why people respond differently to the same message remains remarkably relevant to pastoral experience and evangelistic efforts.

verses.text: Then his disciples asked him, “What does this parable mean?”
verses.explainer: The disciples ask what this parable means. They do not automatically understand.

verses.text:
He said,
“To you it is given to know the mysteries of God’s Kingdom, but to the rest in parables; that ‘seeing they may not see, and hearing they may not understand.’
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of God, but for others they are in parables, so that seeing they may not see, and hearing they may not understand.' Parables are both revelation and concealment -- they invite the open-hearted and confound the resistant.

verses.text: Now the parable is this: The seed is the word of God.
verses.explainer: 'The seed is the word of God.' The interpretation is straightforward: the parable is about how different people respond to God's message.

verses.text: Those along the road are those who hear, then the devil comes, and takes away the word from their heart, that they may not believe and be saved.
verses.explainer: 'Those along the path are those who have heard; then the devil comes and takes away the word from their hearts, so that they may not believe and be saved.' The first category: the word is snatched before it can take root.

verses.text: Those on the rock are they who, when they hear, receive the word with joy; but these have no root, who believe for a while, then fall away in time of temptation.
verses.explainer: 'Those on the rock are those who, when they hear the word, receive it with joy. But they have no root; they believe for a while, and in time of testing fall away.' The second category: enthusiastic but shallow response that collapses under pressure.

verses.text: That which fell among the thorns, these are those who have heard, and as they go on their way they are choked with cares, riches, and pleasures of life, and bring no fruit to maturity.
verses.explainer: 'As for what fell among the thorns, they are those who hear, but as they go on their way they are choked by the cares and riches and pleasures of life, and their fruit does not mature.' The third category: the word is not rejected but crowded out by competing priorities.

thenVsNow.then: The thorns represent not sins but good things -- wealth, pleasure, legitimate concerns -- that displace the word. This makes the third soil the most dangerous because it looks healthy on the outside while producing nothing of lasting value.
thenVsNow.now: The third soil may be the most relevant to modern affluent societies. The danger is not persecution or shallow commitment but distraction -- lives so full of activity and acquisition that there is no space for spiritual maturity.

verses.text: Those in the good ground, these are those who with an honest and good heart, having heard the word, hold it tightly, and produce fruit with perseverance.
verses.explainer: 'As for that in the good soil, they are those who, hearing the word, hold it fast in an honest and good heart, and bear fruit with patience.' Good soil is characterized by honesty, retention, and patience. Fruitfulness takes time.

verses.text: “No one, when he has lit a lamp, covers it with a container, or puts it under a bed; but puts it on a stand, that those who enter in may see the light.
verses.explainer: 'No one after lighting a lamp covers it with a jar or puts it under a bed, but puts it on a stand, so that those who enter may see the light.' The truth of the kingdom is meant to be visible, not hidden.

verses.text: For nothing is hidden, that will not be revealed; nor anything secret, that will not be known and come to light.
verses.explainer: 'For nothing is hidden that will not be made manifest, nor is anything secret that will not be known and come to light.' A principle of ultimate transparency -- all things will eventually be revealed.

verses.text: Be careful therefore how you hear. For whoever has, to him will be given; and whoever doesn’t have, from him will be taken away even that which he thinks he has.”
verses.explainer: 'Take care then how you hear, for to the one who has, more will be given, and from the one who does not have, even what he thinks that he has will be taken away.' Hearing well leads to greater understanding; careless hearing leads to loss of what little understanding one had.

verses.text: His mother and brothers came to him, and they could not come near him for the crowd.
verses.explainer: Jesus' mother and brothers come to him but cannot reach him because of the crowd. Luke places this immediately after the sower parable, making family ties a test case for hearing the word.

verses.text: Some people told him, “Your mother and your brothers stand outside, desiring to see you.”
verses.explainer: He is told: 'Your mother and your brothers are standing outside, desiring to see you.'

verses.text:
But he answered them,
“My mother and my brothers are these who hear the word of God, and do it.”
verses.explainer: He answers: 'My mother and my brothers are those who hear the word of God and do it.' Spiritual kinship based on obedience takes precedence over biological kinship. This redefines family around the word of God.

verses.text:
Now on one of those days, he entered into a boat, himself and his disciples, and he said to them,
“Let’s go over to the other side of the lake.” So they launched out.
verses.explainer: One day Jesus gets into a boat with his disciples and says: 'Let us go across to the other side of the lake.' They set out. The 'other side' is Gentile territory.

verses.text: But as they sailed, he fell asleep. A wind storm came down on the lake, and they were taking on dangerous amounts of water.
verses.explainer: As they sail, Jesus falls asleep. A windstorm comes down on the lake; they are filling with water and are in danger. The Sea of Galilee, 700 feet below sea level, is surrounded by hills that funnel violent storms down onto the water without warning.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Storms on the Sea of Galilee', 'explanation': 'The Sea of Galilee sits in a basin surrounded by hills and ravines that channel wind down onto the water. Sudden, violent storms were (and still are) a well-known hazard. Several of the disciples were professional fishermen who had navigated these waters their entire lives, so their terror indicates a storm of unusual severity.'}

verses.text: They came to him, and awoke him, saying, “Master, master, we are dying!” He awoke, and rebuked the wind and the raging of the water, and they ceased, and it was calm.
verses.explainer: They wake Jesus, saying: 'Master, Master, we are perishing!' He rebukes the wind and the raging waves, and they cease, and there is calm. Jesus exercises the same authority over nature that the Old Testament attributes to God alone.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Where is your faith?” Being afraid they marveled, saying to one another, “Who is this then, that he commands even the winds and the water, and they obey him?”
verses.explainer: He says to them: 'Where is your faith?' They are afraid and marvel, saying to one another: 'Who then is this, that he commands even winds and water, and they obey him?' The question the disciples ask is the central question of the Gospel: who is this man?

verses.text: They arrived at the country of the Gadarenes, which is opposite Galilee.
verses.explainer: They sail to the country of the Gerasenes, which is opposite Galilee. This is Gentile territory on the eastern shore of the Sea of Galilee.

verses.text: When Jesus stepped ashore, a certain man out of the city who had demons for a long time met him. He wore no clothes, and didn’t live in a house, but in the tombs.
verses.explainer: When Jesus steps ashore, a man from the city who has demons meets him. For a long time he has worn no clothes and lives not in a house but among the tombs. Luke paints a picture of complete dehumanization -- naked, homeless, living among the dead.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Living among tombs', 'explanation': "Tombs in the ancient world were caves or rock-cut chambers outside city limits. Living among them made one perpetually ritually unclean. The man's existence among the dead symbolizes his complete exclusion from the community of the living."}

verses.text: When he saw Jesus, he cried out, and fell down before him, and with a loud voice said, “What do I have to do with you, Jesus, you Son of the Most High God? I beg you, don’t torment me!”
verses.explainer: When he sees Jesus, he cries out, falls down before him, and shouts: 'What have you to do with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, do not torment me.' The demons recognize Jesus' divine authority and fear his power.

verses.text: For Jesus was commanding the unclean spirit to come out of the man. For the unclean spirit had often seized the man. He was kept under guard, and bound with chains and fetters. Breaking the bonds apart, he was driven by the demon into the desert.
verses.explainer: For Jesus had commanded the unclean spirit to come out. Many times it had seized him; he was kept under guard and bound with chains and shackles, but he would break the bonds and be driven by the demon into the desert. Human restraints cannot contain supernatural oppression.

verses.text:
Jesus asked him,
“What is your name?”
He said, “Legion,” for many demons had entered into him.
verses.explainer: Jesus asks: 'What is your name?' He replies: 'Legion,' for many demons had entered him. A Roman legion consisted of about 5,000 soldiers. The name evokes both the number of demons and the Roman military occupation.

verses.text: They begged him that he would not command them to go into the abyss.
verses.explainer: The demons beg Jesus not to command them to go into the abyss. They recognize his authority to consign them to ultimate punishment.

verses.text: Now there was there a herd of many pigs feeding on the mountain, and they begged him that he would allow them to enter into those. He allowed them.
verses.explainer: A large herd of pigs is feeding on the hillside. The demons beg to be allowed to enter the pigs, and Jesus permits it. The presence of pigs confirms this is Gentile territory -- Jews did not raise pigs.

verses.text: The demons came out of the man, and entered into the pigs, and the herd rushed down the steep bank into the lake, and were drowned.
verses.explainer: The demons come out of the man and enter the pigs, and the herd rushes down the steep bank into the lake and drowns. The destructive nature of the demons is revealed -- what they were doing to the man, they now do to the pigs.

verses.text: When those who fed them saw what had happened, they fled, and told it in the city and in the country.
verses.explainer: When the herdsmen see what has happened, they flee and report it in the city and in the country. The economic loss of the herd would have been devastating for the community.

verses.text: People went out to see what had happened. They came to Jesus, and found the man from whom the demons had gone out, sitting at Jesus’ feet, clothed and in his right mind; and they were afraid.
verses.explainer: People come to see what happened and find the man from whom the demons had gone, sitting at Jesus' feet, clothed and in his right mind. They are afraid. The transformation is complete: from naked, homeless, and demon-possessed to clothed, rational, and learning at Jesus' feet.

thenVsNow.then: Being found 'clothed and in his right mind' reverses every element of his previous condition. The restoration is holistic -- physical (clothed), mental (right mind), social (sitting with Jesus), and spiritual (freed from demons).
thenVsNow.now: This story has been important for Christian ministry to those suffering from mental illness, addiction, and social marginalization. It affirms that no one is beyond the reach of God's healing power, no matter how far gone they appear.

verses.text: Those who saw it told them how he who had been possessed by demons was healed.
verses.explainer: Those who had seen it tell them how the demon-possessed man had been healed. The eyewitnesses spread the news.

verses.text: All the people of the surrounding country of the Gadarenes asked him to depart from them, for they were very much afraid. He entered into the boat, and returned.
verses.explainer: All the people of the surrounding country of the Gerasenes ask Jesus to depart from them, for they are seized with great fear. They prefer their pigs to the liberated man. Fear of disruption outweighs gratitude for healing.

verses.text: But the man from whom the demons had gone out begged him that he might go with him, but Jesus sent him away, saying,
verses.explainer: The healed man begs to go with Jesus, but Jesus sends him away, saying: 'Return to your home, and declare how much God has done for you.' The man becomes an evangelist to his own community.

verses.text: “Return to your house, and declare what great things God has done for you.” He went his way, proclaiming throughout the whole city what great things Jesus had done for him.
verses.explainer: He goes away, proclaiming throughout the whole city how much Jesus had done for him. He obeys Jesus' instruction and becomes the first missionary to the Gentile region.

verses.text: When Jesus returned, the multitude welcomed him, for they were all waiting for him.
verses.explainer: When Jesus returns, the crowd welcomes him, for they were all waiting for him. The contrast with the Gerasenes' rejection is sharp.

verses.text: Behold, there came a man named Jairus, and he was a ruler of the synagogue. He fell down at Jesus’ feet, and begged him to come into his house,
verses.explainer: A man named Jairus, a ruler of the synagogue, falls at Jesus' feet and begs him to come to his house. His only daughter, about twelve years of age, is dying.

verses.text: for he had an only daughter, about twelve years of age, and she was dying. But as he went, the multitudes pressed against him.
verses.explainer: As Jesus goes, the crowds press around him. The urgency of Jairus' request is being delayed by the crush of people.

verses.text: A woman who had a flow of blood for twelve years, who had spent all her living on physicians, and could not be healed by any,
verses.explainer: A woman who has had a discharge of blood for twelve years, who has spent all her living on physicians and could not be healed by anyone, touches the fringe of his garment.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Twelve years of hemorrhage', 'explanation': 'A continuous flow of blood made a woman perpetually ritually unclean (Leviticus 15:25-27). Anything she touched became unclean. She could not enter the Temple, attend synagogue, or have normal social contact. For twelve years she lived in isolation, shame, and poverty -- having spent all her money on failed treatments.'}

verses.text: came behind him, and touched the fringe of his cloak, and immediately the flow of her blood stopped.
verses.explainer: She comes up behind Jesus and touches the fringe of his garment, and immediately her discharge of blood ceases. She reaches out in desperation, and the power of Jesus responds instantly.

verses.text:
Jesus said,
“Who touched me?”
When all denied it, Peter and those with him said, “Master, the multitudes press and jostle you, and you say,
‘Who touched me?’”
verses.explainer: Jesus asks: 'Who was it that touched me?' Peter says: 'Master, the crowds surround you and press against you!' But Jesus knows the difference between incidental contact and the touch of faith.

verses.text:
But Jesus said,
“Someone did touch me, for I perceived that power has gone out of me.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Someone touched me, for I perceive that power has gone out from me.' Healing costs something. There is a transfer of power that Jesus is aware of.

verses.text: When the woman saw that she was not hidden, she came trembling, and falling down before him declared to him in the presence of all the people the reason why she had touched him, and how she was healed immediately.
verses.explainer: The woman, seeing that she is not hidden, comes trembling, falls before Jesus, and declares before all the people why she touched him and how she was immediately healed. Her public testimony is part of her restoration -- she is no longer hiding in shame.

verses.text:
He said to her,
“Daughter, cheer up. Your faith has made you well. Go in peace.”
verses.explainer: He says to her: 'Daughter, your faith has made you well; go in peace.' The address 'Daughter' is tender and familial. She is not unclean but healed; not an outcast but a daughter. Faith, not the touch itself, is the mechanism of salvation.

thenVsNow.then: By calling her 'Daughter,' Jesus publicly restores her to community membership. Her healing is not just physical but social and religious -- she can now participate in worship, touch people, and live normally for the first time in twelve years.
thenVsNow.now: This story has been especially meaningful for people who feel their condition makes them unworthy to approach God. Jesus' response demonstrates that reaching out in faith, even imperfect and desperate faith, is always welcomed.

verses.text: While he still spoke, one from the ruler of the synagogue’s house came, saying to him, “Your daughter is dead. Don’t trouble the Teacher.”
verses.explainer: While Jesus is still speaking, someone from the ruler's house comes and says: 'Your daughter is dead; do not trouble the Teacher any more.' The delay caused by the hemorrhaging woman appears to have cost Jairus his daughter.

verses.text:
But Jesus hearing it, answered him,
“Don’t be afraid. Only believe, and she will be healed.”
verses.explainer: But Jesus, hearing this, answers Jairus: 'Do not fear; only believe, and she will be well.' Faith is demanded in the face of the worst possible news.

verses.text: When he came to the house, he didn’t allow anyone to enter in, except Peter, John, James, the father of the child, and her mother.
verses.explainer: When he arrives at the house, he permits no one to enter with him except Peter, John, and James, and the father and mother of the child. The inner circle of three disciples witnesses this most intimate miracle.

verses.text:
All were weeping and mourning her, but he said,
“Don’t weep. She isn’t dead, but sleeping.”
verses.explainer: All are weeping and mourning for her, but Jesus says: 'Do not weep, for she is not dead but sleeping.' From Jesus' perspective, death is temporary -- a sleep from which one can be awakened.

verses.text: They were ridiculing him, knowing that she was dead.
verses.explainer: They laugh at him, knowing that she is dead. The mourners' laughter reveals the gap between human certainty about death and Jesus' authority over it.

verses.text:
But he put them all outside, and taking her by the hand, he called, saying,
“Child, arise!”
verses.explainer: Taking her by the hand, he calls: 'Child, arise.' He touches a corpse, which would normally cause ritual impurity. Again, Jesus' touch reverses contamination -- instead of becoming unclean, the dead girl becomes alive.

verses.text: Her spirit returned, and she rose up immediately. He commanded that something be given to her to eat.
verses.explainer: Her spirit returns, and she gets up at once. He directs them to give her something to eat. The practical instruction to feed her confirms the physical reality of the resurrection -- she is not a ghost but a living, hungry child.

verses.text: Her parents were amazed, but he commanded them to tell no one what had been done.
verses.explainer: Her parents are amazed, and he charges them to tell no one what has happened. Jesus continues to control the timing and manner of revelations about his identity.

explainer.summary: Chapter 9 is a turning point in Luke's Gospel. Jesus sends the Twelve on their first mission, feeds 5,000 with five loaves and two fish, and elicits Peter's confession that he is the Christ. He then reveals that the Christ must suffer and die. The Transfiguration confirms his divine glory, but the disciples fail to heal a boy with a demon. Jesus resolutely 'sets his face toward Jerusalem' -- beginning the long journey to the cross that will dominate the rest of the Gospel.
themes: The disciples' commission and its implications
Jesus' identity revealed and misunderstood
The necessity of suffering for the Messiah
The Transfiguration as a glimpse of divine glory
Setting the face toward Jerusalem
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 is the hinge of Luke's Gospel. Everything before leads to Peter's confession; everything after flows from Jesus' decision to go to Jerusalem. The Galilean ministry ends here, and the travel narrative begins.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The anointed one, the Messiah
wordStudies.meaning: The anointed one, the Messiah. Peter's confession that Jesus is 'the Christ of God' is the theological climax of the first half of Luke. But Jesus immediately redefines what 'Christ' means: not a conquering king but a suffering servant.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Literally 'departure
wordStudies.meaning: Literally 'departure.' At the Transfiguration, Moses and Elijah discuss Jesus' 'exodus' -- his departure that he is about to accomplish in Jerusalem. The word deliberately evokes Israel's Exodus from Egypt, suggesting that Jesus' death will be a greater act of liberation.

verses.text: He called the twelve together, and gave them power and authority over all demons, and to cure diseases.
verses.explainer: Jesus calls the twelve together and gives them power and authority over all demons and to cure diseases. He delegates his own authority to his followers.

verses.text: He sent them out to preach God’s Kingdom and to heal the sick.
verses.explainer: He sends them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal. The dual mission -- preaching and healing -- mirrors Jesus' own ministry.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Take nothing for your journey—neither staffs, nor wallet, nor bread, nor money; neither have two coats each.
verses.explainer: He tells them: 'Take nothing for your journey, no staff, no bag, no bread, no money, and do not have two tunics.' Radical dependence on God's provision and the hospitality of others.

thenVsNow.then: Traveling with no provisions meant total reliance on the hospitality of strangers. This was a statement of trust in God and a test of the communities they visited. If a town received them, it demonstrated openness to the message.
thenVsNow.now: While few modern missionaries travel with literally nothing, the principle of simplicity and dependence on God remains central to mission practice. Material self-sufficiency can become a barrier to genuine trust.

verses.text: Into whatever house you enter, stay there, and depart from there.
verses.explainer: 'Whatever house you enter, stay there, and from there depart.' Do not upgrade accommodations. Accept the first welcome you receive.

verses.text: As many as don’t receive you, when you depart from that city, shake off even the dust from your feet for a testimony against them.”
verses.explainer: 'Wherever they do not receive you, when you leave that town shake off the dust from your feet as a testimony against them.' Shaking dust off one's feet was a gesture Jews used when leaving Gentile territory, symbolically rejecting contamination. Using it against Jewish towns was provocative.

verses.text: They departed, and went throughout the villages, preaching the Good News, and healing everywhere.
verses.explainer: They depart and go through the villages, preaching the gospel and healing everywhere. The mission multiplies Jesus' reach exponentially.

verses.text: Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all that was done by him; and he was very perplexed, because it was said by some that John had risen from the dead,
verses.explainer: Herod the tetrarch hears about all that is happening and is perplexed, because some say John has been raised from the dead. Jesus' ministry has reached the political establishment.

verses.text: and by some that Elijah had appeared, and by others that one of the old prophets had risen again.
verses.explainer: Others say Elijah has appeared, and others that one of the prophets of old has risen. The public is wrestling with Jesus' identity through the lens of prophetic categories.

verses.text: Herod said, “John I beheaded, but who is this, about whom I hear such things?” He sought to see him.
verses.explainer: Herod says: 'John I beheaded, but who is this about whom I hear such things?' And he seeks to see Jesus. Herod's curiosity will eventually be satisfied -- when Pilate sends Jesus to him (23:8).

verses.text:
The apostles, when they had returned, told him what things they had done.
He took them, and withdrew apart to a desert region of a city called Bethsaida.
verses.explainer: When the apostles return, they tell Jesus everything they have done. He takes them and withdraws privately to a town called Bethsaida. He needs to debrief them away from the crowds.

verses.text: But the multitudes, perceiving it, followed him. He welcomed them, and spoke to them of God’s Kingdom, and he cured those who needed healing.
verses.explainer: But the crowds discover where he has gone and follow him. He welcomes them, speaks to them about the kingdom of God, and heals those who need healing. Jesus' compassion overrides his need for rest.

verses.text: The day began to wear away; and the twelve came, and said to him, “Send the multitude away, that they may go into the surrounding villages and farms, and lodge, and get food, for we are here in a deserted place.”
verses.explainer: As the day begins to wear away, the twelve come to him and say: 'Send the crowd away to go into the surrounding villages and countryside to find lodging and provisions, for we are here in a desolate place.' The disciples see a logistical problem.

verses.text:
But he said to them,
“You give them something to eat.”
They said, “We have no more than five loaves and two fish, unless we should go and buy food for all these people.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'You give them something to eat.' They say: 'We have no more than five loaves and two fish -- unless we are to go and buy food for all these people.' Jesus turns the problem back on the disciples, exposing their limited thinking.

verses.text:
For they were about five thousand men.
He said to his disciples,
“Make them sit down in groups of about fifty each.”
verses.explainer: For there are about five thousand men. Jesus tells his disciples: 'Have them sit down in groups of about fifty each.' The organization of the crowd prepares for orderly distribution.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Five thousand men', 'explanation': "Luke says five thousand 'men' (andres), likely not counting women and children. The total crowd may have been 10,000-15,000 people. In a region where the largest towns had a few thousand inhabitants, this was an enormous gathering."}

verses.text: They did so, and made them all sit down.
verses.explainer: They do so, and have them all sit down. The disciples obey despite not understanding what will happen.

verses.text: He took the five loaves and the two fish, and looking up to the sky, he blessed them, and broke them, and gave them to the disciples to set before the multitude.
verses.explainer: Taking the five loaves and two fish, Jesus looks up to heaven, blesses them, breaks them, and gives them to the disciples to set before the crowd. The four verbs -- took, blessed, broke, gave -- will reappear at the Last Supper (22:19). Every Eucharistic celebration echoes this meal.

verses.text: They ate, and were all filled. They gathered up twelve baskets of broken pieces that were left over.
verses.explainer: They all eat and are satisfied, and twelve baskets of broken pieces are left over. The surplus -- twelve baskets, one for each tribe of Israel -- indicates an abundance that far exceeds the need. God's provision is not merely adequate but extravagant.

verses.text:
As he was praying alone, the disciples were with him, and he asked them,
“Who do the multitudes say that I am?”
verses.explainer: While Jesus is praying in private, his disciples are with him. He asks them: 'Who do the crowds say that I am?' Luke notes that Jesus is praying before this crucial conversation -- prayer precedes revelation.

verses.text: They answered, “‘John the Baptizer,’ but others say, ‘Elijah,’ and others, that one of the old prophets is risen again.”
verses.explainer: They answer: 'John the Baptist. But others say, Elijah, and others, that one of the prophets of old has risen.' The same categories Herod heard. The public perceives Jesus as a prophetic figure but has not grasped his unique identity.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“But who do you say that I am?”
Peter answered, “The Christ of God.”
verses.explainer: He asks: 'But who do you say that I am?' Peter answers: 'The Christ of God.' This is the confession the entire Gospel has been building toward. Peter identifies Jesus as the Messiah, the anointed king Israel has been waiting for.

thenVsNow.then: For Peter to declare Jesus as the Christ was politically explosive. Messianic claims attracted Roman attention and could result in execution for sedition. Peter is making a dangerous confession.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's confession remains the foundational Christian affirmation. Every creed, every baptismal formula, every statement of faith echoes this moment: Jesus is the Christ, the Messiah, the anointed one of God.

verses.text: But he warned them, and commanded them to tell this to no one,
verses.explainer: He strictly charges and commands them to tell this to no one. The messianic secret: Jesus does not want the title 'Christ' proclaimed until its meaning has been redefined by the cross.

verses.text:
saying,
“The Son of Man must suffer many things, and be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and scribes, and be killed, and the third day be raised up.”
verses.explainer: 'The Son of Man must suffer many things and be rejected by the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be killed, and on the third day be raised.' The first passion prediction. Jesus redefines the Christ as a suffering figure, not a conquering one.

verses.text:
He said to all,
“If anyone desires to come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross,
and follow me.
verses.explainer: 'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily and follow me.' Discipleship means self-denial, daily cross-bearing, and following Jesus' path. 'Daily' is Luke's addition -- this is not a one-time decision but a way of life.

verses.text: For whoever desires to save his life will lose it, but whoever will lose his life for my sake, the same will save it.
verses.explainer: 'Whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will save it.' The paradox at the heart of Christian discipleship: self-preservation leads to loss; self-giving leads to life.

verses.text: For what does it profit a man if he gains the whole world, and loses or forfeits his own self?
verses.explainer: 'What does it profit a person to gain the whole world and lose or forfeit himself?' The ultimate cost-benefit analysis. No amount of worldly gain compensates for the loss of one's true self.

verses.text: For whoever will be ashamed of me and of my words, of him will the Son of Man be ashamed, when he comes in his glory, and the glory of the Father, and of the holy angels.
verses.explainer: 'Whoever is ashamed of me and of my words, of him will the Son of Man be ashamed when he comes in his glory and the glory of the Father and of the holy angels.' Future vindication is promised, but it comes only to those who are not ashamed of Jesus in the present.

verses.text: But I tell you the truth: There are some of those who stand here, who will in no way taste of death, until they see God’s Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: 'I tell you truly, there are some standing here who will not taste death until they see the kingdom of God.' This likely refers to the Transfiguration, which follows immediately and provides a glimpse of the kingdom's glory.

verses.text: About eight days after these sayings, he took with him Peter, John, and James, and went up onto the mountain to pray.
verses.explainer: About eight days after these sayings, Jesus takes Peter, John, and James and goes up on the mountain to pray. Again, prayer is the setting for a major revelation. The inner circle of three witnesses is present.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The mountain', 'explanation': 'The mountain is traditionally identified as Mount Tabor, though Mount Hermon is another possibility. Mountains in biblical tradition are places of divine encounter -- Sinai, Moriah, Carmel. Jesus goes up to pray and is transformed.'}

verses.text: As he was praying, the appearance of his face was altered, and his clothing became white and dazzling.
verses.explainer: As he is praying, the appearance of his face is altered, and his clothing becomes dazzling white. The Transfiguration reveals Jesus' divine glory breaking through his human form.

verses.text: Behold, two men were talking with him, who were Moses and Elijah,
verses.explainer: Two men are talking with him -- Moses and Elijah. Moses represents the Law, Elijah represents the Prophets. Together they represent the entire Old Testament testimony pointing to Jesus.

verses.text: who appeared in glory, and spoke of his departure, which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: They appear in glory and speak of his 'exodus,' which he is about to accomplish in Jerusalem. The word 'exodus' frames Jesus' death as a liberation event greater than Israel's escape from Egypt.

thenVsNow.then: Moses led the original Exodus from slavery in Egypt. Now Moses discusses a greater exodus with Jesus -- liberation from sin and death, accomplished through the cross in Jerusalem.
thenVsNow.now: The Transfiguration has shaped Christian theology about Jesus' dual nature -- fully human and fully divine. The fleeting glimpse of glory on the mountain is understood as a preview of the resurrection and the ultimate transformation of all creation.

verses.text: Now Peter and those who were with him were heavy with sleep, but when they were fully awake, they saw his glory, and the two men who stood with him.
verses.explainer: Peter and those with him are heavy with sleep, but when they wake they see Jesus' glory and the two men standing with him. They almost miss the greatest revelation because they are sleeping -- a pattern that will recur at Gethsemane.

verses.text: As they were parting from him, Peter said to Jesus, “Master, it is good for us to be here. Let’s make three tents: one for you, and one for Moses, and one for Elijah,” not knowing what he said.
verses.explainer: As Moses and Elijah are departing, Peter says: 'Master, it is good that we are here. Let us make three tents, one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.' Peter wants to preserve the mountaintop experience, not realizing he is placing Jesus on the same level as Moses and Elijah.

verses.text: While he said these things, a cloud came and overshadowed them, and they were afraid as they entered into the cloud.
verses.explainer: A cloud comes and overshadows them, and they are afraid as they enter the cloud. The cloud is the shekinah glory -- the same cloud that covered Sinai and filled the Temple.

verses.text: A voice came out of the cloud, saying, “This is my beloved Son. Listen to him!”
verses.explainer: A voice comes out of the cloud: 'This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him!' The divine voice corrects Peter's error: Jesus is not one among three but the unique Son. 'Listen to him' echoes the prophecy of a prophet like Moses.

verses.text: When the voice came, Jesus was found alone. They were silent, and told no one in those days any of the things which they had seen.
verses.explainer: When the voice has spoken, Jesus is found alone. The disciples keep silent and tell no one in those days anything of what they have seen. The experience is too overwhelming to process, let alone communicate.

verses.text: On the next day, when they had come down from the mountain, a great multitude met him.
verses.explainer: On the next day, when they come down from the mountain, a great crowd meets them. The descent from glory to the chaos of human need is abrupt and deliberate.

verses.text: Behold, a man from the crowd called out, saying, “Teacher, I beg you to look at my son, for he is my only child.
verses.explainer: A man from the crowd cries out: 'Teacher, I beg you to look at my son, for he is my only son.' The desperation of a father with a suffering only child. Luke emphasizes 'only son' -- heightening the pathos.

verses.text: Behold, a spirit takes him, he suddenly cries out, and it convulses him so that he foams, and it hardly departs from him, bruising him severely.
verses.explainer: A spirit seizes the boy, and he suddenly cries out. It convulses him so that he foams at the mouth, and shatters him and will hardly leave him. The description is vivid and distressing.

verses.text: I begged your disciples to cast it out, and they couldn’t.”
verses.explainer: 'I begged your disciples to cast it out, but they could not.' The disciples' failure contrasts sharply with the power Jesus gave them in verse 1. Authority delegated does not guarantee successful application.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“Faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'O faithless and twisted generation, how long am I to be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here.' The frustration is palpable -- the gap between what should be possible and what the disciples actually achieve is exasperating.

verses.text: While he was still coming, the demon threw him down and convulsed him violently. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, and healed the boy, and gave him back to his father.
verses.explainer: As the boy is coming, the demon throws him to the ground and convulses him. But Jesus rebukes the unclean spirit, heals the boy, and gives him back to his father. Again, the phrase 'gave him back to his father' echoes the Elijah and Nain stories.

verses.text:
They were all astonished at the majesty of God.
But while all were marveling at all the things which Jesus did, he said to his disciples,
verses.explainer: All are astonished at the majesty of God. But while they are all marveling, Jesus says to his disciples: 'Let these words sink into your ears: The Son of Man is about to be delivered into the hands of men.' The second passion prediction comes at the height of popular acclaim -- Jesus redirects attention from glory to suffering.

verses.text: “Let these words sink into your ears, for the Son of Man will be delivered up into the hands of men.”
verses.explainer: He tells them to let these words sink into their ears. The emphasis on listening suggests the disciples are not truly hearing what he says about suffering.

verses.text: But they didn’t understand this saying. It was concealed from them, that they should not perceive it, and they were afraid to ask him about this saying.
verses.explainer: But they do not understand this saying, and it is concealed from them so that they might not perceive it. They are afraid to ask him about it. Fear of the answer prevents them from seeking understanding.

verses.text: There arose an argument among them about which of them was the greatest.
verses.explainer: An argument arises among them as to which of them is the greatest. Immediately after a passion prediction, the disciples argue about status. The irony is crushing.

verses.text: Jesus, perceiving the reasoning of their hearts, took a little child, and set him by his side,
verses.explainer: Jesus, perceiving the thought of their hearts, takes a child and puts the child by his side. Children in the ancient world had no social status or legal rights. Jesus places the lowest on the social scale at the center.

verses.text:
and said to them,
“Whoever receives this little child in my name receives me. Whoever receives me receives him who sent me. For whoever is least among you all, this one will be great.”
verses.explainer: 'Whoever receives this child in my name receives me, and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me. For the one who is least among you all is the one who is great.' Greatness is measured by willingness to serve the powerless.

thenVsNow.then: Children were valued for their potential but had no standing in adult society. They could not own property, testify in court, or participate in religious discussions. Using a child as a model of greatness reversed the entire social hierarchy.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus' elevation of children contributed to a gradual cultural shift in how children are valued. Many of the institutions that protect children's rights trace their ethical roots to the Christian conviction that children have inherent dignity and worth.

verses.text: John answered, “Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we forbade him, because he doesn’t follow with us.”
verses.explainer: John says: 'Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him, because he does not follow with us.' The disciples want exclusive control over Jesus' brand.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Don’t forbid him, for he who is not against us is for us.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Do not stop him, for the one who is not against you is for you.' Jesus refuses to restrict his work to his own circle. The kingdom is broader than any one group's control.

verses.text: It came to pass, when the days were near that he should be taken up, he intently set his face to go to Jerusalem,
verses.explainer: When the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem. This is the pivotal verse of Luke's Gospel. The journey to Jerusalem begins -- a journey that will occupy ten chapters (9:51-19:44) and lead to the cross.

thenVsNow.then: The phrase 'set his face' echoes Isaiah 50:7, where the Suffering Servant sets his face like flint against opposition. Jesus' decision to go to Jerusalem is deliberate, resolute, and fully aware of what awaits him there.
thenVsNow.now: Luke's travel narrative has shaped the Christian understanding of life as a journey toward God. Pilgrimage traditions, Lenten practices of spiritual preparation, and the metaphor of 'the way' all draw on this section of Luke.

verses.text: and sent messengers before his face. They went, and entered into a village of the Samaritans, so as to prepare for him.
verses.explainer: He sends messengers ahead of him. They enter a village of the Samaritans to make preparations for him.

verses.text: They didn’t receive him, because he was traveling with his face set towards Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: But the Samaritans do not receive him, because his face is set toward Jerusalem. The hostility between Jews and Samaritans runs deep -- Samaritans had their own temple on Mount Gerizim and rejected Jerusalem's claims.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Jewish-Samaritan hostility', 'explanation': 'Samaritans were descendants of the northern Israelite kingdom who intermarried with Assyrian settlers. They accepted only the five books of Moses and worshiped at Mount Gerizim, not Jerusalem. The mutual hostility between Jews and Samaritans was centuries old and deeply bitter.'}

verses.text: When his disciples, James and John, saw this, they said, “Lord, do you want us to command fire to come down from the sky, and destroy them, just as Elijah did?”
verses.explainer: James and John say: 'Lord, do you want us to tell fire to come down from heaven and consume them?' They want to replay Elijah's destruction of enemy soldiers -- using divine power as a weapon of retaliation.

verses.text:
But he turned and rebuked them,
“You don’t know of what kind of spirit you are.
verses.explainer: But Jesus turns and rebukes them. Some manuscripts add: 'You do not know what manner of spirit you are of.' Jesus categorically rejects violence against those who reject him.

verses.text:
For the Son of Man didn’t come to destroy men’s lives, but to save them.”
They went to another village.
verses.explainer: They go on to another village. The rejected Messiah moves on without retaliation -- a pattern that will continue all the way to the cross.

verses.text: As they went on the way, a certain man said to him, “I want to follow you wherever you go, Lord.”
verses.explainer: As they are going along the road, someone says to Jesus: 'I will follow you wherever you go.' The first of three would-be followers encounters Jesus' honesty about the cost.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“The foxes have holes, and the birds of the sky have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.' Following Jesus means embracing homelessness and insecurity. Even animals have more comfort than the Son of Man.

verses.text:
He said to another,
“Follow me!”
But he said, “Lord, allow me first to go and bury my father.”
verses.explainer: To another he says: 'Follow me.' But the man says: 'Lord, let me first go and bury my father.' The request seems entirely reasonable -- burying one's parents was the most sacred family duty in Jewish culture.

verses.text:
But Jesus said to him,
“Leave the dead to bury their own dead, but you go and announce God’s Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Leave the dead to bury their own dead. But as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God.' This is one of Jesus' most shocking sayings. He subordinates the most sacred family obligation to the urgency of the kingdom.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Burying one's father", 'explanation': "Honoring one's parents, especially through burial, was among the highest obligations in Judaism. It overrode almost every other religious duty, including Torah study and Temple service. Jesus' demand that the kingdom takes priority even over this was deeply provocative."}

verses.text: Another also said, “I want to follow you, Lord, but first allow me to say good-bye to those who are at my house.”
verses.explainer: Another says: 'I will follow you, Lord, but let me first say farewell to those at my home.' A reasonable request that echoes Elisha's request to Elijah (1 Kings 19:20).

verses.text:
But Jesus said to him,
“No one, having put his hand to the plow, and looking back, is fit for God’s Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.' Plowing requires forward focus; looking back creates crooked furrows. The kingdom demands undivided commitment.

thenVsNow.then: Plowing with a single-handled plow required constant forward attention. A momentary backward glance could ruin the furrow. The image was vivid for an agricultural audience.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus' uncompromising demands on would-be followers continue to challenge comfortable Christianity. The call to radical commitment without reservation sets a standard that every generation must wrestle with.

explainer.summary: Jesus sends seventy-two disciples ahead of him in pairs, expanding the mission beyond the Twelve. They return with joy, and Jesus rejoices that God has revealed truth to the simple rather than the wise. A lawyer's question about eternal life leads to the parable of the Good Samaritan -- one of the most influential stories ever told. The chapter closes with Mary and Martha, where Jesus affirms contemplative listening over anxious activity.
themes: Mission expansion beyond the Twelve
Joy in the kingdom's advance
Neighbor-love without boundaries (Good Samaritan)
The one thing needful (Mary and Martha)
explainer.contextInBook: The sending of the seventy-two extends the mission that began with the Twelve in chapter 9. The Good Samaritan and Mary/Martha stories are unique to Luke and define the shape of discipleship: love of neighbor and attention to Jesus' word.

wordStudies.translatedAs: In Jewish interpretation, 'neighbor' in Leviticus
wordStudies.meaning: In Jewish interpretation, 'neighbor' in Leviticus 19:18 was understood to mean fellow Israelites. The lawyer's question 'Who is my neighbor?' sought to define the boundaries of obligation. Jesus' parable exploded those boundaries entirely.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A member of the community descended from the north
wordStudies.meaning: A member of the community descended from the northern Israelite kingdom, considered heretical by Jews. Making a Samaritan the hero of this parable was shocking and offensive to Jesus' Jewish audience -- as if he deliberately chose the most despised outsider to model God's love.

verses.text: Now after these things, the Lord also appointed seventy others, and sent them two by two ahead of him into every city and place, where he was about to come.
verses.explainer: After this, the Lord appoints seventy-two others and sends them two by two ahead of his face into every town and place where he himself is about to go. The number seventy-two recalls the seventy nations listed in Genesis 10, symbolizing a mission to the whole world.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Seventy-two missionaries', 'explanation': "Some manuscripts read 'seventy' rather than 'seventy-two.' Either number is symbolic: seventy elders assisted Moses (Numbers 11:16-25), and the Jewish tradition held that there were seventy or seventy-two nations in the world (Genesis 10). The mission signals the universal scope of the gospel."}

verses.text:
Then he said to them,
“The harvest is indeed plentiful, but the laborers are few. Pray therefore to the Lord of the harvest, that he may send out laborers into his harvest.
verses.explainer: 'The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few. Therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest.' The first instruction is not to work but to pray. God must supply workers for God's harvest.

verses.text: Go your ways. Behold, I send you out as lambs among wolves.
verses.explainer: 'Go your way; behold, I am sending you out as lambs in the midst of wolves.' The imagery is deliberately alarming. The missionaries have no natural defense. Their safety depends entirely on the Shepherd who sends them.

verses.text: Carry no purse, nor wallet, nor sandals. Greet no one on the way.
verses.explainer: 'Carry no moneybag, no knapsack, no sandals, and greet no one on the road.' The urgency is extreme. Even the courtesy of roadside greetings is sacrificed for speed.

verses.text: Into whatever house you enter, first say, ‘Peace be to this house.’
verses.explainer: 'Whatever house you enter, first say, Peace be to this house.' The greeting of peace (shalom) is not merely polite -- it is a pronouncement of divine blessing.

verses.text: If a son of peace is there, your peace will rest on him; but if not, it will return to you.
verses.explainer: 'If a son of peace is there, your peace will rest upon him. But if not, it will return to you.' Peace is treated as a real force that either finds a receptive home or returns to its source.

verses.text: Remain in that same house, eating and drinking the things they give, for the laborer is worthy of his wages. Don’t go from house to house.
verses.explainer: 'Remain in the same house, eating and drinking what they provide, for the laborer deserves his wages.' Missionaries should accept hospitality without guilt. Their work has value.

verses.text: Into whatever city you enter, and they receive you, eat the things that are set before you.
verses.explainer: 'Whenever you enter a town and they receive you, eat what is set before you.' This instruction may imply eating non-kosher food in Gentile homes -- a radical concession for Jewish missionaries.

verses.text: Heal the sick who are therein, and tell them, ‘God’s Kingdom has come near to you.’
verses.explainer: 'Heal the sick in it and say to them, The kingdom of God has come near to you.' Healing and proclamation go together. The kingdom is not just announced but demonstrated.

verses.text: But into whatever city you enter, and they don’t receive you, go out into its streets and say,
verses.explainer: 'But whenever you enter a town and they do not receive you, go into its streets and say...' Rejection is anticipated as a real possibility.

verses.text: ‘Even the dust from your city that clings to us, we wipe off against you. Nevertheless know this, that God’s Kingdom has come near to you.’
verses.explainer: 'Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet we wipe off against you. Nevertheless know this, that the kingdom of God has come near.' The message was delivered whether accepted or not. Rejection does not invalidate the kingdom's approach.

verses.text: I tell you, it will be more tolerable in that day for Sodom than for that city.
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, it will be more bearable on that day for Sodom than for that town.' Sodom, the epitome of wickedness in Jewish tradition, will fare better than towns that reject the kingdom's messengers.

verses.text: “Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon which were done in you, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.' Gentile cities would have responded better than Jewish cities that witnessed Jesus' miracles.

verses.text: But it will be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon in the judgment than for you.
verses.explainer: 'But it will be more bearable in the judgment for Tyre and Sidon than for you.' Greater privilege brings greater responsibility. Seeing miracles and rejecting them is worse than never having seen them.

verses.text: You, Capernaum, who are exalted to heaven, will be brought down to Hades.
verses.explainer: 'And you, Capernaum, will you be exalted to heaven? You will be brought down to Hades.' Even Jesus' adopted hometown, the base of his Galilean ministry, is condemned for its ultimate failure to believe.

verses.text: Whoever listens to you listens to me, and whoever rejects you rejects me. Whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me.”
verses.explainer: 'The one who hears you hears me, and the one who rejects you rejects me, and the one who rejects me rejects him who sent me.' A chain of representation: the missionaries represent Jesus, who represents the Father. Rejection escalates all the way to God.

verses.text: The seventy returned with joy, saying, “Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name!”
verses.explainer: The seventy-two return with joy, saying: 'Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name!' Their mission exceeded their expectations -- even demons obeyed.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“I saw Satan having fallen like lightning from heaven.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.' As the missionaries worked, Jesus witnessed a cosmic defeat of evil. Their local victories reflected a universal spiritual reality.

thenVsNow.then: Satan's fall 'like lightning' suggests a sudden, spectacular overthrow. The seventy-two's mission was not just a preaching tour but a battle in the cosmic war against evil. Every healing and exorcism was a territorial advance.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has shaped Christian understanding of spiritual warfare -- the idea that earthly ministry has cosmic spiritual dimensions. Mission work is understood as participation in the ongoing defeat of evil.

verses.text: Behold, I give you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy. Nothing will in any way hurt you.
verses.explainer: 'Behold, I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy, and nothing shall hurt you.' Serpents and scorpions symbolize demonic powers. The authority given to the missionaries is comprehensive.

verses.text: Nevertheless, don’t rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.”
verses.explainer: 'Nevertheless, do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.' Power over demons is impressive but secondary. What matters most is one's relationship with God.

verses.text:
In that same hour Jesus rejoiced in the Holy Spirit, and said,
“I thank you, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for so it was well-pleasing in your sight.”
verses.explainer: In that same hour Jesus rejoices in the Holy Spirit and says: 'I thank you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little children.' This is one of the few times Luke records Jesus expressing joy. God reveals truth to the humble, not the intellectually elite.

verses.text:
Turning to the disciples, he said,
“All things have been delivered to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is, except the Father, and who the Father is, except the Son, and he to whomever the Son desires to reveal him.”
verses.explainer: 'All things have been delivered to me by my Father, and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, or who the Father is except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.' A remarkable claim of unique mutual knowledge between Father and Son -- rare in the Synoptic Gospels, more characteristic of John.

verses.text:
Turning to the disciples, he said privately,
“Blessed are the eyes which see the things that you see,
verses.explainer: Turning to the disciples privately, he says: 'Blessed are the eyes that see what you see!' The disciples are witnessing what prophets and kings longed to see.

verses.text: for I tell you that many prophets and kings desired to see the things which you see, and didn’t see them, and to hear the things which you hear, and didn’t hear them.”
verses.explainer: 'For I tell you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see, and did not see it, and to hear what you hear, and did not hear it.' The disciples occupy a uniquely privileged moment in salvation history.

verses.text: Behold, a certain lawyer stood up and tested him, saying, “Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?”
verses.explainer: A lawyer stands up to test Jesus: 'Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?' The question is genuine but the motivation is testing -- he wants to see if Jesus' answer aligns with orthodox teaching.

verses.text:
He said to him,
“What is written in the law? How do you read it?”
verses.explainer: Jesus answers with a question: 'What is written in the Law? How do you read it?' Jesus redirects the man to his own area of expertise. The answer is already in the text he has studied his whole life.

verses.text: He answered, “You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, with all your strength, and with all your mind; and your neighbor as yourself.”
verses.explainer: The lawyer answers: 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind, and your neighbor as yourself.' He combines Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18 -- the same summary Jesus himself affirms as the greatest commandment.

verses.text:
He said to him,
“You have answered correctly. Do this, and you will live.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'You have answered correctly; do this, and you will live.' The answer is right. The challenge is doing it.

verses.text: But he, desiring to justify himself, asked Jesus, “Who is my neighbor?”
verses.explainer: But the lawyer, desiring to justify himself, asks: 'And who is my neighbor?' He wants to draw a boundary around his obligation. The question is self-serving: tell me the minimum I must do.

thenVsNow.then: In Jewish legal discussion, 'neighbor' was debated. Most Pharisees included all Jews; some included resident aliens; very few would include Samaritans or Gentiles. The lawyer wants a boundary that limits his obligation.
thenVsNow.now: The question 'Who is my neighbor?' is still asked whenever people try to limit their moral obligations. Jesus' parable makes the question unanswerable in its original form and replaces it with a better one.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who both stripped him and beat him, and departed, leaving him half dead.
verses.explainer: Jesus answers with a parable: 'A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who stripped him and beat him and departed, leaving him half dead.' The Jericho road descended 3,400 feet through desolate, rocky terrain -- a notoriously dangerous route.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The Jericho road', 'explanation': "The 17-mile road from Jerusalem to Jericho descended through barren, winding terrain with many caves -- perfect hiding spots for bandits. It was so dangerous it was known as 'The Way of Blood.' Everyone in Jesus' audience knew this road's reputation."}

verses.text: By chance a certain priest was going down that way. When he saw him, he passed by on the other side.
verses.explainer: 'By chance a priest was going down that road, and when he saw him he passed by on the other side.' The priest may have feared the man was dead; touching a corpse would make him ritually unclean and unable to perform Temple duties. He chose purity rules over compassion.

verses.text: In the same way a Levite also, when he came to the place, and saw him, passed by on the other side.
verses.explainer: 'So likewise a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side.' A Levite -- a Temple assistant -- does the same. The religious establishment fails twice. The audience would expect the third figure to be a Jewish layperson who succeeds where the clergy failed.

verses.text: But a certain Samaritan, as he traveled, came where he was. When he saw him, he was moved with compassion,
verses.explainer: 'But a Samaritan, as he journeyed, came to where he was, and when he saw him, he had compassion.' The shock: a Samaritan. Not a Jewish layman but the most despised outsider imaginable. And he has compassion -- the same word used for Jesus' response to the widow of Nain.

thenVsNow.then: Making a Samaritan the hero was deeply offensive to Jesus' Jewish audience. Samaritans and Jews had centuries of mutual hatred. A modern equivalent might be making a member of one's most hated outgroup the moral hero of the story.
thenVsNow.now: The Good Samaritan is one of the most widely known stories in Western civilization. The phrase 'Good Samaritan' has entered every language to mean someone who helps a stranger. Good Samaritan laws, hospitals, and charities bear the name. It has profoundly shaped the Western ethic of compassion for strangers.

verses.text: came to him, and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. He set him on his own animal, and brought him to an inn, and took care of him.
verses.explainer: 'He went to him and bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he set him on his own animal and brought him to an inn and took care of him.' The Samaritan does everything: first aid, transportation, lodging, and personal nursing care. His compassion is practical, costly, and sustained.

verses.text: On the next day, when he departed, he took out two denarii, and gave them to the host, and said to him, ‘Take care of him. Whatever you spend beyond that, I will repay you when I return.’
