verses.explainer: 'The next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper, saying, Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I will repay you when I come back.' Two denarii would cover roughly two weeks of care. And the offer to pay any additional costs is open-ended generosity.

verses.text: Now which of these three do you think seemed to be a neighbor to him who fell among the robbers?”
verses.explainer: 'Which of these three, do you think, proved to be a neighbor to the man who fell among the robbers?' Jesus flips the original question. The lawyer asked 'Who is my neighbor?' (who deserves my help?). Jesus asks 'Who proved to be a neighbor?' (who acted with love?).

verses.text:
He said, “He who showed mercy on him.”
Then Jesus said to him,
“Go and do likewise.”
verses.explainer: The lawyer answers: 'The one who showed him mercy.' He cannot bring himself to say 'the Samaritan.' Jesus says: 'You go, and do likewise.' The command is universal: be like the Samaritan. Show mercy without boundaries.

verses.text: As they went on their way, he entered into a certain village, and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.
verses.explainer: As they continue their journey, Jesus enters a village. A woman named Martha welcomes him into her house. The village is Bethany, near Jerusalem, though Luke does not name it here.

verses.text: She had a sister called Mary, who also sat at Jesus’ feet, and heard his word.
verses.explainer: She has a sister called Mary, who sits at the Lord's feet and listens to his teaching. 'Sitting at someone's feet' was the technical posture of a disciple learning from a rabbi. Mary takes the position of a male disciple.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Sitting at a rabbi's feet", 'explanation': "In Judaism, to 'sit at the feet of' a teacher meant to be that teacher's student. Paul 'sat at the feet of Gamaliel' (Acts 22:3). For a woman to assume this position was countercultural -- women were not typically accepted as rabbinic students. Jesus' acceptance of Mary in this role is quietly revolutionary."}

verses.text: But Martha was distracted with much serving, and she came up to him, and said, “Lord, don’t you care that my sister left me to serve alone? Ask her therefore to help me.”
verses.explainer: Martha is distracted with much serving. She approaches Jesus and says: 'Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her to help me.' Martha's complaint is understandable -- she is doing all the work while Mary does nothing.

verses.text:
Jesus answered her,
“Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things,
verses.explainer: 'Martha, Martha, the Lord answered, you are anxious and troubled about many things.' The repetition of her name is tender, not harsh. Jesus acknowledges her anxiety without dismissing her work.

verses.text: but one thing is needed. Mary has chosen the good part, which will not be taken away from her.”
verses.explainer: 'But one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion, which will not be taken away from her.' Listening to Jesus' word is the 'one thing' that matters most. Martha's service is not wrong, but Mary's attentiveness is better.

thenVsNow.then: By affirming Mary's choice to learn at his feet, Jesus validates women as theological students and disciples. In a culture where women were expected to serve and not study, this was a quiet revolution.
thenVsNow.now: The Mary-Martha story has shaped centuries of Christian reflection on the balance between action and contemplation. It is frequently cited in discussions about spiritual burnout, the primacy of being over doing, and the danger of letting service crowd out relationship with God.

explainer.summary: Jesus teaches his disciples to pray (Luke's version of the Lord's Prayer, shorter than Matthew's), then tells the parable of the persistent friend at midnight. He casts out a demon and is accused of working through Beelzebul, the prince of demons. Jesus refutes the charge with devastating logic and warns of the danger of spiritual emptiness. He pronounces woes on the Pharisees and lawyers for their hypocrisy -- majoring on minors while neglecting justice and the love of God.
themes: Prayer as the heartbeat of discipleship
The Holy Spirit as God's greatest gift
Jesus' power over evil and the Beelzebul accusation
Spiritual emptiness and the danger of moral neutrality
Prophetic critique of religious hypocrisy
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 11 deepens the contrast between genuine and false religion. The Lord's Prayer models authentic communion with God; the woes on the Pharisees expose religion reduced to external performance. Prayer and prophetic critique are two sides of the same coin.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Jesus teaches his disciples to address God simply
wordStudies.meaning: Jesus teaches his disciples to address God simply as 'Father' -- an intimate, familial term that was unusual in Jewish prayer, which typically used more formal titles. This one word captures the revolution in how Jesus relates to God and invites his followers to do the same.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The name means 'lord of the flies' or 'lord of the
wordStudies.meaning: The name means 'lord of the flies' or 'lord of the dwelling.' Originally a Philistine deity (2 Kings 1:2), it became a Jewish name for the prince of demons. The accusation that Jesus works through Beelzebul is the most serious charge his opponents make.

verses.text: When he finished praying in a certain place, one of his disciples said to him, “Lord, teach us to pray, just as John also taught his disciples.”
verses.explainer: Jesus is praying in a certain place, and when he finishes, one of his disciples says: 'Lord, teach us to pray, as John taught his disciples.' The request comes after observing Jesus pray. They want to learn his way of communicating with God.

thenVsNow.then: Jewish groups often had distinctive prayers that identified them. John's disciples had their own prayer. The request for Jesus to teach them a prayer was a request for a communal identity marker.
thenVsNow.now: The Lord's Prayer is the most widely prayed text in Christianity, recited daily by billions of people across every tradition, language, and culture. It has been called the perfect prayer because of its balance of worship, petition, and dependence on God.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“When you pray, say,
‘Our Father in heaven,
may your name be kept holy.
May your Kingdom come.
May your will be done on earth, as it is in heaven.
verses.explainer: He says: 'When you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come.' Luke's version is shorter and simpler than Matthew's. It begins with intimate address (Father) and moves to cosmic hope (your kingdom come).

verses.text: Give us day by day our daily bread.
verses.explainer: 'Give us each day our daily bread.' Daily bread -- not weekly or yearly. The prayer teaches dependence on God for basic needs, one day at a time. The word 'daily' (epiousios) is so rare that its exact meaning is debated.

verses.text:
Forgive us our sins,
for we ourselves also forgive everyone who is indebted to us.

Bring us not into temptation,
but deliver us from the evil one.’”
verses.explainer: 'And forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation.' Three petitions: daily provision, forgiveness (linked to forgiving others), and protection from testing. The prayer is communal ('us,' not 'me') throughout.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Which of you, if you go to a friend at midnight, and tell him, ‘Friend, lend me three loaves of bread,
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Which of you who has a friend will go to him at midnight and say to him, Friend, lend me three loaves...' The parable of the persistent friend begins. Midnight requests are inconvenient in any era.

verses.text: for a friend of mine has come to me from a journey, and I have nothing to set before him,’
verses.explainer: '...for a friend of mine has arrived on a journey, and I have nothing to set before him.' In a hospitality culture, failing to feed a guest was a serious social failure. The man is desperate because honor is at stake.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hospitality obligations', 'explanation': "In the ancient Near East, hospitality was a sacred duty, not optional generosity. A host was obligated to provide food for any guest, no matter the hour. Failure to do so brought shame on the entire village. The man's midnight request was driven by communal honor."}

verses.text: and he from within will answer and say, ‘Don’t bother me. The door is now shut, and my children are with me in bed. I can’t get up and give it to you’?
verses.explainer: 'And he will answer from within, Do not bother me; the door is already locked, and my children are with me in bed. I cannot get up and give you anything.' The friend inside has every reason to refuse -- it is midnight, the family is sleeping, and the door is barred.

verses.text: I tell you, although he will not rise and give it to him because he is his friend, yet because of his persistence, he will get up and give him as many as he needs.
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, though he will not get up and give him anything because he is his friend, yet because of his impudence he will rise and give him whatever he needs.' The key word is 'impudence' (anaideia) -- shameless persistence. The man gets what he wants not through relationship but through relentless asking.

verses.text: “I tell you, keep asking, and it will be given you. Keep seeking, and you will find. Keep knocking, and it will be opened to you.
verses.explainer: 'And I tell you, ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you.' The three imperatives are in the present tense: keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. Persistence, not a single request, is the model.

verses.text: For everyone who asks receives. He who seeks finds. To him who knocks it will be opened.
verses.explainer: 'For everyone who asks receives, and the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks it will be opened.' The promise is universal ('everyone') and unconditional. God responds to persistent prayer.

verses.text: “Which of you fathers, if your son asks for bread, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, he won’t give him a snake instead of a fish, will he?
verses.explainer: 'What father among you, if his son asks for a fish, will instead of a fish give him a serpent?' The argument moves from human fatherhood to divine fatherhood: if imperfect human fathers give good gifts, how much more will God?

verses.text: Or if he asks for an egg, he won’t give him a scorpion, will he?
verses.explainer: 'Or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion?' The comparisons are vivid: a coiled serpent could resemble a fish, a curled scorpion could resemble an egg. But no father would make such a substitution.

verses.text: If you then, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him?”
verses.explainer: 'If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!' Luke's version climaxes with the Holy Spirit as God's greatest gift. Matthew says 'good things'; Luke says 'the Holy Spirit.' The Spirit is the ultimate answer to prayer.

thenVsNow.then: The promise of the Holy Spirit anticipates Pentecost (Acts 2). Luke sees the Spirit as the defining gift of the new age -- the presence of God living within believers.
thenVsNow.now: This verse grounds the Christian practice of praying for the Holy Spirit. Many traditions emphasize that the Spirit is not an impersonal force but God's own presence given in response to believing prayer.

verses.text: He was casting out a demon, and it was mute. When the demon had gone out, the mute man spoke; and the multitudes marveled.
verses.explainer: Jesus casts out a demon that is mute. When the demon leaves, the mute man speaks, and the people marvel. The exorcism is the occasion for the theological controversy that follows.

verses.text: But some of them said, “He casts out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of the demons.”
verses.explainer: But some of them say: 'He casts out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons.' Unable to deny the miracle, they attribute it to satanic power. This is the most dangerous accusation against Jesus -- that his power comes from evil.

verses.text: Others, testing him, sought from him a sign from heaven.
verses.explainer: Others, to test him, seek from him a sign from heaven. Not satisfied with exorcisms and healings, they want cosmic signs -- fire from heaven, perhaps, or a celestial display.

verses.text:
But he, knowing their thoughts, said to them,
“Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation. A house divided against itself falls.
verses.explainer: But Jesus, knowing their thoughts, says: 'Every kingdom divided against itself is laid waste, and a divided household falls.' He begins his rebuttal with basic logic: division leads to destruction.

verses.text: If Satan also is divided against himself, how will his kingdom stand? For you say that I cast out demons by Beelzebul.
verses.explainer: 'And if Satan also is divided against himself, how will his kingdom stand? For you say that I cast out demons by Beelzebul.' If Jesus works for Satan, then Satan is destroying his own agents. That makes no strategic sense.

verses.text: But if I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your children cast them out? Therefore will they be your judges.
verses.explainer: 'And if I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they will be your judges.' Jewish exorcists existed and were not accused of demonic collaboration. The double standard is exposed.

verses.text: But if I by God’s finger cast out demons, then God’s Kingdom has come to you.
verses.explainer: 'But if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you.' 'The finger of God' echoes the Egyptian magicians' recognition of God's power through Moses. If Jesus' power is divine, then the kingdom has already arrived.

verses.text: “When the strong man, fully armed, guards his own dwelling, his goods are safe.
verses.explainer: 'When a strong man, fully armed, guards his own palace, his goods are safe.' Satan is the strong man who holds people captive.

verses.text: But when someone stronger attacks him and overcomes him, he takes from him his whole armor in which he trusted, and divides his plunder.
verses.explainer: 'But when one stronger than he attacks him and overcomes him, he takes away his armor in which he trusted and divides his spoil.' Jesus is the stronger one who overpowers Satan and liberates his captives. The exorcisms are evidence of this cosmic victory.

verses.text: “He that is not with me is against me. He who doesn’t gather with me scatters.
verses.explainer: 'Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.' In the battle between Jesus and Satan, neutrality is impossible. There is no middle ground.

verses.text: The unclean spirit, when he has gone out of the man, passes through dry places, seeking rest, and finding none, he says, ‘I will turn back to my house from which I came out.’
verses.explainer: 'When the unclean spirit has gone out of a person, it passes through waterless places seeking rest, and finding none it says, I will return to my house from which I came.' The parable of the returning demon warns against spiritual emptiness.

verses.text: When he returns, he finds it swept and put in order.
verses.explainer: 'And when it comes, it finds the house swept and put in order.' Clean but empty. Moral reformation without spiritual filling creates a vacuum.

verses.text: Then he goes, and takes seven other spirits more evil than himself, and they enter in and dwell there. The last state of that man becomes worse than the first.”
verses.explainer: 'Then it goes and brings seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they enter and dwell there. And the last state of that person is worse than the first.' Being cleaned up is not enough; one must be filled with the Spirit. An empty, reformed life is more vulnerable than before.

verses.text: It came to pass, as he said these things, a certain woman out of the multitude lifted up her voice, and said to him, “Blessed is the womb that bore you, and the breasts which nursed you!”
verses.explainer: A woman in the crowd raises her voice and says: 'Blessed is the womb that bore you, and the breasts at which you nursed!' She praises Jesus' mother -- a natural response in a culture that honored mothers of great men.

verses.text:
But he said,
“On the contrary, blessed are those who hear the word of God, and keep it.”
verses.explainer: But Jesus says: 'Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and keep it!' Jesus redirects blessing from biological relationship to obedience. This echoes 8:21 -- his true family is defined by hearing and doing God's word.

verses.text:
When the multitudes were gathering together to him, he began to say,
“This is an evil generation. It seeks after a sign. No sign will be given to it but the sign of Jonah, the prophet.
verses.explainer: When the crowds increase, he begins to say: 'This generation is an evil generation. It seeks for a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah.' They want spectacle; Jesus offers the sign of a prophet who called for repentance.

verses.text: For even as Jonah became a sign to the Ninevites, so will also the Son of Man be to this generation.
verses.explainer: 'For as Jonah became a sign to the people of Nineveh, so will the Son of Man be to this generation.' Jonah's preaching led to the repentance of a pagan city. Jesus' generation, with far greater revelation, is less responsive.

verses.text: The Queen of the South will rise up in the judgment with the men of this generation, and will condemn them: for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and behold, one greater than Solomon is here.
verses.explainer: 'The queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with the men of this generation and condemn them, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and behold, something greater than Solomon is here.' A Gentile queen sought wisdom; this generation refuses it.

verses.text: The men of Nineveh will stand up in the judgment with this generation, and will condemn it: for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, one greater than Jonah is here.
verses.explainer: 'The men of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and behold, something greater than Jonah is here.' Pagan cities will judge Israel's failure to respond.

verses.text: “No one, when he has lit a lamp, puts it in a cellar or under a basket, but on a stand, that those who come in may see the light.
verses.explainer: 'No one after lighting a lamp puts it in a cellar or under a basket, but on a stand, so that those who enter may see the light.' Light is meant to be visible. Truth should illuminate, not be concealed.

verses.text: The lamp of the body is the eye. Therefore when your eye is good, your whole body is also full of light; but when it is evil, your body also is full of darkness.
verses.explainer: 'Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eye is healthy, your whole body is full of light, but when it is bad, your body is full of darkness.' The eye as lamp suggests that how you perceive reality determines whether you live in light or darkness.

verses.text: Therefore see whether the light that is in you isn’t darkness.
verses.explainer: 'Therefore be careful lest the light in you be darkness.' The most dangerous condition: thinking you see clearly when you are actually blind. False certainty is worse than admitted ignorance.

verses.text: If therefore your whole body is full of light, having no part dark, it will be wholly full of light, as when the lamp with its bright shining gives you light.”
verses.explainer: 'If then your whole body is full of light, having no part dark, it will be wholly bright, as when a lamp with its rays gives you light.' Complete spiritual clarity produces a life that radiates light.

verses.text: Now as he spoke, a certain Pharisee asked him to dine with him. He went in, and sat at the table.
verses.explainer: While Jesus is speaking, a Pharisee asks him to dine with him. He goes in and reclines at table. This is the second Pharisee dinner invitation in Luke.

verses.text: When the Pharisee saw it, he marveled that he had not first washed himself before dinner.
verses.explainer: The Pharisee is astonished to see that Jesus does not first wash before dinner. The issue is not hygiene but ritual purity -- ceremonial hand-washing before meals was a Pharisaic practice, not a biblical command.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Ritual hand-washing', 'explanation': 'The Pharisees practiced ceremonial hand-washing before meals to maintain ritual purity. This was part of their oral tradition (later codified in the Mishnah), not a requirement of the written Torah. The practice extended Temple purity into everyday life.'}

verses.text:
The Lord said to him,
“Now you Pharisees cleanse the outside of the cup and of the platter, but your inward part is full of extortion and wickedness.
verses.explainer: The Lord says to him: 'Now you Pharisees cleanse the outside of the cup and of the dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness.' External cleanliness masks internal corruption.

verses.text: You foolish ones, didn’t he who made the outside make the inside also?
verses.explainer: 'You fools! Did not he who made the outside make the inside also?' God created the whole person. Cleaning only the exterior is nonsensical to the One who sees both.

verses.text: But give for gifts to the needy those things which are within, and behold, all things will be clean to you.
verses.explainer: 'But give as alms those things that are within, and behold, everything is clean for you.' Inner generosity purifies the whole person. Real cleanliness comes from the heart, not the hands.

verses.text: But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe mint and rue and every herb, but you bypass justice and God’s love. You ought to have done these, and not to have left the other undone.
verses.explainer: 'But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe mint and rue and every herb, and neglect justice and the love of God. These you ought to have done, without neglecting the others.' Jesus does not reject tithing but condemns prioritizing minor details over major principles.

thenVsNow.then: Tithing herbs was an extreme application of the agricultural tithe laws. The Pharisees counted out a tenth of even tiny herb plants while overlooking the Torah's demands for justice and love. Jesus says both matter, but justice and love are primary.
thenVsNow.now: The tendency to focus on minor religious requirements while ignoring major ethical demands remains a persistent temptation in every religious tradition. Jesus' critique applies wherever ritual compliance substitutes for genuine love and justice.

verses.text: Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the best seats in the synagogues, and the greetings in the marketplaces.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the best seat in the synagogues and greetings in the marketplaces.' Status-seeking disguised as piety.

verses.text: Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you are like hidden graves, and the men who walk over them don’t know it.”
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you! For you are like unmarked graves, and people walk over them without knowing it.' An unmarked grave caused ritual contamination for anyone who walked over it. The Pharisees appear clean but actually contaminate others.

verses.text: One of the lawyers answered him, “Teacher, in saying this you insult us also.”
verses.explainer: One of the lawyers answers: 'Teacher, in saying these things you insult us also.' The lawyers feel the sting and object. Jesus does not apologize -- he adds three more woes.

verses.text:
He said,
“Woe to you lawyers also! For you load men with burdens that are difficult to carry, and you yourselves won’t even lift one finger to help carry those burdens.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you lawyers also! For you load people with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not touch the burdens with one of your fingers.' They create rules but do not help people follow them.

verses.text: Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets whom your fathers killed.' They honor dead prophets while continuing the pattern of rejecting living ones.

verses.text: So you testify and consent to the works of your fathers. For they killed them, and you build their tombs.
verses.explainer: 'So you are witnesses and you consent to the deeds of your fathers, for they killed them, and you build their tombs.' Building tombs for prophets seems respectful but actually memorializes the murder while continuing the pattern.

verses.text: Therefore also the wisdom of God said, ‘I will send to them prophets and apostles; and some of them they will kill and persecute,
verses.explainer: 'Therefore also the Wisdom of God said, I will send them prophets and apostles, some of whom they will kill and persecute.' The pattern of rejecting God's messengers will continue.

verses.text: that the blood of all the prophets, which was shed from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation;
verses.explainer: 'So that the blood of all the prophets, shed from the foundation of the world, may be charged against this generation.' The accumulated guilt of centuries of prophet-rejection falls on the generation that rejects the ultimate prophet.

verses.text: from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zachariah, who perished between the altar and the sanctuary.’ Yes, I tell you, it will be required of this generation.
verses.explainer: 'From the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who perished between the altar and the sanctuary.' Abel was the first martyr (Genesis 4); Zechariah was the last in the Hebrew Bible's canonical order (2 Chronicles 24:20-22). The entire sweep of prophetic blood is encompassed.

verses.text: Woe to you lawyers! For you took away the key of knowledge. You didn’t enter in yourselves, and those who were entering in, you hindered.”
verses.explainer: 'Woe to you lawyers! For you have taken away the key of knowledge. You did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering.' The most devastating woe: the experts in God's word have made it inaccessible. They neither understand it themselves nor allow others to.

verses.text: As he said these things to them, the scribes and the Pharisees began to be terribly angry, and to draw many things out of him;
verses.explainer: As he went away from there, the scribes and Pharisees begin to press him hard and to provoke him to speak about many things.

verses.text: lying in wait for him, and seeking to catch him in something he might say, that they might accuse him.
verses.explainer: Lying in wait for him, to catch him in something he might say. The woes have turned opposition into active hostility. They are now seeking grounds for a formal charge.

explainer.summary: Jesus addresses an enormous crowd on themes of fearless confession, the danger of greed, trust in God's provision, readiness for the master's return, and the division his message brings. The parable of the rich fool warns against storing up treasures for oneself while being bankrupt toward God. Jesus teaches that anxiety about material needs reveals a failure to trust the Father. Servants must be ready because the Son of Man comes at an unexpected hour.
themes: Fearless confession of faith under persecution
The foolishness of greed and material hoarding
God's care for sparrows and disciples alike
Readiness for the Son of Man's return
The division that Jesus' message inevitably creates
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 12 follows the woes of chapter 11 with teaching about what authentic discipleship looks like. If the Pharisees represent false religion, this chapter describes the real thing: fearless trust in God, freedom from material anxiety, and constant readiness for the kingdom.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Originally a theater term meaning 'acting' or 'pla
wordStudies.meaning: Originally a theater term meaning 'acting' or 'playing a part.' In Jesus' usage, it describes the gap between public performance and private reality. The leaven of the Pharisees is hypocrisy -- the pretense of piety that masks inner corruption.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Can mean 'life,' 'soul,' or 'self
wordStudies.meaning: Can mean 'life,' 'soul,' or 'self.' In the parable of the rich fool, God says 'your soul is demanded of you' -- meaning your life, your very self. All the grain in the world cannot preserve the soul from death.

verses.text:
Meanwhile, when a multitude of many thousands had gathered together, so much so that they trampled on each other, he began to tell his disciples first of all,
“Beware of the yeast of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.
verses.explainer: Meanwhile, when so many thousands of the crowd have gathered together that they are trampling one another, Jesus begins to say to his disciples first of all: 'Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.' The massive crowd creates an atmosphere of excitement, but Jesus' first word is a warning about religious pretense.

verses.text: But there is nothing covered up, that will not be revealed, nor hidden, that will not be known.
verses.explainer: 'Nothing is covered up that will not be revealed, or hidden that will not be known.' Hypocrisy is always temporary. Everything hidden will eventually be exposed.

verses.text: Therefore whatever you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light. What you have spoken in the ear in the inner rooms will be proclaimed on the housetops.
verses.explainer: 'Whatever you have said in the dark shall be heard in the light, and what you have whispered in private rooms shall be proclaimed on the housetops.' The principle of ultimate transparency applies to everyone -- what is done in secret will become public.

verses.text: “I tell you, my friends, don’t be afraid of those who kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do.
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, my friends, do not fear those who kill the body, and after that have nothing more that they can do.' Jesus calls the disciples 'friends' -- a term of intimacy. Human persecutors can only affect the body.

verses.text:
But I will warn you whom you should fear. Fear him, who after he has killed, has power to cast into Gehenna.
Yes, I tell you, fear him.
verses.explainer: 'But I will warn you whom to fear: fear him who, after he has killed, has authority to cast into hell. Yes, I tell you, fear him!' The only legitimate fear is of God, who has authority over both body and soul.

verses.text: “Aren’t five sparrows sold for two assaria coins? Not one of them is forgotten by God.
verses.explainer: 'Are not five sparrows sold for two pennies? And not one of them is forgotten before God.' Sparrows were the cheapest animals sold in the marketplace. Even the most insignificant creatures are known to God.

thenVsNow.then: Sparrows were sold as food for the very poor -- five for two assarion (tiny copper coins worth about 1/16 of a denarius). They were essentially worthless in economic terms. Yet God remembers every one.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been a source of immense comfort for people who feel insignificant or overlooked. If God remembers sparrows, he certainly remembers each human being. It grounds the theology of divine providence in the most humble terms possible.

verses.text: But the very hairs of your head are all counted. Therefore don’t be afraid. You are of more value than many sparrows.
verses.explainer: 'Why, even the hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not; you are of more value than many sparrows.' God's knowledge extends to such trivial details as the number of hairs on each person's head. This level of divine attentiveness makes fear unnecessary.

verses.text: “I tell you, everyone who confesses me before men, him will the Son of Man also confess before the angels of God;
verses.explainer: 'And I tell you, everyone who acknowledges me before men, the Son of Man also will acknowledge before the angels of God.' Public confession of Jesus has eternal consequences -- the Son of Man will advocate for the faithful.

verses.text: but he who denies me in the presence of men will be denied in the presence of the angels of God.
verses.explainer: 'But the one who denies me before men will be denied before the angels of God.' Denial has equally serious consequences. The symmetry is stark.

verses.text: Everyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but those who blaspheme against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
verses.explainer: 'And everyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but the one who blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.' The unforgivable sin is not a single act but a sustained rejection of the Spirit's testimony about Jesus. Speaking against Jesus in ignorance can be forgiven; willfully resisting the Spirit's witness cannot.

thenVsNow.then: In context, blaspheming the Holy Spirit likely refers to attributing the Spirit's work to Satan (the Beelzebul accusation of 11:15). It is an ongoing posture of resistance, not a momentary lapse.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has caused enormous anxiety among sensitive believers who fear they may have committed the unforgivable sin. Most pastors and theologians agree that anyone worried about having committed it almost certainly has not -- the very concern demonstrates spiritual sensitivity rather than hardened resistance.

verses.text: When they bring you before the synagogues, the rulers, and the authorities, don’t be anxious how or what you will answer, or what you will say;
verses.explainer: 'And when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious about how you should defend yourself or what you should say.' Persecution is assumed as inevitable, not possible.

verses.text: for the Holy Spirit will teach you in that same hour what you must say.”
verses.explainer: 'For the Holy Spirit will teach you in that very hour what you ought to say.' The Spirit provides in the moment of need. Preparation is not forbidden, but ultimate reliance is on God's real-time guidance.

verses.text: One of the multitude said to him, “Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.”
verses.explainer: Someone in the crowd says to Jesus: 'Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me.' In the middle of teaching about eternal things, someone wants Jesus to settle a property dispute.

verses.text:
But he said to him,
“Man, who made me a judge or an arbitrator over you?”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Man, who made me a judge or arbitrator over you?' Jesus refuses the role of legal mediator. His mission is more fundamental than settling estate disputes.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Beware! Keep yourselves from covetousness, for a man’s life doesn’t consist of the abundance of the things which he possesses.”
verses.explainer: He says to them: 'Take care, and be on your guard against all covetousness, for one's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions.' The principle is stated before the parable illustrates it: life and possessions are not the same thing.

verses.text:
He spoke a parable to them, saying,
“The ground of a certain rich man produced abundantly.
verses.explainer: He tells them a parable: 'The land of a rich man produced plentifully.' The abundance is not earned but given -- the land produced. The man's wealth is a gift, not an achievement.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Agricultural wealth', 'explanation': 'In the ancient world, land was the primary source of wealth. A bumper crop was considered a divine blessing. The question was always what to do with surplus -- and the Torah taught that surplus should be shared with the poor, widows, orphans, and resident aliens.'}

verses.text: He reasoned within himself, saying, ‘What will I do, because I don’t have room to store my crops?’
verses.explainer: 'And he thought to himself, What shall I do, for I have nowhere to store my crops?' The rich man's problem is a good problem -- too much abundance. His first thought is storage, not sharing.

verses.text: He said, ‘This is what I will do. I will pull down my barns, and build bigger ones, and there I will store all my grain and my goods.
verses.explainer: 'I will do this: I will tear down my barns and build larger ones, and there I will store all my grain and my goods.' His plan is entirely self-referential. He talks only to himself and only about his own needs.

verses.text: I will tell my soul, “Soul, you have many goods laid up for many years. Take your ease, eat, drink, be merry.”’
verses.explainer: 'And I will say to my soul, Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry.' He addresses his own soul as if material security equals spiritual satisfaction. His vision of the good life is consumption without purpose.

thenVsNow.then: The rich fool's monologue uses first-person pronouns ('I,' 'my') obsessively. There is no mention of God, family, neighbors, or the poor. His world has shrunk to himself and his possessions.
thenVsNow.now: The parable of the rich fool has become a classic warning against materialism and self-centered accumulation. It challenges retirement planning that prioritizes personal comfort over generosity and purpose.

verses.text: “But God said to him, ‘You foolish one, tonight your soul is required of you. The things which you have prepared—whose will they be?’
verses.explainer: 'But God said to him, Fool! This night your soul is required of you, and the things you have prepared, whose will they be?' God speaks for the only time in this parable, and his first word is 'Fool.' Death makes all accumulation pointless.

verses.text: So is he who lays up treasure for himself, and is not rich toward God.”
verses.explainer: 'So is the one who lays up treasure for himself and is not rich toward God.' The summary: self-directed wealth is ultimately foolish. Only being 'rich toward God' -- investing in what has eternal value -- makes sense.

verses.text:
He said to his disciples,
“Therefore I tell you, don’t be anxious for your life, what you will eat, nor yet for your body, what you will wear.
verses.explainer: He says to his disciples: 'Therefore I tell you, do not be anxious about your life, what you will eat, nor about your body, what you will put on.' The transition from the rich fool's hoarding to teaching against anxiety shows that both greed and worry stem from the same root: lack of trust in God.

verses.text: Life is more than food, and the body is more than clothing.
verses.explainer: 'For life is more than food, and the body more than clothing.' If God gave you life and a body, he will certainly provide what sustains them. The greater gift guarantees the lesser.

verses.text: Consider the ravens: they don’t sow, they don’t reap, they have no warehouse or barn, and God feeds them. How much more valuable are you than birds!
verses.explainer: 'Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, they have neither storehouse nor barn, and yet God feeds them. Of how much more value are you than the birds!' Ravens were considered unclean birds in Jewish law, yet God feeds even them.

verses.text: Which of you by being anxious can add a cubit to his height?
verses.explainer: 'And which of you by being anxious can add a single hour to his span of life?' Worry accomplishes nothing. It cannot extend life by even a moment.

verses.text: If then you aren’t able to do even the least things, why are you anxious about the rest?
verses.explainer: 'If then you are not able to do as small a thing as that, why are you anxious about the rest?' If anxiety is powerless over the smallest concern, why entrust it with larger ones?

verses.text: Consider the lilies, how they grow. They don’t toil, neither do they spin; yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.
verses.explainer: 'Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin, yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.' God's artistry in a wildflower surpasses the wealthiest king's wardrobe.

verses.text: But if this is how God clothes the grass in the field, which today exists, and tomorrow is cast into the oven, how much more will he clothe you, O you of little faith?
verses.explainer: 'But if God so clothes the grass, which is alive in the field today, and tomorrow is thrown into the oven, how much more will he clothe you, O you of little faith!' The argument from lesser to greater: if God adorns temporary grass, he will certainly care for his children.

verses.text: Don’t seek what you will eat or what you will drink; neither be anxious.
verses.explainer: 'And do not seek what you are to eat and what you are to drink, nor be worried.' The command is clear: stop obsessing about material provision.

verses.text: For the nations of the world seek after all of these things, but your Father knows that you need these things.
verses.explainer: 'For all the nations of the world seek after these things, and your Father knows that you need them.' Pagans chase after material security because they have no Father who cares for them. Disciples do.

verses.text: But seek God’s Kingdom, and all these things will be added to you.
verses.explainer: 'Instead, seek his kingdom, and these things will be added to you.' The singular focus should be God's kingdom. Material needs are handled as byproducts of that primary pursuit.

thenVsNow.then: In a subsistence economy where most people lived close to the edge of survival, this teaching was radical. Jesus was asking people who genuinely worried about their next meal to trust God for provision.
thenVsNow.now: In affluent societies, the anxiety Jesus addresses may be less about survival and more about maintaining lifestyle, status, and security. The principle remains: kingdom priorities should displace material anxieties.

verses.text: Don’t be afraid, little flock, for it is your Father’s good pleasure to give you the Kingdom.
verses.explainer: 'Fear not, little flock, for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom.' One of the most tender statements in the Gospels. 'Little flock' acknowledges the disciples' smallness and vulnerability; 'the Father's good pleasure' assures them that the kingdom is a gift, not an achievement.

verses.text: Sell that which you have, and give gifts to the needy. Make for yourselves purses which don’t grow old, a treasure in the heavens that doesn’t fail, where no thief approaches, neither moth destroys.
verses.explainer: 'Sell your possessions, and give to the needy. Provide yourselves with moneybags that do not grow old, with a treasure in the heavens that does not fail, where no thief approaches and no moth destroys.' Kingdom economics: liquidate earthly assets and invest in heavenly ones.

verses.text: For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.
verses.explainer: 'For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.' The heart follows the investment. What you value most determines who you are.

verses.text: “Let your waist be dressed and your lamps burning.
verses.explainer: 'Stay dressed for action and keep your lamps burning.' The imagery shifts to servants waiting for their master's return. Readiness requires active vigilance.

verses.text: Be like men watching for their lord, when he returns from the marriage feast; that, when he comes and knocks, they may immediately open to him.
verses.explainer: 'And be like men who are waiting for their master to come home from the wedding feast, so that they may open the door to him at once when he comes and knocks.' The parable of the watchful servants: be ready to respond immediately when the master returns.

verses.text: Blessed are those servants, whom the lord will find watching when he comes. Most certainly I tell you, that he will dress himself, and make them recline, and will come and serve them.
verses.explainer: 'Blessed are those servants whom the master finds awake when he comes. Truly, I say to you, he will dress himself for service and have them recline at table, and he will come and serve them.' The astonishing reversal: the returning master serves his servants. This is unique to Luke and anticipates Jesus washing the disciples' feet.

verses.text: They will be blessed if he comes in the second or third watch, and finds them so.
verses.explainer: 'If he comes in the second watch, or in the third, and finds them awake, blessed are those servants!' The night watches ran from 9 PM to 6 AM. Whether the master comes at midnight or 3 AM, readiness is rewarded.

verses.text: But know this, that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming, he would have watched, and not allowed his house to be broken into.
verses.explainer: 'But know this, that if the master of the house had known at what hour the thief was coming, he would not have left his house to be broken into.' The shift in metaphor from master to thief emphasizes the unexpected timing.

verses.text: Therefore be ready also, for the Son of Man is coming in an hour that you don’t expect him.”
verses.explainer: 'You also must be ready, for the Son of Man is coming at an hour you do not expect.' The application is direct: be ready for Jesus' return at all times.

verses.text: Peter said to him, “Lord, are you telling this parable to us, or to everybody?”
verses.explainer: Peter asks: 'Lord, are you telling this parable for us or for all?' Peter wants to know if the demanding teaching applies to the inner circle or to everyone.

verses.text:
The Lord said,
“Who then is the faithful and wise steward, whom his lord will set over his household, to give them their portion of food at the right times?
verses.explainer: The Lord says: 'Who then is the faithful and wise manager, whom his master will set over his household, to give them their portion of food at the proper time?' Jesus answers with another parable about stewardship, implying the teaching applies especially to leaders.

verses.text: Blessed is that servant whom his lord will find doing so when he comes.
verses.explainer: 'Blessed is that servant whom his master will find so doing when he comes.' Active faithfulness at the moment of the master's return is what matters.

verses.text: Truly I tell you, that he will set him over all that he has.
verses.explainer: 'Truly, I say to you, he will set him over all his possessions.' Faithful stewardship in small things leads to greater responsibility.

verses.text: But if that servant says in his heart, ‘My lord delays his coming,’ and begins to beat the menservants and the maidservants, and to eat and drink, and to be drunken,
verses.explainer: 'But if that servant says to himself, My master is delayed, and begins to beat the male and female servants, and to eat and drink and get drunk...' The opposite of faithful stewardship: exploiting others and indulging oneself when accountability seems distant.

verses.text: then the lord of that servant will come in a day when he isn’t expecting him, and in an hour that he doesn’t know, and will cut him in two, and place his portion with the unfaithful.
verses.explainer: 'The master of that servant will come on a day when he does not expect him and at an hour he does not know, and will cut him in pieces and put him with the unfaithful.' The judgment is severe and sudden. Presuming on delayed accountability is catastrophically foolish.

verses.text: That servant, who knew his lord’s will, and didn’t prepare, nor do what he wanted, will be beaten with many stripes,
verses.explainer: 'And that servant who knew his master's will but did not get ready or act according to his will, will receive a severe beating.' Knowledge increases responsibility. Those who know what is right and fail to do it face greater consequences.

verses.text: but he who didn’t know, and did things worthy of stripes, will be beaten with few stripes. To whomever much is given, of him will much be required; and to whom much was entrusted, of him more will be asked.
verses.explainer: 'But the one who did not know, and did what deserved a beating, will receive a light beating. Everyone to whom much was given, of him much will be required, and from him to whom they entrusted much, they will demand the more.' The principle of proportional accountability: privilege and knowledge bring corresponding obligation.

thenVsNow.then: This principle challenged the assumption that knowing the Torah was sufficient. Knowledge without obedience increases guilt rather than reducing it.
thenVsNow.now: The principle 'to whom much is given, much is required' has become one of the most widely quoted ethical maxims in Western culture. It applies to wealth, talent, education, opportunity, and any form of privilege.

verses.text: “I came to throw fire on the earth. I wish it were already kindled.
verses.explainer: 'I came to cast fire on the earth, and would that it were already kindled!' Jesus' mission includes judgment and purification. The fire is not destruction but the refining fire of the Spirit and truth.

verses.text: But I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how distressed I am until it is accomplished!
verses.explainer: 'I have a baptism to be baptized with, and how great is my distress until it is accomplished!' The 'baptism' is his coming death -- an immersion in suffering. Jesus feels the weight of anticipation.

verses.text: Do you think that I have come to give peace in the earth? I tell you, no, but rather division.
verses.explainer: 'Do you think that I have come to give peace on earth? No, I tell you, but rather division.' This seems to contradict the angelic announcement of peace (2:14). But the peace Jesus brings first creates division -- between those who accept and those who reject him.

verses.text: For from now on, there will be five in one house divided, three against two, and two against three.
verses.explainer: 'For from now on in one house there will be five divided, three against two and two against three.' The division cuts through families -- the most intimate human bonds are disrupted by the kingdom.

verses.text: They will be divided, father against son, and son against father; mother against daughter, and daughter against her mother; mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law, and daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law.”
verses.explainer: 'They will be divided, father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law.' The list covers every family relationship. Jesus' message is not a gentle addition to existing life but a sword that separates.

verses.text: He said to the multitudes also, “When you see a cloud rising from the west, immediately you say, ‘A shower is coming,’ and so it happens.
verses.explainer: He says to the crowds: 'When you see a cloud rising in the west, you say at once, A shower is coming. And so it happens.' Weather prediction from the Mediterranean direction is basic competence.

verses.text: When a south wind blows, you say, ‘There will be a scorching heat,’ and it happens.
verses.explainer: 'And when you see the south wind blowing, you say, There will be scorching heat, and it happens.' The hot wind from the southern desert is equally predictable.

verses.text: You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of the earth and the sky, but how is it that you don’t interpret this time?
verses.explainer: 'You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of earth and sky, but why do you not know how to interpret the present time?' They can read weather signs but cannot read the signs of the kingdom's arrival. The irony is biting.

verses.text: Why don’t you judge for yourselves what is right?
verses.explainer: 'And why do you not judge for yourselves what is right?' They do not need a teacher to tell them what is morally obvious. Their failure is willful, not intellectual.

verses.text: For when you are going with your adversary before the magistrate, try diligently on the way to be released from him, lest perhaps he drag you to the judge, and the judge deliver you to the officer, and the officer throw you into prison.
verses.explainer: 'As you go with your accuser before the magistrate, make an effort to settle with him on the way, lest he drag you before the judge, and the judge hand you over to the officer, and the officer put you in prison.' The practical advice is also a parable: settle your account with God before it is too late.

verses.text: I tell you, you will by no means get out of there, until you have paid the very last penny.”
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, you will never get out until you have paid the very last penny.' The warning is stark: unresolved accounts will be enforced to the last detail. Now is the time for reconciliation.

explainer.summary: Jesus addresses two current events -- Pilate's slaughter of Galileans and a tower collapse in Siloam -- to teach that tragedy is not proof of special sinfulness but a call to universal repentance. The parable of the barren fig tree warns that God's patience has a limit. A Sabbath healing of a bent woman leads to another controversy. Parables of the mustard seed and leaven describe the kingdom's surprising growth. Jesus warns that the door to the kingdom is narrow and will eventually close. He laments over Jerusalem, the city that kills its prophets.
themes: Repentance as universal necessity
God's patience and the urgency of response
The kingdom's small beginnings and vast growth
The narrow door and the urgency of entering
Jesus' grief over Jerusalem's rejection
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 13 intensifies the travel narrative's urgency. Time is running out. The fig tree has one more year. The door will close. Jerusalem is heading toward judgment. Every parable and teaching pushes toward the same conclusion: respond now.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To change one's mind and direction fundamentally
wordStudies.meaning: To change one's mind and direction fundamentally. In chapter 13, Jesus insists twice that without repentance, everyone will perish. This is not repentance for specific sins but a total reorientation of life toward God.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God's reign or rule, breaking into the present whi
wordStudies.meaning: God's reign or rule, breaking into the present while awaiting full realization. The mustard seed and leaven parables show the kingdom starting small and growing to encompass everything -- an unexpected pattern for those expecting an immediate, dramatic divine intervention.

verses.text: Now there were some present at the same time who told him about the Galileans, whose blood Pilate had mixed with their sacrifices.
verses.explainer: At that time some report to Jesus about Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices. These Galileans were killed by Roman soldiers while offering sacrifices at the Temple -- a horrific act of desecration.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Pilate's massacre of Galileans", 'explanation': "This event is not recorded outside Luke, but it is consistent with what we know of Pilate's brutality. Josephus records similar atrocities. Killing people during worship in the Temple was the most provocative act possible -- defiling the sacred space with innocent blood."}

verses.text:
Jesus answered them,
“Do you think that these Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans, because they suffered such things?
verses.explainer: Jesus asks: 'Do you think that these Galileans were worse sinners than all the other Galileans, because they suffered in this way?' He addresses the common assumption that suffering is punishment for sin.

verses.text: I tell you, no, but unless you repent, you will all perish in the same way.
verses.explainer: 'No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.' Jesus refuses to speculate about the victims' sins. Instead, he turns the tragedy into a call for universal repentance.

verses.text: Or those eighteen, on whom the tower in Siloam fell, and killed them; do you think that they were worse offenders than all the men who dwell in Jerusalem?
verses.explainer: 'Or those eighteen on whom the tower in Siloam fell and killed them: do you think that they were worse offenders than all the others who lived in Jerusalem?' A second example -- an accidental death, not political violence. Same question, same answer.

verses.text: I tell you, no, but, unless you repent, you will all perish in the same way.”
verses.explainer: 'No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all likewise perish.' The repetition drives the point home. The question is not why they died but whether you will be ready when your time comes.

verses.text:
He spoke this parable.
“A certain man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking fruit on it, and found none.
verses.explainer: He tells this parable: 'A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard, and he came seeking fruit on it and found none.' The fig tree in the vineyard -- Israel planted in God's care -- has failed to produce.

verses.text: He said to the vine dresser, ‘Behold, these three years I have come looking for fruit on this fig tree, and found none. Cut it down. Why does it waste the soil?’
verses.explainer: 'He said to the vinedresser, Look, for three years now I have come seeking fruit on this fig tree, and I find none. Cut it down. Why should it use up the ground?' Three years of fruitlessness justify removal.

verses.text: He answered, ‘Lord, leave it alone this year also, until I dig around it, and fertilize it.
verses.explainer: 'The vinedresser answers: Sir, let it alone this year also, until I dig around it and put on manure.' The vinedresser intercedes, asking for one more year of intensive care.

verses.text: If it bears fruit, fine; but if not, after that, you can cut it down.’”
verses.explainer: 'Then if it should bear fruit next year, well and good; but if not, you can cut it down.' Grace has a deadline. God's patience is real but not infinite. This parable captures the tension between mercy and judgment.

thenVsNow.then: The 'one more year' likely symbolizes the period of Jesus' ministry -- one last chance for Israel to repent before judgment falls. The destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD was understood by early Christians as the fulfillment of this warning.
thenVsNow.now: The parable challenges any presumption on God's patience. The extra year is grace, not entitlement. It motivates urgency rather than complacency.

verses.text: He was teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbath day.
verses.explainer: Jesus is teaching in one of the synagogues on the Sabbath. This is the last synagogue scene in Luke before the Temple.

verses.text: Behold, there was a woman who had a spirit of infirmity eighteen years, and she was bent over, and could in no way straighten herself up.
verses.explainer: A woman is there who has had a disabling spirit for eighteen years. She is bent over and cannot fully straighten herself. Eighteen years of physical suffering attributed to spiritual oppression.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'A spirit of infirmity', 'explanation': "Luke identifies the woman's condition as caused by a 'spirit of infirmity' -- a demonic cause for a physical ailment. Ancient medicine did not sharply distinguish between physical and spiritual causes of disease. Luke the physician records this attribution without challenge."}

verses.text:
When Jesus saw her, he called her, and said to her,
“Woman, you are freed from your infirmity.”
verses.explainer: When Jesus sees her, he calls her over and says: 'Woman, you are freed from your disability.' He initiates the encounter -- she did not ask. His compassion is proactive.

verses.text: He laid his hands on her, and immediately she stood up straight, and glorified God.
verses.explainer: He lays his hands on her, and immediately she is made straight, and she glorifies God. Eighteen years of being bent double end in an instant. Her first act as a healed woman is worship.

verses.text: The ruler of the synagogue, being indignant because Jesus had healed on the Sabbath, said to the multitude, “There are six days in which men ought to work. Therefore come on those days and be healed, and not on the Sabbath day!”
verses.explainer: The ruler of the synagogue, indignant because Jesus has healed on the Sabbath, tells the people: 'There are six days in which work ought to be done. Come on those days and be healed, and not on the Sabbath day.' He addresses the crowd rather than Jesus directly -- a cowardly deflection.

thenVsNow.then: The synagogue ruler's argument was technically defensible within certain Pharisaic interpretations. Non-life-threatening conditions could wait until after the Sabbath. But his logic reduces a woman's eighteen years of suffering to a scheduling problem.
thenVsNow.now: The conflict between institutional rules and human compassion recurs in every religious and bureaucratic system. Jesus' response consistently prioritizes human need over procedural compliance.

verses.text:
Therefore the Lord answered him,
“You hypocrites! Doesn’t each one of you free his ox or his donkey from the stall on the Sabbath, and lead him away to water?
verses.explainer: The Lord answers: 'You hypocrites! Does not each of you on the Sabbath untie his ox or his donkey from the manger and lead it away to water it?' If animals deserve care on the Sabbath, how much more a human being.

verses.text: Ought not this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan had bound eighteen long years, be freed from this bondage on the Sabbath day?”
verses.explainer: 'And ought not this woman, a daughter of Abraham whom Satan bound for eighteen years, be loosed from this bond on the Sabbath day?' Jesus calls her 'a daughter of Abraham' -- dignifying her with covenant identity. If livestock are unbound on the Sabbath, surely a covenant member can be unbound from Satan.

verses.text: As he said these things, all his adversaries were disappointed, and all the multitude rejoiced for all the glorious things that were done by him.
verses.explainer: All his adversaries are put to shame, and the whole crowd rejoices at all the glorious things he does. The people see what the leaders refuse to see.

verses.text:
He said,
“What is God’s Kingdom like? To what shall I compare it?
verses.explainer: He says: 'What is the kingdom of God like? And to what shall I compare it?' Jesus searches for the right analogy for something without precedent.

verses.text: It is like a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and put in his own garden. It grew, and became a large tree, and the birds of the sky live in its branches.”
verses.explainer: 'It is like a grain of mustard seed that a man took and sowed in his garden, and it grew and became a tree, and the birds of the air made nests in its branches.' The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest seed. Its growth into a tree large enough for bird nests is disproportionate -- as is the kingdom's growth from tiny beginnings.

verses.text:
Again he said,
“To what shall I compare God’s Kingdom?
verses.explainer: 'And again he says: To what shall I compare the kingdom of God?'

verses.text:
It is like yeast, which a woman took and hid in three measures

of flour, until it was all leavened.”
verses.explainer: 'It is like leaven that a woman took and hid in three measures of flour, until it was all leavened.' Three measures of flour would make bread for about 100 people -- an enormous batch. The leaven works invisibly until the entire mass is transformed.

verses.text: He went on his way through cities and villages, teaching, and traveling on to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jesus goes on his way through towns and villages, teaching and journeying toward Jerusalem. Luke reminds the reader of the destination -- everything is moving toward the cross.

verses.text:
One said to him, “Lord, are they few who are saved?”
He said to them,
verses.explainer: Someone asks: 'Lord, will those who are saved be few?' The question seeks statistical reassurance. Jesus refuses to give a number.

verses.text: “Strive to enter in by the narrow door, for many, I tell you, will seek to enter in, and will not be able.
verses.explainer: 'Strive to enter through the narrow door. For many, I tell you, will seek to enter and will not be able.' The door is narrow, and effort is required. Not everyone who tries will succeed.

verses.text: When once the master of the house has risen up, and has shut the door, and you begin to stand outside, and to knock at the door, saying, ‘Lord, Lord, open to us!’ then he will answer and tell you, ‘I don’t know you or where you come from.’
verses.explainer: 'When once the master of the house has risen and shut the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock at the door, saying, Lord, open to us, then he will answer you, I do not know where you come from.' The door closes. Late arrival is too late.

verses.text: Then you will begin to say, ‘We ate and drank in your presence, and you taught in our streets.’
verses.explainer: 'Then you will begin to say, We ate and drank in your presence, and you taught in our streets.' Proximity to Jesus is not the same as relationship with him. Having been in his audience does not guarantee admission.

verses.text: He will say, ‘I tell you, I don’t know where you come from. Depart from me, all you workers of iniquity.’
verses.explainer: 'But he will say, I tell you, I do not know where you come from. Depart from me, all you workers of evil!' Acquaintance is not enough. The master requires genuine relationship.

verses.text: There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when you see Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and all the prophets, in God’s Kingdom, and yourselves being thrown outside.
verses.explainer: 'In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when you see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God but you yourselves cast out.' The patriarchs are in; those who assumed they belonged may be out.

verses.text: They will come from the east, west, north, and south, and will sit down in God’s Kingdom.
verses.explainer: 'And people will come from east and west, and from north and south, and recline at table in the kingdom of God.' The kingdom banquet welcomes people from every direction -- Gentiles from all over the world. The guest list is not what the original audience expected.

thenVsNow.then: The image of Gentiles feasting with the patriarchs while some of Israel is excluded was deeply provocative. It challenged the assumption that ethnic identity guaranteed salvation.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been foundational for the global missionary movement and for understanding the church as a multiethnic community. The kingdom's table has room for people from every nation and culture.

verses.text: Behold, there are some who are last who will be first, and there are some who are first who will be last.”
verses.explainer: 'And behold, some are last who will be first, and some are first who will be last.' The great reversal: those who assumed first place may find themselves last, and vice versa.

verses.text: On that same day, some Pharisees came, saying to him, “Get out of here, and go away, for Herod wants to kill you.”
verses.explainer: At that very hour some Pharisees come and say: 'Get away from here, for Herod wants to kill you.' Not all Pharisees are hostile -- some warn Jesus about danger. This nuanced portrayal is unique to Luke.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Go and tell that fox, ‘Behold, I cast out demons and perform cures today and tomorrow, and the third day I complete my mission.
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'Go and tell that fox, Behold, I cast out demons and perform cures today and tomorrow, and the third day I finish my course.' Calling Herod a 'fox' was an insult -- foxes were associated with slyness and insignificance. Jesus is not intimidated.

verses.text: Nevertheless I must go on my way today and tomorrow and the next day, for it can’t be that a prophet would perish outside of Jerusalem.’
verses.explainer: 'Nevertheless, I must go on my way today and tomorrow and the day following, for it cannot be that a prophet should perish away from Jerusalem.' Jerusalem is where prophets are killed. Jesus knows his destination and his fate.

verses.text: “Jerusalem, Jerusalem, that kills the prophets, and stones those who are sent to her! How often I wanted to gather your children together, like a hen gathers her own brood under her wings, and you refused!
verses.explainer: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!' One of the most emotionally intense statements in the Gospels. Jesus grieves over Jerusalem like a mother grieving for wayward children.

thenVsNow.then: The image of God as a mother bird protecting her chicks comes from the Old Testament (Deuteronomy 32:11, Ruth 2:12, Psalm 91:4). Jesus applies this maternal metaphor to his own desire to shelter Jerusalem -- and laments their refusal.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is frequently cited as an expression of divine grief over human rejection. It presents God not as an angry judge but as a heartbroken parent. The image of the hen gathering chicks has shaped Christian art and theology about God's compassionate nature.

verses.text: Behold, your house is left to you desolate. I tell you, you will not see me, until you say, ‘Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!’”
verses.explainer: 'Behold, your house is forsaken. And I tell you, you will not see me until you say, Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.' The Temple will be abandoned, and Jerusalem will not see Jesus again until it recognizes him for who he is. This anticipates both the Triumphal Entry and the destruction of the Temple.

explainer.summary: At a third Pharisee's dinner, Jesus heals a man with dropsy on the Sabbath and silences his critics. He teaches about humility at banquets, telling guests to take the lowest seat and hosts to invite the poor rather than the rich. The parable of the great banquet shows those originally invited making excuses while the poor and outsiders fill the table. Jesus concludes with stark warnings about the cost of discipleship -- anyone who does not carry his cross cannot follow him.
themes: Sabbath as a day for liberation
Humility and the reversal of social status
The great banquet and the inclusion of outcasts
The radical cost of following Jesus
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 is structured around a dinner party that becomes a classroom. Jesus uses the social dynamics of a formal meal to teach about the kingdom. Every lesson inverts normal expectations: take the low seat, invite the uninvited, count the cost before committing.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Edema -- abnormal accumulation of fluid causing sw
wordStudies.meaning: Edema -- abnormal accumulation of fluid causing swelling. Luke the physician uses the precise medical term. In some rabbinic texts, dropsy was associated with greed or sexual sin, which may add a layer of social stigma to the man's condition.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A formal dinner or feast
wordStudies.meaning: A formal dinner or feast. Luke uses meals as theological settings more than any other Gospel. In this chapter, the human banquet becomes a parable of the heavenly banquet -- and the question is who will actually be there.

verses.text: When he went into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, they were watching him.
verses.explainer: One Sabbath, when Jesus goes to dine at the house of a ruler of the Pharisees, they are watching him carefully. This is the third Pharisee dinner in Luke. The atmosphere is surveillance, not hospitality.

verses.text: Behold, a certain man who had dropsy was in front of him.
verses.explainer: And behold, there is a man before him who has dropsy. The man with dropsy may have been placed there deliberately as a test -- will Jesus heal on the Sabbath again?

verses.text:
Jesus, answering, spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying,
“Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath?”
verses.explainer: Jesus responds to the lawyers and Pharisees: 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?' He puts the question to them first. They cannot answer without either condoning his healing or appearing heartless.

verses.text:
But they were silent.
He took him, and healed him, and let him go.
verses.explainer: They remain silent. He takes the man, heals him, and sends him away. Their silence speaks volumes -- they cannot defend their position publicly.

verses.text:
He answered them,
“Which of you, if your son
or an ox fell into a well, wouldn’t immediately pull him out on a Sabbath day?”
verses.explainer: He says to them: 'Which of you, having a son or an ox that has fallen into a well on a Sabbath day, will not immediately pull him out?' The argument from common practice: they already make exceptions for animals and family. Why not for a sick man?

verses.text: They couldn’t answer him regarding these things.
verses.explainer: And they cannot reply to these things. Jesus wins the argument through logic and compassion combined.

verses.text: He spoke a parable to those who were invited, when he noticed how they chose the best seats, and said to them,
verses.explainer: Now he tells a parable to those who are invited, when he notices how they choose the places of honor. Jesus observes the social jockeying at the dinner table and uses it as a teaching moment.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Places of honor at table', 'explanation': 'Formal meals had a strict seating hierarchy. The host reclined at the center, with the most honored guests nearest to him. Claiming a higher seat than your status warranted was a social gamble -- if someone more important arrived, you would be publicly demoted.'}

verses.text: “When you are invited by anyone to a marriage feast, don’t sit in the best seat, since perhaps someone more honorable than you might be invited by him,
verses.explainer: 'When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not sit down in a place of honor, lest someone more distinguished than you be invited by him.' The practical advice is also a parable about the kingdom.

verses.text: and he who invited both of you would come and tell you, ‘Make room for this person.’ Then you would begin, with shame, to take the lowest place.
verses.explainer: 'And he who invited you both will come and say to you, Give your place to this person, and then you will begin with shame to take the lowest place.' Public demotion in an honor-shame culture was devastating.

verses.text: But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when he who invited you comes, he may tell you, ‘Friend, move up higher.’ Then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at the table with you.
verses.explainer: 'But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when your host comes he may say to you, Friend, move up higher. Then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at table with you.' The strategy of humility: take the lowest place and let the host elevate you.

verses.text: For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted.”
verses.explainer: 'For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted.' The great reversal stated as a universal principle. This verse appears three times in Luke (14:11, 18:14; cf. 1:52).

verses.text:
He also said to the one who had invited him,
“When you make a dinner or a supper, don’t call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor rich neighbors, or perhaps they might also return the favor, and pay you back.
verses.explainer: He says to the man who invited him: 'When you give a dinner or a banquet, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and you be repaid.' The host gets his own lesson: hospitality should not be a social investment strategy.

verses.text: But when you make a feast, ask the poor, the maimed, the lame, or the blind;
verses.explainer: 'But when you give a feast, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind.' These are the people who cannot repay you. Hospitality becomes genuine generosity only when reciprocation is impossible.

thenVsNow.then: In the honor-based social system, dinner invitations were a form of social currency. You invited people who could invite you back -- it was networking through meals. Jesus overturns this entirely.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of inviting those who cannot repay challenges every social gathering organized around mutual benefit. It applies to charitable giving, church outreach, and any context where genuine generosity is tested by the impossibility of return.

verses.text: and you will be blessed, because they don’t have the resources to repay you. For you will be repaid in the resurrection of the righteous.”
verses.explainer: 'And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you. For you will be repaid at the resurrection of the just.' The reward comes from God, not from social reciprocity.

verses.text: When one of those who sat at the table with him heard these things, he said to him, “Blessed is he who will feast in God’s Kingdom!”
verses.explainer: One of those at table says: 'Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!' A pious platitude that Jesus will immediately complicate with a parable.

verses.text:
But he said to him,
“A certain man made a great supper, and he invited many people.
verses.explainer: But Jesus says: 'A man once gave a great banquet and invited many.' The parable of the great banquet begins. The host represents God; the banquet represents the kingdom.

verses.text: He sent out his servant at supper time to tell those who were invited, ‘Come, for everything is ready now.’
verses.explainer: 'And at the time for the banquet he sent his servant to say to those who had been invited, Come, for everything is now ready.' The double invitation was standard practice: first an initial invitation, then a servant sent when the meal was prepared.

verses.text:
They all as one began to make excuses.
“The first said to him, ‘I have bought a field, and I must go and see it. Please have me excused.’
