verses.explainer: 'But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said to him, I have bought a field, and I must go out and see it. Please have me excused.' Property interests override the invitation.

verses.text: “Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I must go try them out. Please have me excused.’
verses.explainer: 'And another said, I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to examine them. Please have me excused.' Business interests override the invitation. Five yoke of oxen represented significant wealth.

verses.text: “Another said, ‘I have married a wife, and therefore I can’t come.’
verses.explainer: 'And another said, I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come.' Family interests override the invitation. Under Deuteronomy 24:5, a newly married man was exempt from military service for one year. The man extends this exemption to the banquet.

verses.text: “That servant came, and told his lord these things. Then the master of the house, being angry, said to his servant, ‘Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in the poor, maimed, blind, and lame.’
verses.explainer: 'The master of the house became angry and said to his servant, Go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame.' The same four categories Jesus mentioned in verse 13. The uninvitable become the invited. The guest list is reversed.

thenVsNow.then: The parable reflects the early church's experience: the Jewish establishment largely rejected the gospel, while the poor, tax collectors, and sinners received it. The outsiders filled the seats the insiders refused.
thenVsNow.now: The parable speaks to any situation where those who should be most receptive to good news are the most resistant, while those considered least worthy embrace it enthusiastically.

verses.text: “The servant said, ‘Lord, it is done as you commanded, and there is still room.’
verses.explainer: 'And the servant said, Sir, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.' Even after the outcasts are gathered, the banquet has space. God's table is larger than anyone expects.

verses.text: “The lord said to the servant, ‘Go out into the highways and hedges, and compel them to come in, that my house may be filled.
verses.explainer: 'And the master said to the servant, Go out to the highways and hedges and compel people to come in, that my house may be filled.' Beyond the city walls, into the countryside -- the invitation extends to travelers and strangers. The word 'compel' (anagkason) suggests urgent persuasion, not force.

verses.text: For I tell you that none of those men who were invited will taste of my supper.’”
verses.explainer: 'For I tell you, none of those men who were invited shall taste my banquet.' The original invitees have excluded themselves through their excuses. The door remains open -- but not forever.

verses.text: Now great multitudes were going with him. He turned and said to them,
verses.explainer: Now great crowds accompany him, and he turns and says to them: The crowd is enthusiastic, but Jesus challenges their commitment rather than encouraging their numbers.

verses.text:
“If anyone comes to me, and doesn’t disregard
his own father, mother, wife, children, brothers, and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he can’t be my disciple.
verses.explainer: 'If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.' The word 'hate' is Semitic hyperbole for 'love less by comparison.' Jesus demands that loyalty to him surpass even the deepest family bonds.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hating family', 'explanation': "In Semitic idiom, 'hate' could mean 'love less' or 'place second.' The Hebrew Bible uses it this way: God 'loved' Jacob and 'hated' Esau (Malachi 1:2-3), meaning preferred one over the other. Jesus is not commanding emotional hatred but absolute prioritization of discipleship over every other loyalty."}

verses.text: Whoever doesn’t bear his own cross, and come after me, can’t be my disciple.
verses.explainer: 'Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple.' The cross was not a metaphor for inconvenience but the Roman instrument of execution. Anyone who had seen a crucifixion procession knew what carrying a cross meant -- walking toward death.

verses.text: For which of you, desiring to build a tower, doesn’t first sit down and count the cost, to see if he has enough to complete it?
verses.explainer: 'For which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost, whether he has enough to complete it?' Jesus wants informed commitment, not impulsive enthusiasm.

verses.text: Or perhaps, when he has laid a foundation, and is not able to finish, everyone who sees begins to mock him,
verses.explainer: 'Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him.' An unfinished building is a monument to poor planning and inadequate commitment.

verses.text: saying, ‘This man began to build, and wasn’t able to finish.’
verses.explainer: 'Saying, This man began to build and was not able to finish.' The shame of incompletion. Half-hearted discipleship is worse than no discipleship.

verses.text: Or what king, as he goes to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?
verses.explainer: 'Or what king, going out to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and deliberate whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?' A military analogy: wise leaders assess the odds before committing to battle.

verses.text: Or else, while the other is yet a great way off, he sends an envoy, and asks for conditions of peace.
verses.explainer: 'And if not, while the other is yet a great way off, he sends a delegation and asks for terms of peace.' If you cannot win, negotiate. The point is not cowardice but realism about what commitment requires.

verses.text: So therefore whoever of you who doesn’t renounce all that he has, he can’t be my disciple.
verses.explainer: 'So therefore, any one of you who does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple.' The summary is absolute: discipleship requires total surrender of possessions and self-determination.

verses.text: Salt is good, but if the salt becomes flat and tasteless, with what do you season it?
verses.explainer: 'Salt is good, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored?' Disciples are like salt -- valuable only if they maintain their distinctive character.

verses.text: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile. It is thrown out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.”
verses.explainer: 'It is of no use either for the soil or for the manure pile. It is thrown away. He who has ears to hear, let him hear.' Useless salt is utterly worthless. The warning applies to disciples who lose their commitment.

explainer.summary: Chapter 15 is the theological heart of Luke's Gospel and perhaps the most beautiful chapter in the entire Bible. Three parables -- the lost sheep, the lost coin, and the prodigal son -- respond to the Pharisees' grumbling that Jesus welcomes sinners and eats with them. Each parable celebrates the joy of recovery: a shepherd searches for one lost sheep, a woman sweeps her house for one lost coin, and a father waits and runs to embrace a son who squandered his inheritance. The third parable extends into the story of the older brother, who represents the Pharisees -- faithful in duty but lacking in love, unable to celebrate his brother's return.
themes: God's relentless pursuit of the lost
The scandal of grace
Joy in heaven over one sinner who repents
The elder brother's resentment as a mirror of Pharisaic religion
The father's love as a portrait of God
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 answers the question posed throughout Luke: why does Jesus associate with sinners? The answer is not tolerance but passionate love. God is not indifferent to the lost -- he searches, sweeps, waits, and runs. The chapter is the climax of Luke's theme of divine compassion for outcasts.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The word carries a double meaning: 'lost' and 'des
wordStudies.meaning: The word carries a double meaning: 'lost' and 'destroyed/perished.' The sheep is not merely misplaced but in mortal danger. The coin is not just missing but useless where it lies. The son is not wandering but 'dead' (v.24). Being lost is a life-threatening condition that requires rescue.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To be moved in one's bowels/gut -- the deepest vis
wordStudies.meaning: To be moved in one's bowels/gut -- the deepest visceral emotion. When the father sees his son returning, he is 'moved with compassion' -- the same word used for Jesus' response to the widow of Nain (7:13) and the Good Samaritan's response to the beaten man (10:33). It describes an emotion so intense it produces immediate action.

wordStudies.greek: Not a biblical word; English 'prodigal' means 'recklessly extravagant'
wordStudies.translatedAs: The parable is commonly called 'the Prodigal Son,'
wordStudies.meaning: The parable is commonly called 'the Prodigal Son,' though many scholars suggest 'the Prodigal Father' would be more accurate -- it is the father's extravagant, reckless love that is the true focus of the story.

verses.text: Now all the tax collectors and sinners were coming close to him to hear him.
verses.explainer: Now the tax collectors and sinners are all drawing near to hear Jesus. The 'wrong' people are attracted to Jesus. His message resonates with those whom respectable society has rejected.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Tax collectors and sinners', 'explanation': "Tax collectors collaborated with Rome and were ritually and socially unclean. 'Sinners' was a catch-all term for people who did not observe the Torah -- whether through occupation (shepherds, tanners), behavior (prostitutes), or ignorance. These were the people the religious system had written off."}

verses.text: The Pharisees and the scribes murmured, saying, “This man welcomes sinners, and eats with them.”
verses.explainer: And the Pharisees and the scribes grumble, saying: 'This man receives sinners and eats with them.' The complaint that triggers the three greatest parables Jesus ever told. Eating with sinners means accepting them as equals -- which violates every purity boundary.

thenVsNow.then: The Pharisees' complaint was rooted in genuine theological concern. Table fellowship implied acceptance, and accepting sinners without requiring prior repentance and ritual purification undermined the holiness system. Their question was real: how can God's representative welcome the unholy?
thenVsNow.now: The Pharisees' question resurfaces in every generation: who belongs at the table? Who must change before being welcomed? Jesus' response -- that God actively pursues the lost rather than waiting for them to become worthy -- remains the most radical claim in Christian theology.

verses.text: He told them this parable.
verses.explainer: So he tells them this parable: Jesus' answer to the grumbling is not argument but story. Three parables, each escalating in emotional intensity.

verses.text: “Which of you men, if you had one hundred sheep, and lost one of them, wouldn’t leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness, and go after the one that was lost, until he found it?
verses.explainer: 'What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it?' The question assumes any reasonable person would do this. But leaving ninety-nine sheep unprotected to search for one is actually reckless -- unless the one matters that much.

verses.text: When he has found it, he carries it on his shoulders, rejoicing.
verses.explainer: 'And when he has found it, he lays it on his shoulders, rejoicing.' The shepherd does not scold the sheep. He does not make it walk back as punishment. He carries it home on his shoulders, rejoicing. The response to finding the lost is joy, not anger.

verses.text: When he comes home, he calls together his friends and his neighbors, saying to them, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep which was lost!’
verses.explainer: 'And when he comes home, he calls together his friends and his neighbors, saying to them, Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost.' The joy is communal -- finding the lost is cause for celebration, not private relief.

verses.text: I tell you that even so there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents, than over ninety-nine righteous people who need no repentance.
verses.explainer: 'Just so, I tell you, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance.' Heaven's joy is disproportionately focused on the recovered lost. The implication stings: the grumbling Pharisees are not sharing heaven's joy.

thenVsNow.then: This verse inverted the expected religious calculus. The righteous assumed they were the cause of heaven's joy. Jesus says heaven's party is for the repentant sinner, not the perpetually observant.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has profoundly shaped Christian soteriology (theology of salvation). It grounds the missionary impulse: if heaven rejoices over one repentant sinner, then seeking the lost is the most important work imaginable.

verses.text:
Or what woman, if she had ten drachma
coins, if she lost one drachma coin, wouldn’t light a lamp, sweep the house, and seek diligently until she found it?
verses.explainer: 'Or what woman, having ten silver coins, if she loses one coin, does not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek diligently until she finds it?' The second parable shifts to a woman's world. Ten silver coins (drachmas) may have been her entire savings or her dowry -- coins sometimes worn as a headband for a marriage dowry.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Silver drachma', 'explanation': "A drachma was roughly equivalent to a day's wage. Ten drachmas may have constituted the woman's dowry or savings -- her entire economic security. The loss of one coin was not trivial but devastating. Some scholars suggest the coins were part of a married woman's headdress, making the loss both financial and sentimental."}

verses.text: When she has found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, ‘Rejoice with me, for I have found the drachma which I had lost.’
verses.explainer: 'And when she has found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, Rejoice with me, for I have found the coin that I had lost.' Again, communal joy at the recovery of what was lost.

verses.text: Even so, I tell you, there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner repenting.”
verses.explainer: 'Just so, I tell you, there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents.' The point is driven home again: heaven celebrates repentance.

verses.text:
He said,
“A certain man had two sons.
verses.explainer: And he says: 'There was a man who had two sons.' The parable of the prodigal son begins -- the longest, most detailed, and most emotionally complex parable in the Gospels. It is really a parable about the father and both sons.

thenVsNow.then: The opening line sets up a story that every listener would recognize: two sons, one father, and an inheritance dispute. Family inheritance conflicts were common in first-century Palestine, and they were always devastating.
thenVsNow.now: The prodigal son is one of the most famous stories in world literature. It has inspired paintings by Rembrandt and Rubens, novels, films, operas, and countless sermons. Henri Nouwen's 'The Return of the Prodigal Son' is among the most widely read spiritual books of the 20th century.

verses.text: The younger of them said to his father, ‘Father, give me my share of your property.’ He divided his livelihood between them.
verses.explainer: 'And the younger of them said to his father, Father, give me the share of property that is coming to me. And he divided his property between them.' The younger son's request is shocking -- it is tantamount to saying, 'Father, I wish you were dead.' He wants his inheritance while his father is still alive.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Requesting inheritance early', 'explanation': "Under Jewish law (Mishnah Baba Batra 8:7), a father could divide his estate during his lifetime, but the sons would not normally receive the proceeds until the father's death. The younger son's demand for his portion now was a profound insult -- treating his father as already dead. It violated the fifth commandment ('Honor your father and mother') in the most personal way possible."}

verses.text: Not many days after, the younger son gathered all of this together and traveled into a far country. There he wasted his property with riotous living.
verses.explainer: 'Not many days later, the younger son gathered all he had and took a journey into a far country, and there he squandered his property in reckless living.' He converts his inheritance to cash, leaves the land, and wastes everything. Each step takes him farther from his father and his identity.

verses.text: When he had spent all of it, there arose a severe famine in that country, and he began to be in need.
verses.explainer: 'And when he had spent everything, a severe famine arose in that country, and he began to be in need.' External crisis compounds personal failure. The combination of self-inflicted poverty and famine creates total destitution.

verses.text: He went and joined himself to one of the citizens of that country, and he sent him into his fields to feed pigs.
verses.explainer: 'So he went and hired himself out to one of the citizens of that country, who sent him into his fields to feed pigs.' For a Jewish listener, this is the absolute bottom. Pigs are unclean animals. A Jewish man feeding pigs in a Gentile country has lost everything -- money, dignity, religious identity, and community.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Feeding pigs', 'explanation': "Pigs were the most unclean animal in Jewish law (Leviticus 11:7). The Talmud later said, 'Cursed is the man who raises pigs.' For this Jewish son to be feeding pigs meant he had fallen below the lowest point any Jewish audience could imagine. He had become, functionally, a Gentile."}

verses.text: He wanted to fill his belly with the husks that the pigs ate, but no one gave him any.
verses.explainer: 'And he was longing to be fed with the pods that the pigs ate, and no one gave him anything.' He envies pigs. The carob pods were animal fodder, not human food. No one -- not one person in this far country -- shows him kindness. He has reached absolute zero.

thenVsNow.then: The detail that 'no one gave him anything' contrasts with the generous hospitality Jesus teaches elsewhere. In a foreign land with no covenant community, the son discovers that the world without the father is merciless.
thenVsNow.now: The son's descent has resonated with millions who have experienced addiction, financial ruin, or moral collapse. The progression from freedom to slavery, from wealth to pig food, mirrors the trajectory of any life lived purely for self-gratification.

verses.text: But when he came to himself he said, ‘How many hired servants of my father’s have bread enough to spare, and I’m dying with hunger!
verses.explainer: 'But when he came to himself, he said, How many of my father's hired servants have more than enough bread, but I perish here with hunger!' 'He came to himself' -- the moment of self-awareness. His father's servants live better than he does as a free man. The contrast breaks through his delusion.

verses.text: I will get up and go to my father, and will tell him, “Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in your sight.
verses.explainer: 'I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you.' He prepares a confession that acknowledges sin against both God ('heaven') and his father. The speech is rehearsed -- a calculated appeal for mercy.

verses.text: I am no more worthy to be called your son. Make me as one of your hired servants.”’
verses.explainer: 'I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me as one of your hired servants.' His plan is modest: he asks for a servant's position, not restoration as a son. He has no illusions about his worthiness.

verses.text: “He arose, and came to his father. But while he was still far off, his father saw him, and was moved with compassion, and ran, and fell on his neck, and kissed him.
verses.explainer: 'And he arose and came to his father. But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him.' This is the pivotal verse of the parable and perhaps of the entire Gospel. The father sees him 'a long way off' -- he has been watching, waiting, scanning the horizon. He runs -- in the ancient Near East, a dignified older man never ran; it was shameful to hitch up his robes and sprint. He embraces and kisses the filthy, pig-smelling son before a word of confession is spoken.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The running father', 'explanation': 'In Mediterranean honor culture, a patriarch never ran. Running required lifting the hem of his robe, exposing his legs -- an undignified act. That the father runs to the son means he is willing to absorb shame upon himself to reach his child. Some scholars see this as a picture of the atonement: the father bears the shame so the son does not have to.'}

thenVsNow.then: Every element of the father's response violates social convention. He should wait for the son to approach, demand an accounting, impose a period of penance, and conditionally restore him. Instead, he runs, embraces, and restores before the son can even finish his speech.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is the single most powerful depiction of God's grace in the Bible. It has shaped how billions of people understand God's response to human failure. The running father is not a passive deity waiting for perfect repentance but an active, pursuing, embracing love that meets sinners where they are.

verses.text: The son said to him, ‘Father, I have sinned against heaven, and in your sight. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.’
verses.explainer: 'And the son said to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.' He begins his rehearsed speech, but notice: he never gets to the part about being a hired servant. The father interrupts the confession with restoration.

verses.text: “But the father said to his servants, ‘Bring out the best robe, and put it on him. Put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet.
verses.explainer: 'But the father said to his servants, Bring quickly the best robe, and put it on him, and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet.' Each gift is a symbol of restored status: the robe is the finest garment in the house (the father's own?), the ring signifies authority and family membership, and shoes mark him as a son, not a servant (servants went barefoot).

verses.text: Bring the fattened calf, kill it, and let us eat, and celebrate;
verses.explainer: 'And bring the fattened calf and kill it, and let us eat and celebrate.' The fattened calf was reserved for the most special occasions. This is not a routine meal but a banquet. The celebration is immediate and extravagant.

verses.text: for this, my son, was dead, and is alive again. He was lost, and is found.’ They began to celebrate.
verses.explainer: 'For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found. And they began to celebrate.' Dead and alive. Lost and found. The same language that structures all three parables climaxes here in personal, familial terms. The father throws a party -- exactly as the shepherd and the woman did.

verses.text: “Now his elder son was in the field. As he came near to the house, he heard music and dancing.
verses.explainer: 'Now his older son was in the field, and as he came and drew near to the house, he heard music and dancing.' The parable is not over. The older son -- representing the Pharisees and scribes who grumbled in verse 2 -- now enters the story.

verses.text: He called one of the servants to him, and asked what was going on.
verses.explainer: 'And he called one of the servants and asked what these things meant.' The older son does not enter the house. He stands outside and inquires about the celebration.

verses.text: He said to him, ‘Your brother has come, and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he has received him back safe and healthy.’
verses.explainer: 'And he said to him, Your brother has come, and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he has received him back safe and sound.' The servant reports the facts. The older son's response will reveal his heart.

verses.text: But he was angry, and would not go in. Therefore his father came out, and begged him.
verses.explainer: 'But he was angry and refused to go in. His father came out and entreated him.' The older son refuses to join the celebration. His anger mirrors the Pharisees' grumbling. And the father -- who ran to the younger son -- now comes out to the older one. He pursues both sons.

thenVsNow.then: The older son's refusal to enter the banquet was a public insult to the father -- almost as great as the younger son's original departure. In an honor culture, refusing to celebrate with the family was a declaration of separation.
thenVsNow.now: The older brother represents every religious person who keeps the rules but lacks the father's heart. He is faithful in duty but incapable of joy over grace extended to the undeserving. His resentment is the shadow side of religion: obedience without love.

verses.text: But he answered his father, ‘Behold, these many years I have served you, and I never disobeyed a commandment of yours, but you never gave me a goat, that I might celebrate with my friends.
verses.explainer: 'But he answered his father, Look, these many years I have served you, and I never disobeyed your command, yet you never gave me a young goat, that I might celebrate with my friends.' His complaint reveals everything: he sees himself as a servant ('served you'), not a son. He has obeyed out of duty, not love. He resents the father's generosity to the brother.

verses.text: But when this your son came, who has devoured your living with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him.’
verses.explainer: 'But when this son of yours came, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him!' He says 'this son of yours' -- refusing to say 'my brother.' He adds the detail about prostitutes, which was not mentioned earlier. He exaggerates the offense to justify his resentment.

verses.text: “He said to him, ‘Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.
verses.explainer: 'And he said to him, Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.' The father's response is tender and generous. Everything belongs to the older son -- he has lost nothing. The younger son's restoration does not diminish his inheritance.

verses.text: But it was appropriate to celebrate and be glad, for this, your brother, was dead, and is alive again. He was lost, and is found.’”
verses.explainer: 'It was fitting to celebrate and be glad, for this your brother was dead, and is alive; he was lost, and is found.' The father gently corrects: 'this your brother' -- restoring the family language the older son refused to use. The parable ends without telling us whether the older brother enters the feast. The question is left open -- directed at the grumbling Pharisees and at every reader who identifies with the dutiful, resentful elder child.

thenVsNow.then: The open ending is deliberate. Jesus does not resolve the older brother's story because the resolution depends on the Pharisees' own response. Will they enter the feast and celebrate the return of sinners? Or will they stand outside in self-righteous anger?
thenVsNow.now: The open ending continues to challenge readers. Many people raised in religious environments identify more with the older brother than the younger. The parable insists that both sons need the father's love -- the wayward and the dutiful. Neither earns it.

explainer.summary: Two parables about money frame this chapter. The parable of the shrewd manager is one of the most puzzling in the Gospels -- Jesus apparently praises a dishonest steward for his cleverness. The point is not that dishonesty is admirable but that worldly people are more strategic about their future than spiritual people are. The parable of the rich man and Lazarus provides a devastating portrait of what happens when wealth blinds us to the suffering at our doorstep. Between them, Jesus teaches that faithfulness in small matters (including money) reveals character, and that no one can serve both God and money.
themes: Strategic use of wealth for eternal purposes
Faithfulness in small things as a test of character
The impossibility of serving God and money
The reversal of rich and poor in the afterlife
Scripture as sufficient warning
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 16 extends the economic themes that pervade Luke's Gospel. After the prodigal's squandering in chapter 15, these parables address how to use money wisely. The rich man and Lazarus serves as the negative example that the prodigal son's father provides the positive: generosity versus hoarding.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Wealth or material possessions personified as a ri
wordStudies.meaning: Wealth or material possessions personified as a rival deity. Jesus' statement that no one can serve both God and mammon treats money as a spiritual power that demands allegiance. The word suggests that wealth is not neutral -- it has the capacity to become an idol.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The realm of the dead
wordStudies.meaning: The realm of the dead. In the parable of the rich man and Lazarus, Hades is a place of conscious torment separated from the righteous by an impassable chasm. This is the most detailed picture of the afterlife in any of Jesus' teachings.

verses.text:
He also said to his disciples,
“There was a certain rich man who had a manager. An accusation was made to him that this man was wasting his possessions.
verses.explainer: Jesus also says to the disciples: 'There was a rich man who had a manager, and charges were brought to him that this man was wasting his possessions.' The parable addresses the disciples, not the Pharisees. The manager is about to lose his job.

verses.text: He called him, and said to him, ‘What is this that I hear about you? Give an accounting of your management, for you can no longer be manager.’
verses.explainer: 'And he called him and said to him, What is this that I hear about you? Turn in the account of your management, for you can no longer be manager.' The manager faces termination. His response demonstrates remarkable cunning.

verses.text: “The manager said within himself, ‘What will I do, seeing that my lord is taking away the management position from me? I don’t have strength to dig. I am ashamed to beg.
verses.explainer: 'And the manager said to himself, What shall I do, since my master is taking the management away from me? I am not strong enough to dig, and I am ashamed to beg.' He assesses his limited options honestly: manual labor is beyond him, begging is beneath him.

verses.text: I know what I will do, so that when I am removed from management, they may receive me into their houses.’
verses.explainer: 'I have decided what to do, so that when I am removed from management, people may receive me into their houses.' His plan: create future allies by reducing their debts now. He invests in relationships using his master's resources.

verses.text: Calling each one of his lord’s debtors to him, he said to the first, ‘How much do you owe to my lord?’
verses.explainer: 'So, summoning his master's debtors one by one, he said to the first, How much do you owe my master?' He acts quickly, before the news of his firing becomes known.

verses.text: He said, ‘A hundred batos of oil.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.’
verses.explainer: 'He said, A hundred measures of oil. He said to him, Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.' The debt is cut in half -- a reduction of about 500 denarii, a substantial sum.

verses.text: Then he said to another, ‘How much do you owe?’ He said, ‘A hundred cors of wheat.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and write eighty.’
verses.explainer: 'Then he said to another, And how much do you owe? He said, A hundred measures of wheat. He said to him, Take your bill, and write eighty.' A twenty percent reduction on a large wheat debt.

verses.text: “His lord commended the dishonest manager because he had done wisely, for the children of this world are, in their own generation, wiser than the children of the light.
verses.explainer: 'The master commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness. For the sons of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than the sons of light.' The master praises the cleverness, not the dishonesty. The point: worldly people plan strategically for their future while religious people are naive about theirs.

thenVsNow.then: Some scholars suggest the manager was removing his own commission (the markup he charged on top of the master's debt), in which case his action was not technically dishonest but self-sacrificing. Others see it as genuine financial manipulation that the master grudgingly admires.
thenVsNow.now: The parable's application is not 'be dishonest' but 'be as strategic about eternal things as dishonest people are about temporary things.' Use present resources to create future security -- but in the kingdom of God, not the world of finance.

verses.text: I tell you, make for yourselves friends by means of unrighteous mammon, so that when you fail, they may receive you into the eternal tents.
verses.explainer: 'And I tell you, make friends for yourselves by means of unrighteous wealth, so that when it fails they may receive you into the eternal dwellings.' Use money to serve people and build relationships with eternal value. Money will eventually fail; the relationships it builds can last forever.

verses.text: He who is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much. He who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much.
verses.explainer: 'One who is faithful in a very little is also faithful in much, and one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much.' Character is consistent. How you handle small things reveals how you will handle large ones.

verses.text: If therefore you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?
verses.explainer: 'If then you have not been faithful in the unrighteous wealth, who will entrust to you the true riches?' Money is the lesser test. If you fail with mere money, you will not be trusted with spiritual wealth.

verses.text: If you have not been faithful in that which is another’s, who will give you that which is your own?
verses.explainer: 'And if you have not been faithful in that which is another's, who will give you that which is your own?' All earthly wealth is stewardship of God's resources. Only faithfulness with borrowed goods earns you your own.

verses.text: No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to one, and despise the other. You aren’t able to serve God and Mammon.”
verses.explainer: 'No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.' The definitive statement on money and faith. Dual allegiance is impossible -- one master will always dominate.

verses.text: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, also heard all these things, and they scoffed at him.
verses.explainer: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, heard all these things, and they ridiculed him. Luke identifies their economic interest: their mockery stems from threatened financial values.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“You are those who justify yourselves in the sight of men, but God knows your hearts. For that which is exalted among men is an abomination in the sight of God.
verses.explainer: He says to them: 'You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts. For what is exalted among men is an abomination in the sight of God.' Human honor and divine valuation are inverted. What impresses people disgusts God.

verses.text: The law and the prophets were until John. From that time the Good News of God’s Kingdom is preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it.
verses.explainer: 'The Law and the Prophets were until John; since then the good news of the kingdom of God is preached, and everyone forces his way into it.' John marks the transition point between the old era and the new.

verses.text: But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away, than for one tiny stroke of a pen in the law to fall.
verses.explainer: 'But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one dot of the Law to become void.' The Law is not abolished but fulfilled. Its moral demands remain in force.

verses.text: Everyone who divorces his wife, and marries another, commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
verses.explainer: 'Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.' A specific application of the Law's enduring authority to marriage ethics.

verses.text: “Now there was a certain rich man, and he was clothed in purple and fine linen, living in luxury every day.
verses.explainer: 'There was a rich man who was clothed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day.' The rich man's lifestyle is extravagant: purple dye was enormously expensive, fine linen was luxury clothing, and daily feasting was extreme indulgence.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Purple and fine linen', 'explanation': 'Purple dye, extracted from murex shellfish, was so expensive that it became associated with royalty and extreme wealth. Fine linen (byssos) was imported Egyptian fabric. Together they represent the highest possible standard of living in the ancient world.'}

verses.text: A certain beggar, named Lazarus, was taken to his gate, full of sores,
verses.explainer: 'And at his gate was laid a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores.' Lazarus is the only named character in any of Jesus' parables. His name means 'God helps' -- ironic given his apparent helplessness. He lies at the rich man's gate, visible but ignored.

verses.text: and desiring to be fed with the crumbs that fell from the rich man’s table. Yes, even the dogs came and licked his sores.
verses.explainer: 'who desired to be fed with what fell from the rich man's table. Moreover, even the dogs came and licked his sores.' Lazarus does not even get scraps. The dogs that lick his sores are not companions but scavengers -- adding humiliation to his suffering.

verses.text: The beggar died, and he was carried away by the angels to Abraham’s bosom. The rich man also died, and was buried.
verses.explainer: 'The poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham's side. The rich man also died and was buried.' In death, the reversal begins. Lazarus is carried by angels; the rich man simply dies and is buried. No angels attend the funeral of the wealthy.

verses.text: In Hades, he lifted up his eyes, being in torment, and saw Abraham far off, and Lazarus at his bosom.
verses.explainer: 'And in Hades, being in torment, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far off and Lazarus at his side.' The rich man is in conscious torment. He can see Lazarus in comfort -- the gate has become a chasm, and their positions are reversed.

thenVsNow.then: This parable provides the most vivid picture of afterlife reversal in Jesus' teaching. The rich man who ignored Lazarus at his gate now cannot cross the chasm to reach him. Proximity to suffering in this life, if ignored, becomes separation in the next.
thenVsNow.now: The parable has profoundly influenced Christian thinking about the afterlife, social justice, and the moral consequences of ignoring poverty. It grounds the Christian tradition of caring for the poor in the warning that eternal consequences follow earthly choices.

verses.text: He cried and said, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue! For I am in anguish in this flame.’
verses.explainer: 'And he called out, Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the end of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.' Even in Hades, the rich man treats Lazarus as a servant -- 'send Lazarus to me.' His entitlement persists beyond death.

verses.text: “But Abraham said, ‘Son, remember that you, in your lifetime, received your good things, and Lazarus, in the same way, bad things. But here he is now comforted, and you are in anguish.
verses.explainer: 'But Abraham said, Child, remember that you in your lifetime received your good things, and Lazarus in like manner bad things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in anguish.' The reversal is complete. Luke's beatitudes (6:20-26) are enacted: the poor are comforted, the rich are in anguish.

verses.text: Besides all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed, that those who want to pass from here to you are not able, and that no one may cross over from there to us.’
verses.explainer: 'And besides all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, in order that those who would pass from here to you may not be able, and none may cross from there to us.' The chasm is permanent and impassable. The gate between the rich man's house and Lazarus has become an eternal divide.

verses.text: “He said, ‘I ask you therefore, father, that you would send him to my father’s house;
verses.explainer: 'And he said, Then I beg you, father, to send him to my father's house.' The rich man, unable to help himself, now wants Lazarus sent as a messenger to his brothers.

verses.text: for I have five brothers, that he may testify to them, so they won’t also come into this place of torment.’
verses.explainer: 'For I have five brothers, so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment.' Concern for his brothers -- perhaps the first sign of love for others. But it comes too late for himself.

verses.text: “But Abraham said to him, ‘They have Moses and the prophets. Let them listen to them.’
verses.explainer: 'But Abraham said, They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.' The Scriptures are sufficient warning. The rich man's brothers already have everything they need to know.

verses.text: “He said, ‘No, father Abraham, but if one goes to them from the dead, they will repent.’
verses.explainer: 'And he said, No, father Abraham, but if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent.' The rich man thinks a supernatural sign -- a resurrection appearance -- would convince them.

verses.text: “He said to him, ‘If they don’t listen to Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded if one rises from the dead.’”
verses.explainer: 'He said to him, If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone should rise from the dead.' The devastating conclusion: even resurrection will not convince those who refuse to hear Scripture. For Luke's audience, this is a clear reference to Jesus' own resurrection -- and the ongoing rejection of his message.

thenVsNow.then: Abraham's final statement is among the most sobering in the Gospels. It declares that the problem is not lack of evidence but refusal to listen. Those who ignore Moses and the Prophets will find ways to dismiss even the most spectacular proof.
thenVsNow.now: This verse speaks directly to the modern demand for 'more evidence' before committing to faith. Jesus suggests that the issue is rarely intellectual but moral -- the willingness to listen and respond to what is already known.

explainer.summary: Jesus teaches about the seriousness of causing others to stumble, the duty of forgiveness, and the nature of faith (even mustard-seed-sized faith can accomplish the impossible). The healing of ten lepers, of whom only one -- a Samaritan -- returns to give thanks, illustrates Luke's theme of outsiders responding to grace while insiders take it for granted. Jesus then teaches about the coming of the kingdom: it does not come with observable signs but is 'in your midst,' and the Son of Man's future coming will be sudden and unmistakable.
themes: The gravity of causing others to stumble
Forgiveness and faith
Gratitude as the mark of true healing (the Samaritan leper)
The kingdom already present among you
The sudden, unexpected coming of the Son of Man
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 17 continues the journey toward Jerusalem with a mix of ethical teaching, miracle, and eschatological instruction. The grateful Samaritan leper reinforces Luke's pattern: the outsider understands grace better than the insider.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest see
wordStudies.meaning: The mustard seed was proverbially the smallest seed known to Palestinian farmers. Jesus uses it to teach that the size of one's faith matters less than its genuineness. Even tiny, authentic faith can accomplish enormous things.

wordStudies.translatedAs: This phrase in 17:21 can be translated 'within you
wordStudies.meaning: This phrase in 17:21 can be translated 'within you' or 'in your midst/among you.' Most scholars prefer 'in your midst' -- Jesus is saying the kingdom is present in his person and ministry, not hidden inside individuals' hearts.

verses.text:
He said to the disciples,
“It is impossible that no occasions of stumbling should come, but woe to him through whom they come!
verses.explainer: Jesus says to his disciples: 'Temptations to sin are sure to come, but woe to the one through whom they come!' Sin's occasions are inevitable in a broken world, but those who cause others to stumble bear special guilt.

verses.text: It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck, and he were thrown into the sea, rather than that he should cause one of these little ones to stumble.
verses.explainer: 'It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were cast into the sea than that he should cause one of these little ones to sin.' The millstone image is terrifying: a stone so heavy it requires a donkey to turn it, tied around a person's neck and thrown into deep water. Death is preferable to leading others into sin.

verses.text: Be careful. If your brother sins against you, rebuke him. If he repents, forgive him.
verses.explainer: 'Pay attention to yourselves! If your brother sins, rebuke him, and if he repents, forgive him.' Forgiveness is not passive tolerance but an active cycle: sin is confronted, repentance is offered, and forgiveness is granted.

verses.text: If he sins against you seven times in the day, and seven times returns, saying, ‘I repent,’ you shall forgive him.”
verses.explainer: 'And if he sins against you seven times in the day, and turns to you seven times, saying, I repent, you must forgive him.' Seven times in one day -- the forgiveness demanded is extravagant and seemingly unreasonable. It matches the extravagant grace of the prodigal's father.

verses.text: The apostles said to the Lord, “Increase our faith.”
verses.explainer: The apostles say to the Lord: 'Increase our faith!' The demand for unlimited forgiveness seems beyond their capacity. They ask for more faith.

verses.text:
The Lord said,
“If you had faith like a grain of mustard seed, you would tell this sycamore tree, ‘Be uprooted, and be planted in the sea,’ and it would obey you.
verses.explainer: The Lord says: 'If you had faith like a grain of mustard seed, you could say to this mulberry tree, Be uprooted and planted in the sea, and it would obey you.' The answer is startling: you do not need more faith -- you need any faith at all. Even the tiniest genuine faith accomplishes the impossible.

thenVsNow.then: A mulberry tree had an extensive root system and was proverbially difficult to uproot. Moving it into the sea combines two impossibilities: uprooting what is deeply established and planting it in an inhospitable environment. Faith does what nature forbids.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus' teaching on mustard-seed faith has comforted millions who feel their faith is inadequate. The message is not about quantity but quality -- even small, genuine trust in God can accomplish what seems impossible.

verses.text: But who is there among you, having a servant plowing or keeping sheep, that will say, when he comes in from the field, ‘Come immediately and sit down at the table,’
verses.explainer: 'Will any one of you who has a servant plowing or keeping sheep say to him when he has come in from the field, Come at once and recline at table?' Jesus shifts to a parable about duty and entitlement.

verses.text: and will not rather tell him, ‘Prepare my supper, clothe yourself properly, and serve me, while I eat and drink. Afterward you shall eat and drink’?
verses.explainer: 'Will he not rather say to him, Prepare supper for me, and dress properly, and serve me while I eat and drink, and afterward you will eat and drink?' The servant serves the master before eating himself. This is the normal order.

verses.text: Does he thank that servant because he did the things that were commanded? I think not.
verses.explainer: 'Does he thank the servant because he did what was commanded?' A servant does not earn gratitude for doing his job. Obedience is the baseline, not the exception.

verses.text: Even so you also, when you have done all the things that are commanded you, say, ‘We are unworthy servants. We have done our duty.’”
verses.explainer: 'So you also, when you have done all that you were commanded, say, We are unworthy servants; we have only done what was our duty.' The disciples should view their obedience as obligation, not achievement. Entitlement to divine reward is eliminated.

verses.text: As he was on his way to Jerusalem, he was passing along the borders of Samaria and Galilee.
verses.explainer: On the way to Jerusalem he passes along between Samaria and Galilee. Luke reminds us of the journey's direction and the border territory between Jewish and Samaritan regions.

verses.text: As he entered into a certain village, ten men who were lepers met him, who stood at a distance.
verses.explainer: As he enters a village, he is met by ten lepers, who stand at a distance. The distance is required by law -- lepers could not approach healthy people. Ten lepers together suggests a community formed by shared suffering.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Lepers at a distance', 'explanation': "Leviticus 13:45-46 required lepers to live outside the camp, wear torn clothes, and cry 'Unclean! Unclean!' when anyone approached. They were socially dead -- cut off from family, worship, and community. Standing at a distance was both legal requirement and lived reality."}

verses.text: They lifted up their voices, saying, “Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!”
verses.explainer: They lift up their voices and say: 'Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!' A collective cry for mercy from the margins of society.

verses.text:
When he saw them, he said to them,
“Go and show yourselves to the priests.” As they went, they were cleansed.
verses.explainer: When he sees them, he says: 'Go and show yourselves to the priests.' And as they go they are cleansed. The healing happens during their obedient journey to the priests -- faith in action, before the result is visible.

verses.text: One of them, when he saw that he was healed, turned back, glorifying God with a loud voice.
verses.explainer: Then one of them, when he sees that he is healed, turns back, praising God with a loud voice. Only one out of ten returns. Gratitude is the exception, not the rule.

verses.text: He fell on his face at Jesus’ feet, giving him thanks; and he was a Samaritan.
verses.explainer: And he falls on his face at Jesus' feet, giving him thanks. Now he was a Samaritan. Luke saves the ethnic detail for maximum impact. The one who returns is the outsider -- the Samaritan whom the other nine (presumably Jewish) lepers would have shunned.

thenVsNow.then: The Samaritan's return is doubly significant: he was an ethnic and religious outsider who recognized what the Jewish lepers did not. In the shared community of suffering, the boundaries between Jew and Samaritan blurred; in the response to healing, the Samaritan alone demonstrated genuine faith.
thenVsNow.now: The story of the ten lepers has become a classic teaching on gratitude and worship. The question 'Where are the nine?' continues to challenge communities of faith about whether they take God's grace for granted.

verses.text:
Jesus answered,
“Weren’t the ten cleansed? But where are the nine?
verses.explainer: Then Jesus asks: 'Were not ten cleansed? Where are the nine?' The questions are rhetorical but pointed. Ten received healing; one returned with gratitude.

verses.text: Were there none found who returned to give glory to God, except this stranger?”
verses.explainer: 'Was no one found to return and give praise to God except this foreigner?' The word 'foreigner' (allogenes) emphasizes the Samaritan's outsider status. Again in Luke, the outsider shames the insider.

verses.text:
Then he said to him,
“Get up, and go your way. Your faith has healed you.”
verses.explainer: And he says to him: 'Rise and go your way; your faith has made you well.' All ten were physically healed, but only the Samaritan received the deeper salvation that comes through faith and gratitude. 'Made you well' can also be translated 'saved you.'

verses.text:
Being asked by the Pharisees when God’s Kingdom would come, he answered them,
“God’s Kingdom doesn’t come with observation;
verses.explainer: Being asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God would come, he answers them: 'The kingdom of God is not coming in ways that can be observed.' The kingdom does not arrive with the political and military fanfare the Pharisees expect.

verses.text: neither will they say, ‘Look, here!’ or, ‘Look, there!’ for behold, God’s Kingdom is within you.”
verses.explainer: 'Nor will they say, Look, here it is! or There! for behold, the kingdom of God is in the midst of you.' The kingdom is not a future event to be watched for but a present reality in Jesus' person and ministry.

thenVsNow.then: Most Jews expected the kingdom to come as a dramatic divine intervention -- routing the Romans, establishing Israel's sovereignty, restoring the Temple. Jesus says the kingdom is already present, operating quietly within the existing world order.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has shaped the Christian understanding of 'already but not yet' -- the kingdom is present in Jesus and his community but awaits full realization. Christians live between the kingdom's inauguration and its completion.

verses.text:
He said to the disciples,
“The days will come, when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it.
verses.explainer: And he says to the disciples: 'The days are coming when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see it.' After Jesus leaves, the disciples will long for his visible presence.

verses.text: They will tell you, ‘Look, here!’ or ‘Look, there!’ Don’t go away, nor follow after them,
verses.explainer: 'And they will say to you, Look, there! or Look, here! Do not go out or follow them.' False claims of the Messiah's return should be ignored.

verses.text: for as the lightning, when it flashes out of the one part under the sky, shines to the other part under the sky; so will the Son of Man be in his day.
verses.explainer: 'For as the lightning flashes and lights up the sky from one side to the other, so will the Son of Man be in his day.' When the Son of Man returns, it will be unmistakable -- as obvious as lightning across the entire sky.

verses.text: But first, he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.
verses.explainer: 'But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.' Before glory comes suffering. The passion prediction is woven into the eschatological teaching.

verses.text: As it was in the days of Noah, even so will it be also in the days of the Son of Man.
verses.explainer: 'Just as it was in the days of Noah, so will it be in the days of the Son of Man.' The comparison is not about wickedness but about normalcy -- people going about their daily business, oblivious to coming judgment.

verses.text: They ate, they drank, they married, they were given in marriage, until the day that Noah entered into the ship, and the flood came, and destroyed them all.
verses.explainer: 'They were eating and drinking and marrying and being given in marriage, until the day when Noah entered the ark, and the flood came and destroyed them all.' Ordinary life continued until sudden catastrophe. The warning is against complacency, not specific sins.

verses.text: Likewise, even as it was in the days of Lot: they ate, they drank, they bought, they sold, they planted, they built;
verses.explainer: 'Likewise, just as it was in the days of Lot -- they were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building.' Again, normal commercial and domestic activities.

verses.text: but in the day that Lot went out from Sodom, it rained fire and sulfur from the sky, and destroyed them all.
verses.explainer: 'But on the day when Lot went out from Sodom, fire and sulfur rained from heaven and destroyed them all.' Destruction came without warning on a normal day.

verses.text: It will be the same way in the day that the Son of Man is revealed.
verses.explainer: 'So will it be on the day when the Son of Man is revealed.' The Son of Man's coming will interrupt ordinary life with the same suddenness as the flood and the destruction of Sodom.

verses.text: In that day, he who will be on the housetop, and his goods in the house, let him not go down to take them away. Let him who is in the field likewise not turn back.
verses.explainer: 'On that day, let the one who is on the housetop, with his goods in the house, not come down to take them away, and likewise let the one who is in the field not turn back.' When the moment arrives, there is no time for retrieval. Attachment to possessions becomes fatal.

verses.text: Remember Lot’s wife!
verses.explainer: 'Remember Lot's wife.' The shortest verse Jesus speaks (three words in Greek). Lot's wife looked back at what she was leaving and became a pillar of salt. The warning against clinging to the old life is compressed into three devastating words.

verses.text: Whoever seeks to save his life loses it, but whoever loses his life preserves it.
verses.explainer: 'Whoever seeks to preserve his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life will keep it.' The paradox from 9:24 returns in an eschatological context: clinging to security in the end times is self-defeating.

verses.text: I tell you, in that night there will be two people in one bed. The one will be taken, and the other will be left.
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, in that night there will be two in one bed. One will be taken and the other left.' The separation is intimate and sudden -- even people sharing a bed will be divided.

verses.text: There will be two grinding grain together. One will be taken, and the other will be left.”
verses.explainer: 'There will be two women grinding together. One will be taken and the other left.' The same division in the workplace. The coming judgment divides people in every context.
Some manuscripts include: 'Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left.' This verse is absent from the earliest manuscripts of Luke but present in Matthew 24:40.

verses.text:
They, answering, asked him, “Where, Lord?”
He said to them,
“Where the body is, there will the vultures also be gathered together.”
verses.explainer: And they say to him: 'Where, Lord?' He says to them: 'Where the corpse is, there the vultures will gather.' A cryptic answer: the location of judgment will be self-evident, like vultures circling a carcass. The signs will be unmistakable.

explainer.summary: Two contrasting parables open the chapter: the persistent widow who wears down an unjust judge teaches about unceasing prayer, while the Pharisee and tax collector in the Temple teaches about genuine humility before God. Jesus blesses children, confronts a rich ruler whose wealth prevents him from following, and delivers his third and most detailed passion prediction. A blind man near Jericho receives his sight through persistent faith. Each episode explores what it means to come to God with nothing but need.
themes: Persistence in prayer
Humility versus self-righteousness (the Pharisee and tax collector)
Children as models of receiving the kingdom
The danger of wealth and the impossibility of self-salvation
The third passion prediction
Faith that cries out despite opposition
explainer.contextInBook: As Jesus nears Jerusalem, chapter 18 intensifies the question of who can enter the kingdom. The answer is consistently those who come with nothing: the widow, the tax collector, the children, the blind beggar. The rich ruler, who has everything, cannot enter.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To be declared righteous, to be put right with God
wordStudies.meaning: To be declared righteous, to be put right with God. In 18:14, the tax collector goes home 'justified' while the Pharisee does not. Justification is not earned by moral achievement but received through humble confession. This verse is central to the Reformation debate about justification by faith.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Things that are impossible with humans are possibl
wordStudies.meaning: Things that are impossible with humans are possible with God (18:27). The word means literally 'without power' -- beyond human capability. Only divine power can accomplish salvation.

verses.text: He also spoke a parable to them that they must always pray, and not give up,
verses.explainer: And he tells them a parable to the effect that they ought always to pray and not lose heart. The parable's purpose is stated upfront: prayer must be persistent, not occasional.

verses.text:
saying,
“There was a judge in a certain city who didn’t fear God, and didn’t respect man.
verses.explainer: 'In a certain city there was a judge who neither feared God nor respected man.' The judge is corrupt on every level -- indifferent to both divine and human standards.

verses.text: A widow was in that city, and she often came to him, saying, ‘Defend me from my adversary!’
verses.explainer: 'And there was a widow in that city who kept coming to him and saying, Give me justice against my adversary.' The widow has no advocate, no money for bribes, no social leverage. Her only weapon is persistence.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Widow seeking justice', 'explanation': "Widows were among the most vulnerable people in ancient society. Without a male advocate, they had no effective legal standing. A corrupt judge could ignore their cases indefinitely. This widow's persistence is an act of desperation by someone with no other options."}

verses.text: He wouldn’t for a while, but afterward he said to himself, ‘Though I neither fear God, nor respect man,
verses.explainer: 'For a while he refused, but afterward he said to himself, Though I neither fear God nor respect man...' The judge is honest about his own corruption. He has no noble motivation.

verses.text: yet because this widow bothers me, I will defend her, or else she will wear me out by her continual coming.’”
verses.explainer: '...yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will give her justice, so that she will not beat me down by her continual coming.' The Greek phrase literally means 'so she will not give me a black eye.' Her relentless persistence wears him down.

thenVsNow.then: The parable works from lesser to greater: if a corrupt judge yields to persistence, how much more will a just God respond to his people's persistent prayer? God is not like the judge, but the widow's prayer strategy is a model.
thenVsNow.now: The parable of the persistent widow has encouraged believers in every era not to give up on prayer. It addresses the common experience of prayers that seem unanswered by teaching that persistence itself is part of the prayer journey.

verses.text:
The Lord said,
“Listen to what the unrighteous judge says.
verses.explainer: And the Lord says: 'Hear what the unrighteous judge says.' Jesus draws attention to the judge's reasoning.

verses.text: Won’t God avenge his chosen ones, who are crying out to him day and night, and yet he exercises patience with them?
verses.explainer: 'And will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night? Will he delay long over them?' If an unjust judge responds to persistence, certainly a just God will vindicate those who pray continuously.

verses.text: I tell you that he will avenge them quickly. Nevertheless, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?”
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, he will give justice to them speedily. Nevertheless, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?' The promise of speedy vindication is followed by a sobering question: will anyone still be praying when the answer comes?

verses.text: He spoke also this parable to certain people who were convinced of their own righteousness, and who despised all others.
verses.explainer: He tells this parable to some who trusted in themselves that they were righteous, and treated others with contempt. The target audience is identified: the self-righteous who despise others.

verses.text: “Two men went up into the temple to pray; one was a Pharisee, and the other was a tax collector.
verses.explainer: 'Two men went up into the Temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.' The Temple is the setting. The two figures represent the opposite ends of the religious spectrum.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Praying in the Temple', 'explanation': 'Jews went to the Temple for prayer, especially during the morning and evening sacrifice (approximately 9 AM and 3 PM). The Temple courts were open to all Jewish males; the Pharisee and tax collector would have been in the Court of Israel, standing at a distance from the altar.'}

verses.text: The Pharisee stood and prayed to himself like this: ‘God, I thank you, that I am not like the rest of men, extortionists, unrighteous, adulterers, or even like this tax collector.
verses.explainer: 'The Pharisee, standing by himself, prayed thus: God, I thank you that I am not like other men, extortioners, unjust, adulterers, or even like this tax collector.' His prayer is a self-congratulatory monologue. He thanks God for his own superiority rather than for God's grace.

thenVsNow.then: The Pharisee's prayer would have sounded admirable to most of Jesus' audience. He genuinely was more observant than most people. His fasting and tithing exceeded requirements. The shock is not that his resume is false but that his attitude makes it worthless.
thenVsNow.now: The Pharisee's prayer has become the archetype of religious self-righteousness. It warns against any prayer that is actually a boast, any gratitude that is really comparison, any religion that is ultimately about one's own moral achievement.

verses.text: I fast twice a week. I give tithes of all that I get.’
verses.explainer: 'I fast twice a week; I give tithes of all that I get.' His religious practice exceeds requirements: the Law required fasting only on the Day of Atonement, and tithing only on agricultural produce. He fasts twice weekly and tithes everything.

verses.text: But the tax collector, standing far away, wouldn’t even lift up his eyes to heaven, but beat his breast, saying, ‘God, be merciful to me, a sinner!’
verses.explainer: 'But the tax collector, standing far off, would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, but beat his breast, saying, God, be merciful to me, a sinner!' Everything about him expresses unworthiness: he stands far off, looks down, beats his breast (a gesture of mourning and distress), and asks only for mercy.

verses.text: I tell you, this man went down to his house justified rather than the other; for everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but he who humbles himself will be exalted.”
verses.explainer: 'I tell you, this man went down to his house justified, rather than the other. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.' The tax collector -- the sinner -- goes home right with God. The Pharisee -- the righteous man -- does not. This is one of the most radical reversals in all of Jesus' teaching.

thenVsNow.then: The declaration that a tax collector was justified while a Pharisee was not would have been deeply shocking. It overturned every assumption about who was righteous before God. Justification came through humble confession, not moral achievement.
thenVsNow.now: This parable was central to Martin Luther's theology of justification by grace through faith. It demonstrates that right standing before God is received through humble dependence, not earned through religious performance. It remains foundational to Protestant soteriology.

verses.text: They were also bringing their babies to him, that he might touch them. But when the disciples saw it, they rebuked them.
verses.explainer: Now they are bringing even infants to him that he might touch them. And when the disciples see it, they rebuke them. The disciples see children as unworthy of Jesus' attention.

verses.text:
Jesus summoned them, saying,
“Allow the little children to come to me, and don’t hinder them, for God’s Kingdom belongs to such as these.
verses.explainer: But Jesus calls them to him, saying: 'Let the children come to me, and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of God.' Children represent not innocence but dependence -- they have nothing to offer, only need.

verses.text: Most certainly, I tell you, whoever doesn’t receive God’s Kingdom like a little child, he will in no way enter into it.”
verses.explainer: 'Truly, I say to you, whoever does not receive the kingdom of God like a child shall not enter it.' The kingdom is received, not achieved. A child's posture -- open hands, no credentials, complete dependence -- is the only posture that works.

verses.text: A certain ruler asked him, saying, “Good Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?”
verses.explainer: And a ruler asks him: 'Good Teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?' The question echoes 10:25 (the lawyer and the Good Samaritan). This time the questioner is wealthy and powerful.

verses.text:
Jesus asked him,
“Why do you call me good? No one is good, except one—God.
verses.explainer: And Jesus says: 'Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone.' Jesus does not reject the title but probes the man's understanding. If only God is truly good, calling Jesus 'good' implies something about who Jesus is.

verses.text: You know the commandments: ‘Don’t commit adultery,’ ‘Don’t murder,’ ‘Don’t steal,’ ‘Don’t give false testimony,’ ‘Honor your father and your mother.’”
verses.explainer: 'You know the commandments: Do not commit adultery, Do not murder, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Honor your father and mother.' Jesus lists five of the Ten Commandments -- all from the second tablet, dealing with human relationships.

verses.text: He said, “I have observed all these things from my youth up.”
verses.explainer: And he says: 'All these I have kept from my youth.' His claim is sincere. He is genuinely observant, not hypocritical.

verses.text:
When Jesus heard these things, he said to him,
“You still lack one thing. Sell all that you have, and distribute it to the poor. You will have treasure in heaven. Come, follow me.”
verses.explainer: When Jesus hears this, he says: 'One thing you still lack. Sell all that you have and distribute to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me.' The one thing -- the command that addresses this specific man's idol. His wealth is not merely a blessing but the barrier between him and God.

verses.text: But when he heard these things, he became very sad, for he was very rich.
verses.explainer: But when he hears these things, he becomes very sad, for he is extremely rich. The sadness is genuine -- he recognizes what is being asked and cannot do it. His wealth owns him more than he owns it.

verses.text:
Jesus, seeing that he became very sad, said,
“How hard it is for those who have riches to enter into God’s Kingdom!
verses.explainer: Jesus, seeing that he has become sad, says: 'How difficult it is for those who have wealth to enter the kingdom of God!' The observation is sympathetic, not condemning. Wealth creates difficulty because it provides an alternative to God.

verses.text: For it is easier for a camel to enter in through a needle’s eye, than for a rich man to enter into God’s Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: 'For it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.' The image is deliberately absurd: the largest common animal passing through the smallest opening. It is physically impossible.

thenVsNow.then: Some have tried to soften this by suggesting a small gate called 'the eye of the needle' or that 'camel' should be 'cable.' But Jesus intends the absurdity: human wealth makes entrance to the kingdom humanly impossible. Only God can accomplish it.
thenVsNow.now: This verse remains one of the most uncomfortable in the Gospels for affluent believers. It does not say wealthy people will not enter the kingdom, but that they cannot do so on their own. Only the divine intervention described in verse 27 makes it possible.

verses.text: Those who heard it said, “Then who can be saved?”
verses.explainer: Those who hear it say: 'Then who can be saved?' If the wealthy (considered blessed by God) cannot be saved, then salvation seems impossible for anyone.

verses.text:
But he said,
“The things which are impossible with men are possible with God.”
verses.explainer: But he says: 'What is impossible with man is possible with God.' Salvation is not a human achievement but a divine gift. The impossibility for humans is the possibility for God.

verses.text: Peter said, “Look, we have left everything, and followed you.”
verses.explainer: Peter says: 'See, we have left our homes and followed you.' Peter notes that the disciples have done what the ruler could not.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Most certainly I tell you, there is no one who has left house, or wife, or brothers, or parents, or children, for God’s Kingdom’s sake,
verses.explainer: And Jesus says: 'Truly, I say to you, there is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children, for the sake of the kingdom of God...'

verses.text: who will not receive many times more in this time, and in the world to come, eternal life.”
verses.explainer: '...who will not receive many times more in this time, and in the age to come eternal life.' The return on kingdom investment is both present and eternal. Those who sacrifice for the kingdom receive abundant compensation.

verses.text:
He took the twelve aside, and said to them,
“Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and all the things that are written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man will be completed.
verses.explainer: Taking the twelve aside, he says: 'See, we are going up to Jerusalem, and everything that is written about the Son of Man by the prophets will be accomplished.' The third and most detailed passion prediction. Jesus is clear-eyed about what awaits.

verses.text: For he will be delivered up to the Gentiles, will be mocked, treated shamefully, and spit on.
verses.explainer: 'For he will be delivered over to the Gentiles and will be mocked and shamefully treated and spit upon.' The specific details anticipate the Roman trial and abuse.

verses.text: They will scourge and kill him. On the third day, he will rise again.”
verses.explainer: 'And after flogging him, they will kill him, and on the third day he will rise.' The prediction includes both death and resurrection. Jesus does not share the suffering without also promising the vindication.

verses.text: They understood none of these things. This saying was hidden from them, and they didn’t understand the things that were said.
verses.explainer: But they understood none of these things. This saying was hidden from them, and they did not grasp what was said. Three times now Jesus has predicted his death. Three times the disciples fail to comprehend.

verses.text: As he came near Jericho, a certain blind man sat by the road, begging.
verses.explainer: As he draws near to Jericho, a blind man is sitting by the roadside begging. Jericho is the last major stop before Jerusalem. The journey is almost complete.

verses.text: Hearing a multitude going by, he asked what this meant.
verses.explainer: Hearing a crowd going by, he inquires what this means.

verses.text: They told him that Jesus of Nazareth was passing by.
verses.explainer: They tell him: 'Jesus of Nazareth is passing by.' The blind man hears the name that will change his life.

verses.text: He cried out, “Jesus, you son of David, have mercy on me!”
verses.explainer: And he cries out: 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' 'Son of David' is a messianic title. The blind man sees what the sighted cannot -- he recognizes Jesus as the promised Messiah.

verses.text: Those who led the way rebuked him, that he should be quiet; but he cried out all the more, “You son of David, have mercy on me!”
verses.explainer: Those in front rebuke him, telling him to be silent. But he cries out all the more: 'Son of David, have mercy on me!' The crowd tries to silence him, but he demonstrates the same persistence the persistent widow modeled. He will not be quiet.

verses.text: Standing still, Jesus commanded him to be brought to him. When he had come near, he asked him,
verses.explainer: Jesus stops and commands him to be brought to him. And when he comes near, he asks him: 'What do you want me to do for you?' Jesus asks the obvious question to draw out the man's faith.

verses.text:
“What do you want me to do?”
He said, “Lord, that I may see again.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Lord, let me recover my sight.' A clear, specific, faith-filled request.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Receive your sight. Your faith has healed you.”
