verses.explainer: And Jesus says: 'Recover your sight; your faith has made you well.' The blind man receives what the rich ruler could not: salvation through faith. He came with nothing but need and left with everything.

verses.text: Immediately he received his sight, and followed him, glorifying God. All the people, when they saw it, praised God.
verses.explainer: And immediately he recovered his sight and followed Jesus, glorifying God. And all the people, when they saw it, gave praise to God. The healed man becomes a disciple. The chapter that began with prayer ends with praise.

explainer.summary: Jesus enters Jericho and transforms the life of Zacchaeus, a chief tax collector who responds to Jesus' acceptance with radical generosity. The parable of the ten minas teaches about faithful stewardship while the master is away. Jesus then enters Jerusalem in the Triumphal Entry, weeps over the city's coming destruction, and cleanses the Temple of merchants. The long journey from Galilee is complete -- Jesus has arrived at his destination, and the final confrontation begins.
themes: Salvation coming to the lost (Zacchaeus)
Faithful stewardship and accountability (parable of minas)
Jesus as king entering his capital
Weeping over Jerusalem's judgment
The Temple as a house of prayer, not commerce
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 19 brings the travel narrative (9:51-19:44) to its climax. Jesus has 'set his face' toward Jerusalem; now he arrives. The Triumphal Entry signals his messianic claim; the Temple cleansing signals his prophetic authority. From this point, the conflict escalates toward the cross.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Deliverance, rescue, wholeness
wordStudies.meaning: Deliverance, rescue, wholeness. When Jesus says 'salvation has come to this house' (19:9), he means that Zacchaeus and his household have been rescued from the power of greed and restored to the covenant community. Salvation in Luke is holistic -- spiritual, social, and economic.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A unit of currency equal to about 100 denarii (rou
wordStudies.meaning: A unit of currency equal to about 100 denarii (roughly 3-4 months' wages for a laborer). Unlike Matthew's parable of talents where each servant receives different amounts, in Luke each servant receives one mina -- the same opportunity, tested differently.

verses.text: He entered and was passing through Jericho.
verses.explainer: He enters Jericho and is passing through. Jericho is the final stop before the steep ascent to Jerusalem.

verses.text: There was a man named Zacchaeus. He was a chief tax collector, and he was rich.
verses.explainer: And behold, there is a man named Zacchaeus. He is a chief tax collector and is rich. Luke introduces him with two descriptions that made him the most despised man in Jericho: chief tax collector (supervisor of the entire customs operation) and rich (enriched through the system of exploitation).

verses.text: He was trying to see who Jesus was, and couldn’t because of the crowd, because he was short.
verses.explainer: And he is seeking to see who Jesus is, but on account of the crowd he cannot, because he is small in stature. Zacchaeus' physical shortness prevents him from seeing over the crowd -- and the crowd would not make way for a tax collector.

verses.text: He ran on ahead, and climbed up into a sycamore tree to see him, for he was going to pass that way.
verses.explainer: So he runs ahead and climbs up into a sycamore tree to see Jesus, for he was about to pass that way. A wealthy official climbing a tree is an absurd, undignified act. Zacchaeus' desire to see Jesus overrides his concern for appearances.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Sycamore tree', 'explanation': 'The sycamore-fig tree (ficus sycomorus) is a large, spreading tree with low branches, easy to climb. It was common along roads in the Jordan Valley. It was not a prestigious tree -- its fruit was considered food for the poor.'}

verses.text:
When Jesus came to the place, he looked up and saw him, and said to him,
“Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for today I must stay at your house.”
verses.explainer: And when Jesus came to the place, he looked up and said to him: 'Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for I must stay at your house today.' Jesus knows his name. He does not ask permission -- he invites himself. The word 'must' (dei) indicates divine necessity.

thenVsNow.then: For a Jewish teacher to invite himself to a tax collector's home was scandalous. Entering the house of someone considered ritually and morally unclean made the visitor unclean as well. Jesus deliberately chooses the most disreputable house in Jericho.
thenVsNow.now: The Zacchaeus story is one of the most beloved in Christian teaching about grace. Jesus does not wait for Zacchaeus to repent before coming to his house. He comes first, and the repentance follows. Grace precedes transformation.

verses.text: He hurried, came down, and received him joyfully.
verses.explainer: So he hurries and comes down and receives him joyfully. Zacchaeus' response is immediate, eager, and joyful. The most hated man in town is thrilled to host Jesus.

verses.text: When they saw it, they all murmured, saying, “He has gone in to lodge with a man who is a sinner.”
verses.explainer: And when they see it, they all grumble: 'He has gone in to be the guest of a man who is a sinner.' The crowd's reaction mirrors the Pharisees' complaint in 15:2. The pattern repeats: Jesus welcomes sinners, the righteous grumble.

verses.text: Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord, “Behold, Lord, half of my goods I give to the poor. If I have wrongfully exacted anything of anyone, I restore four times as much.”
verses.explainer: And Zacchaeus stands and says to the Lord: 'Behold, Lord, the half of my goods I give to the poor. And if I have defrauded anyone of anything, I restore it fourfold.' Zacchaeus' repentance is concrete and extravagant. Half his wealth to the poor, plus fourfold restitution to those he cheated. This exceeds what the Torah required (Numbers 5:7 required the principal plus one-fifth).

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Today, salvation has come to this house, because he also is a son of Abraham.
verses.explainer: And Jesus says to him: 'Today salvation has come to this house, since he also is a son of Abraham.' 'Today' -- Luke's characteristic word of present salvation. Zacchaeus, despised as a traitor, is restored to the covenant community as a 'son of Abraham.'

verses.text: For the Son of Man came to seek and to save that which was lost.”
verses.explainer: 'For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.' The mission statement that summarizes not just this episode but the entire Gospel. Jesus is the searching shepherd (15:4), the sweeping woman (15:8), the waiting father (15:20), and the guest of sinners (19:5).

thenVsNow.then: This verse summarizes Jesus' ministry as active pursuit, not passive availability. He seeks the lost before they seek him. The initiative belongs to God.
thenVsNow.now: Luke 19:10 is one of the most quoted verses in evangelistic preaching. It defines the church's mission as identical to Jesus' own: seeking and saving those who are lost, not waiting for them to arrive.

verses.text: As they heard these things, he went on and told a parable, because he was near Jerusalem, and they supposed that God’s Kingdom would be revealed immediately.
verses.explainer: As they hear these things, he proceeds to tell a parable, because he is near to Jerusalem and because they suppose the kingdom of God is about to appear immediately. The crowd expects the kingdom to erupt the moment Jesus enters Jerusalem. The parable corrects this expectation.

verses.text:
He said therefore,
“A certain nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom, and to return.
verses.explainer: 'A nobleman went into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom and then return.' The parable echoes the historical experience of Herod Archelaus, who traveled to Rome to receive his kingdom from Augustus.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Receiving a kingdom from a far country', 'explanation': "When Herod the Great died in 4 BC, his son Archelaus traveled to Rome to have Augustus confirm his rule over Judea. A Jewish delegation followed to protest. Augustus eventually granted Archelaus the territory but with the lesser title of ethnarch. Jesus' audience would have immediately recognized this scenario."}

verses.text:
He called ten servants of his, and gave them ten mina coins,

and told them, ‘Conduct business until I come.’
verses.explainer: 'Calling ten of his servants, he gave them ten minas and said to them, Engage in business until I come.' Each servant receives the same amount -- one mina. The test is what they do with equal opportunity.

verses.text: But his citizens hated him, and sent an envoy after him, saying, ‘We don’t want this man to reign over us.’
verses.explainer: 'But his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him, saying, We do not want this man to reign over us.' This detail, absent from Matthew's similar parable, reflects the Jewish delegation that opposed Archelaus -- and foreshadows Jerusalem's rejection of Jesus.

verses.text: “When he had come back again, having received the kingdom, he commanded these servants, to whom he had given the money, to be called to him, that he might know what they had gained by conducting business.
verses.explainer: 'When he returned, having received the kingdom, he ordered these servants to whom he had given the money to be called to him, that he might know what they had gained by doing business.' Accountability follows absence. The master returns and demands an accounting.

verses.text: The first came before him, saying, ‘Lord, your mina has made ten more minas.’
verses.explainer: 'The first came before him, saying, Lord, your mina has made ten minas more.' A tenfold return -- extraordinary success.

verses.text: “He said to him, ‘Well done, you good servant! Because you were found faithful with very little, you shall have authority over ten cities.’
verses.explainer: 'And he said to him, Well done, good servant! Because you have been faithful in a very little, you shall have authority over ten cities.' The reward for faithful stewardship is greater responsibility. One mina managed well earns governance of ten cities.

verses.text: “The second came, saying, ‘Your mina, Lord, has made five minas.’
verses.explainer: 'And the second came, saying, Lord, your mina has made five minas.' A fivefold return -- also excellent.

verses.text: “So he said to him, ‘And you are to be over five cities.’
verses.explainer: 'And he said to him, And you are to be over five cities.' Proportional reward: the return matches the responsibility given.

verses.text: Another came, saying, ‘Lord, behold, your mina, which I kept laid away in a handkerchief,
verses.explainer: 'Then another came, saying, Lord, here is your mina, which I kept laid away in a handkerchief.' The third servant has done nothing with the opportunity. He preserved the capital but generated no return.

verses.text: for I feared you, because you are an exacting man. You take up that which you didn’t lay down, and reap that which you didn’t sow.’
verses.explainer: 'For I was afraid of you, because you are a severe man. You take what you did not deposit, and reap what you did not sow.' Fear produced paralysis, not faithfulness. His view of the master is distorted.

verses.text: “He said to him, ‘Out of your own mouth will I judge you, you wicked servant! You knew that I am an exacting man, taking up that which I didn’t lay down, and reaping that which I didn’t sow.
verses.explainer: 'He said to him, I will condemn you with your own words, you wicked servant! You knew that I was a severe man, taking what I did not deposit and reaping what I did not sow?' The master uses the servant's own logic against him.

verses.text: Then why didn’t you deposit my money in the bank, and at my coming, I might have earned interest on it?’
verses.explainer: 'Why then did you not put my money in the bank, and at my coming I might have collected it with interest?' Even the minimal effort of banking would have produced something. The servant's failure is not inability but unwillingness.

verses.text: He said to those who stood by, ‘Take the mina away from him, and give it to him who has the ten minas.’
verses.explainer: 'And he said to those who stood by, Take the mina from him, and give it to the one who has the ten minas.' Resources flow to the faithful. The unfaithful lose even what they were given.

verses.text: “They said to him, ‘Lord, he has ten minas!’
verses.explainer: And they said to him: 'Lord, he has ten minas!' The bystanders protest the seeming unfairness.

verses.text: ‘For I tell you that to everyone who has, will more be given; but from him who doesn’t have, even that which he has will be taken away from him.
verses.explainer: 'I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given, but from the one who has not, even what he has will be taken away.' The principle of stewardship: use it or lose it. Faithful management attracts more; unfaithful management loses all.

verses.text: But bring those enemies of mine who didn’t want me to reign over them here, and kill them before me.’”
verses.explainer: 'But as for these enemies of mine, who did not want me to reign over them, bring them here and slaughter them before me.' The judgment on the rebellious citizens is severe. This is not a model for human behavior but a picture of the consequences of rejecting God's appointed king.

verses.text: Having said these things, he went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: And when he had said these things, he went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem. The final ascent begins. Jericho sits at about 850 feet below sea level; Jerusalem at about 2,500 feet above. It is a steep, 17-mile climb.

verses.text: When he came near to Bethsphage and Bethany, at the mountain that is called Olivet, he sent two of his disciples,
verses.explainer: When he draws near to Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount called Olivet, he sends two of the disciples. The Mount of Olives is the last ridge before Jerusalem, with a panoramic view of the Temple.

verses.text:
saying,
“Go your way into the village on the other side, in which, as you enter, you will find a colt tied, which no man had ever sat upon. Untie it, and bring it.
verses.explainer: 'Go into the village in front of you, where on entering you will find a colt tied, on which no one has ever yet sat. Untie it and bring it here.' The colt has never been ridden -- it is consecrated for this specific purpose.

verses.text: If anyone asks you, ‘Why are you untying it?’ say to him: ‘The Lord needs it.’”
verses.explainer: 'If anyone asks you, Why are you untying it? you shall say this: The Lord has need of it.' The explanation assumes the owner will understand and comply.

verses.text: Those who were sent went away, and found things just as he had told them.
verses.explainer: So those who are sent go and find it just as he told them. The details match perfectly, suggesting either prior arrangement or prophetic knowledge.

verses.text: As they were untying the colt, its owners said to them, “Why are you untying the colt?”
verses.explainer: And as they are untying the colt, its owners say to them: 'Why are you untying the colt?' The question comes as predicted.

verses.text: They said, “The Lord needs it.”
verses.explainer: And they say: 'The Lord has need of it.' The answer suffices. The owners release the animal.

verses.text: They brought it to Jesus. They threw their cloaks on the colt, and set Jesus on them.
verses.explainer: And they bring it to Jesus, and throwing their cloaks on the colt, they set Jesus upon it. Cloaks on the colt create an improvised royal saddle.

verses.text: As he went, they spread their cloaks on the road.
verses.explainer: As he rides along, they spread their cloaks on the road. The crowd creates a carpet of garments -- a gesture of royal honor.

verses.text: As he was now getting near, at the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of the disciples began to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works which they had seen,
verses.explainer: As he is drawing near -- already on the way down the Mount of Olives -- the whole multitude of his disciples begins to rejoice and praise God with a loud voice for all the mighty works that they have seen.

verses.text:
saying, “Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord!
 Peace in heaven, and glory in the highest!”
verses.explainer: 'Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord! Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!' Luke's version adds 'the King' to Psalm 118:26, making the messianic claim explicit. 'Peace in heaven' echoes the angelic chorus at Jesus' birth (2:14).

thenVsNow.then: The crowd's acclamation was politically explosive. Calling someone 'King' in the presence of Roman soldiers was an act of insurrection. The joy was real, but the political implications were dangerous.
thenVsNow.now: Palm Sunday commemorates this event in Christian liturgy. The juxtaposition of triumph and impending suffering -- the same crowd that shouts 'Blessed is the King' will soon cry 'Crucify him' -- makes it one of the most emotionally complex moments in the Gospel.

verses.text: Some of the Pharisees from the multitude said to him, “Teacher, rebuke your disciples!”
verses.explainer: And some of the Pharisees in the crowd say to him: 'Teacher, rebuke your disciples.' They want Jesus to silence the messianic acclamations.

verses.text:
He answered them,
“I tell you that if these were silent, the stones would cry out.”
verses.explainer: He answers: 'I tell you, if these were silent, the very stones would cry out.' The truth about Jesus cannot be suppressed. If humans do not declare it, creation itself will.

verses.text: When he came near, he saw the city and wept over it,
verses.explainer: And when he draws near and sees the city, he weeps over it. Jesus does not enter Jerusalem in triumph but in tears. He weeps for the city that will reject him and suffer the consequences.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus weeps because he knows what is coming: the Roman siege of 70 AD that will destroy the Temple and kill hundreds of thousands. His grief is prophetic -- he sees the future that Jerusalem's leaders are blindly creating.
thenVsNow.now: The image of Jesus weeping over Jerusalem has been central to Christian understanding of divine grief. God does not take pleasure in judgment but mourns the consequences of human rejection.

verses.text:
saying,
“If you, even you, had known today the things which belong to your peace! But now, they are hidden from your eyes.
verses.explainer: 'Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes.' The 'things that make for peace' -- recognizing Jesus and his message. The opportunity is being missed.

verses.text: For the days will come on you, when your enemies will throw up a barricade against you, surround you, hem you in on every side,
verses.explainer: 'For the days will come upon you, when your enemies will set up a barricade around you and surround you and hem you in on every side.' This describes the Roman siege of Jerusalem with precise detail.

verses.text: and will dash you and your children within you to the ground. They will not leave in you one stone on another, because you didn’t know the time of your visitation.”
verses.explainer: 'And they will tear you down to the ground, you and your children within you. And they will not leave one stone upon another in you, because you did not know the time of your visitation.' The destruction will be total. The reason: they failed to recognize that God had visited them in Jesus.

verses.text: He entered into the temple, and began to drive out those who bought and sold in it,
verses.explainer: And he enters the Temple and begins to drive out those who sell. The Temple cleansing is Jesus' first act in Jerusalem -- a prophetic action claiming authority over the sacred space.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Temple commerce', 'explanation': "The outer court of the Temple housed vendors who sold sacrificial animals and money changers who converted Roman coins (bearing the emperor's image) into Tyrian shekels acceptable for the Temple tax. The system was necessary but had become exploitative -- controlled by the high priestly family who profited from inflated prices."}

verses.text:
saying to them,
“It is written, ‘My house is a house of prayer,’

but you have made it a ‘den of robbers’!”
verses.explainer: 'Saying to them, It is written, My house shall be a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers.' Jesus combines Isaiah 56:7 and Jeremiah 7:11. The Temple should be a place of prayer for all nations but has become a place of commercial exploitation.

verses.text: He was teaching daily in the temple, but the chief priests and the scribes and the leading men among the people sought to destroy him.
verses.explainer: And he is teaching daily in the Temple. The chief priests and the scribes and the principal men of the people are seeking to destroy him. The battle lines are drawn: Jesus teaches publicly; the authorities plot secretly.

verses.text: They couldn’t find what they might do, for all the people hung on to every word that he said.
verses.explainer: But they do not find anything they can do, for all the people are hanging on his words. Jesus' popularity with the crowd is the only thing preventing his immediate arrest.

explainer.summary: The religious authorities challenge Jesus' authority, and he responds with a counter-question about John's baptism that silences them. The parable of the wicked tenants is a thinly veiled allegory of Israel's leaders rejecting God's messengers and finally his Son. Attempts to trap Jesus on taxes to Caesar and the resurrection are both brilliantly defeated. The chapter shows Jesus in complete intellectual control even as the political noose tightens.
themes: The source of Jesus' authority
Israel's rejection of God's messengers (wicked tenants)
Rendering to Caesar and to God
The reality of resurrection
Jesus' identity as David's Lord
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 20 presents a series of confrontations in the Temple -- the last public debates before the passion. Each exchange demonstrates Jesus' superior wisdom while deepening the opposition's determination to kill him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Power, right, or authorization to act
wordStudies.meaning: Power, right, or authorization to act. The question 'by what authority?' challenges the legitimacy of Jesus' Temple actions. All authority in the Jewish system derived from recognized institutions -- the priesthood, the Sanhedrin, or scribal ordination. Jesus claims an authority from none of these but from God directly.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The stone that determines the alignment of the ent
wordStudies.meaning: The stone that determines the alignment of the entire building. Jesus identifies himself as the stone the builders rejected that becomes the cornerstone. Those who stumble over it are broken; those it falls on are crushed.

verses.text:
On one of those days, as he was teaching the people in the temple and preaching the Good News, the
priests and scribes came to him with the elders.
verses.explainer: One day, as Jesus is teaching the people in the Temple and preaching the gospel, the chief priests and the scribes with the elders come up to him. A delegation of the three groups that compose the Sanhedrin -- the ruling council.

verses.text: They asked him, “Tell us: by what authority do you do these things? Or who is giving you this authority?”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Tell us by what authority you do these things, or who it is that gave you this authority.' The question targets the Temple cleansing: who authorized you to disrupt Temple operations?

verses.text:
He answered them,
“I also will ask you one question. Tell me:
verses.explainer: He answers: 'I also will ask you a question. Now tell me...' Jesus responds with a counter-question, a standard rabbinic technique.

verses.text: the baptism of John, was it from heaven, or from men?”
verses.explainer: 'Was the baptism of John from heaven or from man?' The trap is elegant: if they say 'from heaven,' Jesus will ask why they did not believe John. If they say 'from man,' the crowds who revere John as a prophet will turn against them.

verses.text: They reasoned with themselves, saying, “If we say, ‘From heaven,’ he will say, ‘Why didn’t you believe him?’
verses.explainer: And they discussed it among themselves, saying: 'If we say, From heaven, he will say, Why did you not believe him?'

verses.text: But if we say, ‘From men,’ all the people will stone us, for they are persuaded that John was a prophet.”
verses.explainer: 'But if we say, From man, all the people will stone us, for they are convinced that John was a prophet.' Political calculation overrides honest engagement.

verses.text: They answered that they didn’t know where it was from.
verses.explainer: So they answer that they do not know where it came from. They choose evasion over truth.

verses.text:
Jesus said to them,
“Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.”
verses.explainer: And Jesus says to them: 'Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.' Their refusal to engage honestly forfeits their right to an answer.

verses.text:
He began to tell the people this parable.
“A

man planted a vineyard, and rented it out to some farmers, and went into another country for a long time.
verses.explainer: And he begins to tell the people this parable: 'A man planted a vineyard and let it out to tenants and went into another country for a long while.' The vineyard is a well-known symbol for Israel.

verses.text: At the proper season, he sent a servant to the farmers to collect his share of the fruit of the vineyard. But the farmers beat him, and sent him away empty.
verses.explainer: 'When the time came, he sent a servant to the tenants, so that they would give him some of the fruit of the vineyard. But the tenants beat him and sent him away empty-handed.' The first servant represents the early prophets, rejected by Israel's leaders.

verses.text: He sent yet another servant, and they also beat him, and treated him shamefully, and sent him away empty.
verses.explainer: 'And he sent another servant. But they also beat and treated him shamefully, and sent him away empty-handed.' The pattern of rejection escalates.

verses.text: He sent yet a third, and they also wounded him, and threw him out.
verses.explainer: 'And he sent yet a third. This one also they wounded and cast out.' Three servants rejected with increasing violence.

verses.text: The lord of the vineyard said, ‘What shall I do? I will send my beloved son. It may be that seeing him, they will respect him.’
verses.explainer: 'Then the owner of the vineyard said, What shall I do? I will send my beloved son; perhaps they will respect him.' The phrase 'beloved son' echoes God's declaration at Jesus' baptism (3:22) and Transfiguration (9:35). The audience cannot miss the identification.

verses.text: “But when the farmers saw him, they reasoned among themselves, saying, ‘This is the heir. Come, let’s kill him, that the inheritance may be ours.’
verses.explainer: 'But when the tenants saw him, they said to themselves, This is the heir. Let us kill him, so that the inheritance may be ours.' The tenants' logic is insane: killing the heir will not give them the property. Yet it describes exactly what the authorities are planning.

verses.text: They threw him out of the vineyard, and killed him. What therefore will the lord of the vineyard do to them?
verses.explainer: 'And they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him. What then will the owner of the vineyard do to them?' Jesus outside the vineyard -- Jesus crucified outside Jerusalem's walls.

verses.text:
He will come and destroy these farmers, and will give the vineyard to others.”
When they heard it, they said, “May it never be!”
verses.explainer: 'He will come and destroy those tenants and give the vineyard to others.' The consequence: the current leadership will be replaced. When they hear this, they say: 'Surely not!' -- recognizing the implication.

verses.text:
But he looked at them, and said,
“Then what is this that is written,
‘The stone which the builders rejected,
the same was made the chief cornerstone?’
verses.explainer: But Jesus looks directly at them and says: 'What then is this that is written: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone?' Psalm 118:22 -- the very psalm the crowd quoted during the Triumphal Entry. The rejected stone becomes the foundation.

verses.text:
Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces,

but it will crush whomever it falls on to dust.”
verses.explainer: 'Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him.' The stone is either a stumbling block or a crushing weight. There is no neutral response to Jesus.

verses.text: The chief priests and the scribes sought to lay hands on him that very hour, but they feared the people—for they knew he had spoken this parable against them.
verses.explainer: The scribes and the chief priests seek to lay hands on him at that very hour, for they perceive that he has told this parable against them, but they fear the people. They understand the parable perfectly -- and it enrages them.

verses.text: They watched him, and sent out spies, who pretended to be righteous, that they might trap him in something he said, so as to deliver him up to the power and authority of the governor.
verses.explainer: So they watch him and send spies, who pretend to be sincere, that they might catch him in something he said, so as to deliver him up to the authority and jurisdiction of the governor. The strategy shifts to entrapment.

verses.text: They asked him, “Teacher, we know that you say and teach what is right, and aren’t partial to anyone, but truly teach the way of God.
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Teacher, we know that you speak and teach rightly, and show no partiality, but truly teach the way of God.' The flattery is transparent and calculated.

verses.text: Is it lawful for us to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?”
verses.explainer: 'Is it lawful for us to give tribute to Caesar, or not?' The perfect trap: if Jesus says yes, he alienates the crowds who resent Roman taxation. If he says no, he can be reported to Rome for inciting tax revolt.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The tribute question', 'explanation': "The Roman head tax (tributum capitis) was paid with a denarius bearing the emperor's image and an inscription calling him 'son of the divine Augustus.' Paying it acknowledged Roman sovereignty and, for many Jews, violated the commandment against graven images. The question was designed to force Jesus into a politically fatal choice."}

verses.text:
But he perceived their craftiness, and said to them,
“Why do you test me?
verses.explainer: But he perceives their craftiness and says to them: 'Show me a denarius.' He turns the question into an object lesson.

verses.text:
Show me a denarius. Whose image and inscription are on it?”

They answered, “Caesar’s.”
verses.explainer: 'Whose likeness and inscription does it have?' They say: 'Caesar's.' The coin itself carries the answer.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Then give to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s, and to God the things that are God’s.”
verses.explainer: He says to them: 'Then render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' A response of genius that operates on multiple levels. On the surface: pay your taxes. At a deeper level: Caesar's coin bears Caesar's image, but human beings bear God's image. Give the coin to Caesar; give yourself to God.

thenVsNow.then: Jesus' answer avoided both treason and collaboration. It acknowledged the practical reality of Roman rule while subordinating it to the absolute claim of God. His questioners expected either/or; he gave them both/and.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been foundational for Christian political theology -- the basis for distinguishing between religious and civil authority. It has been used both to support paying taxes and to justify civil disobedience when Caesar's demands conflict with God's.

verses.text: They weren’t able to trap him in his words before the people. They marveled at his answer, and were silent.
verses.explainer: And they are not able in the presence of the people to catch him in what he says, but marveling at his answer they become silent. The trap fails completely.

verses.text: Some of the Sadducees came to him, those who deny that there is a resurrection.
verses.explainer: There come to him some Sadducees, those who deny that there is a resurrection. They ask him a question. A different faction tries a different approach.

verses.text: They asked him, “Teacher, Moses wrote to us that if a man’s brother dies having a wife, and he is childless, his brother should take the wife, and raise up children for his brother.
verses.explainer: 'Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother dies, having a wife but no children, the man must take the widow and raise up offspring for his brother.' They cite the levirate marriage law from Deuteronomy 25:5.

verses.text: There were therefore seven brothers. The first took a wife, and died childless.
verses.explainer: 'Now there were seven brothers. The first took a wife, and died without children.'

verses.text: The second took her as wife, and he died childless.
verses.explainer: 'And the second took her, and the third, and likewise all seven left no children and died.'

verses.text: The third took her, and likewise the seven all left no children, and died.
verses.explainer: 'Afterward the woman also died.'

verses.text: Afterward the woman also died.
verses.explainer: 'In the resurrection, therefore, whose wife will the woman be? For the seven had her as wife.' The scenario is designed to make resurrection look absurd.

verses.text: Therefore in the resurrection whose wife of them will she be? For the seven had her as a wife.”
verses.explainer: The question seems to present an impossible problem if resurrection is real.

verses.text:
Jesus said to them,
“The children of this age marry, and are given in marriage.
verses.explainer: And Jesus says to them: 'The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage.' In the present age, marriage serves biological and social functions.

verses.text: But those who are considered worthy to attain to that age and the resurrection from the dead, neither marry, nor are given in marriage.
verses.explainer: 'But those who are considered worthy to attain to that age and to the resurrection from the dead neither marry nor are given in marriage.' The resurrection life is not a continuation of earthly life but a transformation. Marriage as a human institution does not continue.

verses.text: For they can’t die any more, for they are like the angels, and are children of God, being children of the resurrection.
verses.explainer: 'For they cannot die anymore, because they are equal to angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection.' The resurrected are deathless, like angels. Marriage, which serves procreation in a world of death, is no longer necessary in a world without death.

verses.text: But that the dead are raised, even Moses showed at the bush, when he called the Lord ‘The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.’
verses.explainer: 'But that the dead are raised, even Moses showed, in the passage about the bush, where he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob.' Jesus now proves resurrection from the Torah itself -- the only books the Sadducees accept as authoritative.

verses.text: Now he is not the God of the dead, but of the living, for all are alive to him.”
verses.explainer: 'Now he is not God of the dead, but of the living, for all live to him.' If God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, they must still be alive in some sense. God's covenant relationship with them implies their continued existence.

verses.text: Some of the scribes answered, “Teacher, you speak well.”
verses.explainer: Then some of the scribes answer: 'Teacher, you have spoken well.' Even Jesus' opponents acknowledge the brilliance of his argument.

verses.text: They didn’t dare to ask him any more questions.
verses.explainer: For they no longer dare to ask him any question. The series of public challenges ends. Jesus has defeated every trap.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Why do they say that the Christ is David’s son?
verses.explainer: But he says to them: 'How can they say that the Christ is David's son?' Having answered their questions, Jesus poses his own.

verses.text:
David himself says in the book of Psalms,
‘The Lord said to my Lord,
“Sit at my right hand,
verses.explainer: 'For David himself says in the Book of Psalms, The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand...' Jesus quotes Psalm 110:1, which was universally attributed to David and considered messianic.

verses.text: until I make your enemies the footstool of your feet.”’
verses.explainer: '...until I make your enemies your footstool.' The psalm envisions the Messiah enthroned at God's right hand, with all enemies subdued.

verses.text: “David therefore calls him Lord, so how is he his son?”
verses.explainer: 'David thus calls him Lord, so how is he his son?' If the Messiah is merely David's descendant, David would not call him Lord. The Messiah must be something greater than a human king in David's line.

verses.text: In the hearing of all the people, he said to his disciples,
verses.explainer: And in the hearing of all the people he says to his disciples: 'Beware of the scribes.' A public warning about the religious establishment.

verses.text: “Beware of those scribes who like to walk in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces, the best seats in the synagogues, and the best places at feasts;
verses.explainer: '...who like to walk around in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces and the best seats in the synagogues and the places of honor at feasts.' Their religion is performed for public admiration.

verses.text: who devour widows’ houses, and for a pretense make long prayers: these will receive greater condemnation.”
verses.explainer: '...who devour widows' houses and for a pretense make long prayers. They will receive the greater condemnation.' The most devastating charge: they exploit the most vulnerable while performing elaborate piety. Their punishment will be proportional to their hypocrisy.

explainer.summary: A poor widow's offering of two small coins demonstrates the principle that God measures giving by sacrifice, not amount. Jesus then delivers the Olivet Discourse, his longest prophetic speech in Luke, predicting the Temple's destruction, the fall of Jerusalem, cosmic upheavals, and the coming of the Son of Man. He warns his disciples to endure persecution, flee when Jerusalem is surrounded by armies, and remain alert for his return. The chapter anchors Christian hope in the certainty that 'this generation will not pass away until all has taken place.'
themes: Sacrificial giving versus abundant giving
The destruction of the Temple and Jerusalem
Persecution and endurance
The coming of the Son of Man
Watchfulness and readiness
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 21 transitions from the Temple controversies to eschatological prophecy. The widow's offering closes the theme of true versus false religion. The Olivet Discourse prepares the disciples for the events that will follow: persecution, the fall of Jerusalem, and the long wait for Jesus' return.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The smallest bronze coin in circulation, worth abo
wordStudies.meaning: The smallest bronze coin in circulation, worth about 1/128 of a denarius. Two lepta together were worth less than the price of a sparrow. The widow's offering was financially insignificant but spiritually supreme because it represented everything she had.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Release through the payment of a ransom
wordStudies.meaning: Release through the payment of a ransom. When cosmic signs appear, believers should lift their heads because their 'redemption is drawing near.' The horrors of the end are simultaneously the birth pangs of liberation.

verses.text: He looked up, and saw the rich people who were putting their gifts into the treasury.
verses.explainer: Jesus looks up and sees the rich putting their gifts into the offering box. The Temple treasury had thirteen trumpet-shaped collection vessels in the Court of Women.

verses.text: He saw a certain poor widow casting in two small brass coins.
verses.explainer: And he sees a poor widow put in two small copper coins. Her offering is the smallest possible amount -- two lepta, worth a fraction of a penny.

verses.text:
He said,
“Truly I tell you, this poor widow put in more than all of them,
verses.explainer: And he says: 'Truly, I tell you, this poor widow has put in more than all of them.' By every worldly measure, her offering is the least. By God's measure, it is the most.

verses.text: for all these put in gifts for God from their abundance, but she, out of her poverty, put in all that she had to live on.”
verses.explainer: 'For they all contributed out of their abundance, but she out of her poverty put in all she had to live on.' The rich give from surplus; the widow gives from substance. The difference between the two is the difference between comfortable generosity and total sacrifice.

thenVsNow.then: The widow gives her last two coins -- her entire livelihood -- to the Temple. Coming after Jesus' condemnation of leaders who 'devour widows' houses' (20:47), there is a bitter irony: the very institution that should protect her is consuming her last resources.
thenVsNow.now: The widow's mite has become proverbial for disproportionate generosity. It has inspired charitable giving across cultures by establishing the principle that sacrifice, not amount, is the measure of true giving.

verses.text: As some were talking about the temple and how it was decorated with beautiful stones and gifts, he said,
verses.explainer: And while some are speaking of the Temple, how it is adorned with noble stones and offerings, he says: 'As for these things that you see...' The Temple was one of the wonders of the ancient world, covered in gold and white marble.

verses.text: “As for these things which you see, the days will come, in which there will not be left here one stone on another that will not be thrown down.”
verses.explainer: '...the days will come when there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down.' Total destruction. Every stone dismantled. This was fulfilled precisely in 70 AD when the Romans destroyed the Temple.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "The Temple's destruction", 'explanation': "Herod's Temple was a massive complex that took decades to build. It was the center of Jewish religious, economic, and national identity. Its destruction by the Roman general Titus in 70 AD was the most traumatic event in Jewish history between the Babylonian exile and the Holocaust."}

verses.text: They asked him, “Teacher, so when will these things be? What is the sign that these things are about to happen?”
verses.explainer: And they ask him: 'Teacher, when will these things be, and what will be the sign when these things are about to take place?' The disciples want a timeline and signs.

verses.text:
He said,
“Watch out that you don’t get led astray, for many will come in my name, saying, ‘I am he,’ and, ‘The time is at hand.’ Therefore don’t follow them.
verses.explainer: He says: 'See that you are not led astray. For many will come in my name, saying, I am he! and, The time is at hand! Do not go after them.' The first warning: do not follow false messiahs or panic at false alarms.

verses.text: When you hear of wars and disturbances, don’t be terrified, for these things must happen first, but the end won’t come immediately.”
verses.explainer: 'And when you hear of wars and tumults, do not be terrified, for these things must first take place, but the end will not be at once.' Wars and upheavals are not signs of the end but precursors. Do not confuse them.

verses.text:
Then he said to them,
“Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom.
verses.explainer: 'Then he says to them, Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom.' International conflict on a large scale.

verses.text: There will be great earthquakes, famines, and plagues in various places. There will be terrors and great signs from heaven.
verses.explainer: 'There will be great earthquakes, and in various places famines and pestilences. And there will be terrors and great signs from heaven.' Natural disasters and cosmic portents.

verses.text: But before all these things, they will lay their hands on you and will persecute you, delivering you up to synagogues and prisons, bringing you before kings and governors for my name’s sake.
verses.explainer: 'But before all this they will lay their hands on you and persecute you, delivering you up to the synagogues and prisons, and you will be brought before kings and governors for my name's sake.' Persecution is not a possibility but a certainty, and it comes first.

thenVsNow.then: This prophecy was fulfilled precisely in the book of Acts: the apostles were arrested, beaten, imprisoned, and brought before rulers -- including Herod Agrippa I and the Roman governors Felix and Festus.
thenVsNow.now: Christian persecution continues in many parts of the world. Jesus' promise that persecution is both inevitable and purposeful ('it will be your opportunity to bear witness') continues to sustain persecuted believers.

verses.text: It will turn out as a testimony for you.
verses.explainer: 'This will be your opportunity to bear witness.' Persecution is reframed as mission. Trials become testimonies.

verses.text: Settle it therefore in your hearts not to meditate beforehand how to answer,
verses.explainer: 'Settle it therefore in your minds not to meditate beforehand how to answer.' Do not rehearse defense speeches. Trust the moment.

verses.text: for I will give you a mouth and wisdom which all your adversaries will not be able to withstand or to contradict.
verses.explainer: 'For I will give you a mouth and wisdom, which none of your adversaries will be able to withstand or contradict.' Divine wisdom will be provided in the hour of need.

verses.text: You will be handed over even by parents, brothers, relatives, and friends. They will cause some of you to be put to death.
verses.explainer: 'You will be delivered up even by parents and brothers and relatives and friends, and some of you they will put to death.' The division Jesus predicted (12:52-53) reaches its extreme form: family members betraying each other.

verses.text: You will be hated by all men for my name’s sake.
verses.explainer: 'You will be hated by all for my name's sake.' Universal hostility. The disciples should expect no safe quarter.

verses.text: And not a hair of your head will perish.
verses.explainer: 'But not a hair of your head will perish.' A paradox: some will be killed (v.16), yet not a hair will perish. Physical death does not mean ultimate loss. God preserves what matters eternally.

verses.text: “By your endurance you will win your lives.
verses.explainer: 'By your endurance you will gain your lives.' The key virtue in the face of persecution is not victory but endurance. Patient faithfulness is the path to true life.

verses.text: “But when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation is at hand.
verses.explainer: 'But when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, then know that its desolation has come near.' Luke replaces Mark's cryptic 'abomination of desolation' with a clear reference to military siege. His audience can recognize the sign: when armies encircle the city, judgment has arrived.

verses.text: Then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains. Let those who are in the middle of her depart. Let those who are in the country not enter therein.
verses.explainer: 'Then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains, and let those who are inside the city depart, and let not those who are out in the country enter it.' Practical advice for the coming catastrophe: get out of Jerusalem. Early Christian tradition records that the Jerusalem church fled to Pella in Transjordan before the Roman siege.

verses.text: For these are days of vengeance, that all things which are written may be fulfilled.
verses.explainer: 'For these are days of vengeance, to fulfill all that is written.' The destruction of Jerusalem is not random violence but divine judgment fulfilling prophetic warnings.

verses.text: Woe to those who are pregnant and to those who nurse infants in those days! For there will be great distress in the land, and wrath to this people.
verses.explainer: 'Alas for women who are pregnant and for those who are nursing infants in those days! For there will be great distress upon the earth and wrath against this people.' The most vulnerable will suffer most in the coming catastrophe.

verses.text: They will fall by the edge of the sword, and will be led captive into all the nations. Jerusalem will be trampled down by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled.
verses.explainer: 'They will fall by the edge of the sword and be led captive among all nations, and Jerusalem will be trampled underfoot by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled.' This describes the events of 70 AD with remarkable precision: mass death, enslavement, and foreign occupation.

thenVsNow.then: Josephus records that 1.1 million Jews died during the siege and nearly 100,000 were enslaved. The Temple was burned, the city demolished, and the remaining population dispersed. 'The times of the Gentiles' suggests a limited period of Gentile dominance.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has been central to Christian interpretations of Jewish history and the modern state of Israel. The phrase 'until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled' implies an eventual end to Gentile domination, which some link to the return of Jewish sovereignty.

verses.text: There will be signs in the sun, moon, and stars; and on the earth anxiety of nations, in perplexity for the roaring of the sea and the waves;
verses.explainer: 'And there will be signs in sun and moon and stars, and on the earth distress of nations in perplexity because of the roaring of the sea and the waves.' The discourse shifts from Jerusalem's fall to cosmic upheaval.

verses.text: men fainting for fear, and for expectation of the things which are coming on the world: for the powers of the heavens will be shaken.
verses.explainer: 'People fainting with fear and with foreboding of what is coming on the world. For the powers of the heavens will be shaken.' The cosmic order itself is destabilized.

verses.text: Then they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory.
verses.explainer: 'And then they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory.' The climax: the Son of Man arrives in glory. The imagery draws directly from Daniel 7:13-14.

verses.text: But when these things begin to happen, look up, and lift up your heads, because your redemption is near.”
verses.explainer: 'Now when these things begin to take place, straighten up and raise your heads, because your redemption is drawing near.' For believers, the cosmic upheavals are not cause for terror but for hope. The same events that frighten the world signal liberation for God's people.

verses.text:
He told them a parable.
“See the fig tree, and all the trees.
verses.explainer: And he tells them a parable: 'Look at the fig tree, and all the trees.' A simple observation from nature.

verses.text: When they are already budding, you see it and know by your own selves that the summer is already near.
verses.explainer: 'When they are already budding, you see for yourselves and know that the summer is already near.' Leaf buds are reliable signs of approaching summer.

verses.text: Even so you also, when you see these things happening, know that God’s Kingdom is near.
verses.explainer: 'So also, when you see these things taking place, you know that the kingdom of God is near.' The prophesied events are signs of the kingdom, just as buds are signs of summer.

verses.text: Most certainly I tell you, this generation will not pass away until all things are accomplished.
verses.explainer: 'Truly, I say to you, this generation will not pass away until all has taken place.' This likely refers to the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD, which occurred within the lifetime of Jesus' hearers.

verses.text: Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will by no means pass away.
verses.explainer: 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.' The physical universe is impermanent; Jesus' words are eternal. A staggering claim about the authority of his teaching.

verses.text: “So be careful, or your hearts will be loaded down with carousing, drunkenness, and cares of this life, and that day will come on you suddenly.
verses.explainer: 'But watch yourselves lest your hearts be weighed down with dissipation and drunkenness and cares of this life, and that day come upon you suddenly like a trap.' The danger is not just persecution but complacency -- letting pleasure and worry dull spiritual alertness.

verses.text: For it will come like a snare on all those who dwell on the surface of all the earth.
verses.explainer: 'For it will come upon all who dwell on the face of the whole earth.' The final events affect everyone, everywhere.

verses.text: Therefore be watchful all the time, praying that you may be counted worthy to escape all these things that will happen, and to stand before the Son of Man.”
verses.explainer: 'But stay awake at all times, praying that you may have strength to escape all these things that are going to take place, and to stand before the Son of Man.' The practical application: perpetual wakefulness and prayer. The goal is to stand before the Son of Man with confidence.

verses.text: Every day Jesus was teaching in the temple, and every night he would go out and spend the night on the mountain that is called Olivet.
verses.explainer: And every day he is teaching in the Temple, but at night he goes out and lodges on the mount called Olivet. Jesus' daily routine in his final week: teaching in the Temple by day, camping on the Mount of Olives by night.

verses.text: All the people came early in the morning to him in the temple to hear him.
verses.explainer: And all the people come early in the morning to hear him in the Temple. The crowd's enthusiasm is undiminished. The authorities must act at night, away from the people.

explainer.summary: The passion narrative begins. Judas agrees to betray Jesus. At the Passover meal, Jesus institutes the Lord's Supper, predicts Peter's denial, and prepares the disciples for a changed situation. In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus agonizes in prayer while the disciples sleep. Judas arrives with a crowd and betrays Jesus with a kiss. Peter follows at a distance and denies Jesus three times. Jesus is mocked, beaten, and brought before the Sanhedrin, where he acknowledges his identity as the Son of Man.
themes: The Passover fulfilled in the Lord's Supper
Servant leadership versus worldly power
Agonizing prayer in the face of suffering
Betrayal, denial, and the collapse of human loyalty
Jesus' trial before the Jewish council
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 22 brings the Gospel to its crisis point. Everything Luke has narrated converges here: the journey to Jerusalem reaches its destination in the upper room and the garden. The one who came to seek and save the lost is himself handed over, denied, and condemned.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A solemn binding agreement
wordStudies.meaning: A solemn binding agreement. At the Last Supper, Jesus says the cup is 'the new covenant in my blood.' This echoes Jeremiah 31:31-34, where God promised a new covenant written on hearts, not stone tablets. Jesus' death ratifies this new covenant.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Intense emotional struggle or anguish
wordStudies.meaning: Intense emotional struggle or anguish. Luke alone records that Jesus' anguish in Gethsemane was so intense that his sweat became like great drops of blood -- a condition known medically as hematidrosis, caused by extreme stress.

verses.text: Now the feast of unleavened bread, which is called the Passover, was approaching.
verses.explainer: Now the Feast of Unleavened Bread drew near, which is called the Passover. The two festivals -- Passover (Nisan 14) and Unleavened Bread (Nisan 15-21) -- were popularly merged into one event.

verses.text: The chief priests and the scribes sought how they might put him to death, for they feared the people.
verses.explainer: And the chief priests and the scribes are seeking how to put him to death, for they fear the people. The authorities want Jesus dead but cannot arrest him publicly.

verses.text: Satan entered into Judas, who was also called Iscariot, who was counted with the twelve.
verses.explainer: Then Satan enters into Judas called Iscariot, who is of the number of the twelve. Luke attributes Judas' betrayal to satanic influence. Satan, who departed after the temptation 'until an opportune time' (4:13), has found his moment.

verses.text: He went away, and talked with the chief priests and captains about how he might deliver him to them.
verses.explainer: He goes away and confers with the chief priests and officers how he might betray Jesus to them. Judas approaches the authorities, not the reverse. The initiative is his.

verses.text: They were glad, and agreed to give him money.
verses.explainer: And they are glad and agree to give him money. The amount (thirty pieces of silver in Matthew) is not specified by Luke. The religious leaders are delighted to have an insider willing to help.

verses.text: He consented, and sought an opportunity to deliver him to them in the absence of the multitude.
verses.explainer: So he consents and seeks an opportunity to betray him to them in the absence of a crowd. The betrayal requires isolation -- away from the protective crowds.

verses.text: The day of unleavened bread came, on which the Passover must be sacrificed.
verses.explainer: Then came the day of Unleavened Bread, on which the Passover lamb had to be sacrificed. The theological irony: as the Passover lamb is being sacrificed throughout Jerusalem, the true Lamb prepares for his own sacrifice.

verses.text:
Jesus sent Peter and John, saying,
“Go and prepare the Passover for us, that we may eat.”
verses.explainer: Jesus sends Peter and John, saying: 'Go and prepare the Passover for us, that we may eat it.' The inner circle is entrusted with the most important meal in Jewish history -- and the most important meal in Christian history.

verses.text: They said to him, “Where do you want us to prepare?”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Where will you have us prepare it?' The location must be kept secret from Judas.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Behold, when you have entered into the city, a man carrying a pitcher of water will meet you. Follow him into the house which he enters.
verses.explainer: He says: 'Behold, when you have entered the city, a man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him into the house that he enters.' A man carrying water was unusual -- typically a woman's task. He would be easy to spot.

verses.text: Tell the master of the house, ‘The Teacher says to you, “Where is the guest room, where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?”’
verses.explainer: 'And tell the master of the house, The Teacher says to you, Where is the guest room, where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?' A pre-arranged signal, perhaps, or prophetic knowledge.

verses.text: He will show you a large, furnished upper room. Make preparations there.”
verses.explainer: 'And he will show you a large upper room furnished; prepare it there.' The room is ready and furnished with reclining couches.

verses.text: They went, found things as Jesus had told them, and they prepared the Passover.
verses.explainer: And they go and find it just as he has told them, and they prepare the Passover. Everything unfolds as Jesus predicted.

verses.text: When the hour had come, he sat down with the twelve apostles.
verses.explainer: And when the hour came, he reclines at table, and the apostles with him. The last communal meal begins.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer,
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer.' 'Earnestly desired' translates a Hebrew idiom meaning 'with deep longing.' Jesus has looked forward to this meal with intense anticipation.

verses.text: for I tell you, I will no longer by any means eat of it until it is fulfilled in God’s Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: 'For I tell you I will not eat it until it is fulfilled in the kingdom of God.' This Passover finds its ultimate meaning in the kingdom. Jesus will not share this meal again until the kingdom's consummation.

verses.text:
He received a cup, and when he had given thanks, he said,
“Take this, and share it among yourselves,
verses.explainer: And he takes a cup, and when he has given thanks he says: 'Take this, and divide it among yourselves.' The first cup, probably the cup of blessing that opened the Passover seder.

verses.text: for I tell you, I will not drink at all again from the fruit of the vine, until God’s Kingdom comes.”
verses.explainer: 'For I tell you that from now on I will not drink of the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes.' A vow of abstinence: Jesus pledges to fast from wine until the kingdom arrives in fullness.

verses.text:
He took bread, and when he had given thanks, he broke, and gave it to them, saying,
“This is my body which is given for you. Do this in memory of me.”
verses.explainer: And he takes bread, and when he has given thanks, he breaks it and gives it to them, saying: 'This is my body, which is given for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' The institution of the Eucharist. Bread becomes the sign of Jesus' body given in sacrifice. 'Do this in remembrance' establishes a perpetual memorial.

thenVsNow.then: The Passover meal commemorated God's deliverance of Israel from Egypt. Jesus reinterprets it: the bread is no longer about the Exodus from Egypt but about his body, broken for the liberation of humanity from sin and death.
thenVsNow.now: The Lord's Supper (Eucharist, Communion) is the most widely practiced Christian ritual worldwide. Whether understood as transubstantiation, spiritual presence, or memorial, it connects every celebration to this upper room and to Jesus' sacrifice.

verses.text:
Likewise, he took the cup after supper, saying,
“This cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is poured out for you.
verses.explainer: And likewise the cup after they have eaten, saying: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood.' The wine represents Jesus' blood, poured out to seal a new covenant between God and humanity.

verses.text: But behold, the hand of him who betrays me is with me on the table.
verses.explainer: 'But behold, the hand of him who betrays me is with me on the table.' The betrayer is present at this intimate meal. The treachery is not from outside but from within the innermost circle.

verses.text: The Son of Man indeed goes, as it has been determined, but woe to that man through whom he is betrayed!”
verses.explainer: 'For the Son of Man goes as it has been determined, but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed!' Divine sovereignty and human responsibility coexist: the plan is determined, but the betrayer is accountable.

verses.text: They began to question among themselves, which of them it was who would do this thing.
verses.explainer: And they begin to question one another, which of them it could be who is going to do this. The disciples' inability to identify the betrayer among themselves shows how well Judas has concealed his intentions.

verses.text: There arose also a contention among them, which of them was considered to be greatest.
verses.explainer: A dispute also arises among them, as to which of them is to be regarded as the greatest. At the very table where Jesus predicts his death, the disciples argue about rank. The contrast is heartbreaking.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“The kings of the nations lord it over them, and those who have authority over them are called ‘benefactors.’
verses.explainer: And Jesus says to them: 'The kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship over them, and those in authority over them are called benefactors.' The world's model of leadership: power over others, decorated with honorific titles.

verses.text: But not so with you. But one who is the greater among you, let him become as the younger, and one who is governing, as one who serves.
verses.explainer: 'But not so with you. Rather, let the greatest among you become as the youngest, and the leader as one who serves.' The kingdom inverts the world's power structure. Leaders serve; the greatest act youngest.

verses.text: For who is greater, one who sits at the table, or one who serves? Isn’t it he who sits at the table? But I am among you as one who serves.
verses.explainer: 'For who is the greater, one who reclines at table or one who serves? Is it not the one who reclines at table? But I am among you as the one who serves.' Jesus identifies himself as the servant. The greatest person in the room is serving dinner.

verses.text: But you are those who have continued with me in my trials.
verses.explainer: 'You are those who have stayed with me in my trials.' Jesus acknowledges the disciples' loyalty through difficulties, even as he knows it is about to fail.

verses.text: I confer on you a kingdom, even as my Father conferred on me,
verses.explainer: 'And I assign to you, as my Father assigned to me, a kingdom.' The disciples will share in Jesus' kingdom -- not despite their humble position but through it.

verses.text: that you may eat and drink at my table in my Kingdom. You will sit on thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.”
verses.explainer: '...that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel.' The ultimate reward: sharing Jesus' royal table and governing the restored Israel.

verses.text:
The Lord said,
“Simon, Simon, behold, Satan asked to have all of you, that he might sift you as wheat,
verses.explainer: 'Simon, Simon, behold, Satan demanded to have you, that he might sift you like wheat.' Jesus addresses Peter directly with a warning: Satan has requested permission to test the disciples. 'You' is plural -- all of them will be tested.

verses.text: but I prayed for you, that your faith wouldn’t fail. You, when once you have turned again, establish your brothers.”
verses.explainer: 'But I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail. And when you have turned again, strengthen your brothers.' Jesus has specifically prayed for Peter. He assumes Peter will fall ('when you have turned again') but also that he will recover and strengthen others.

verses.text: He said to him, “Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death!”
verses.explainer: Peter says: 'Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death.' Peter's declaration of loyalty is sincere but overconfident.

verses.text:
He said,
“I tell you, Peter, the rooster will by no means crow today until you deny that you know me three times.”
verses.explainer: Jesus says: 'I tell you, Peter, the rooster will not crow this day, until you deny three times that you know me.' The prediction is specific: today, before dawn, three denials.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“When I sent you out without purse, and wallet, and shoes, did you lack anything?”
They said, “Nothing.”
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'When I sent you out with no moneybag or knapsack or sandals, did you lack anything?' They say: 'Nothing.' A reminder of the earlier mission when they needed nothing.

verses.text:
Then he said to them,
“But now, whoever has a purse, let him take it, and likewise a wallet. Whoever has none, let him sell his cloak, and buy a sword.
verses.explainer: He says: 'But now let the one who has a moneybag take it, and likewise a knapsack. And let the one who has no sword sell his cloak and buy one.' The situation has changed. The earlier hospitality that sustained the missionaries will not be available. They will face hostility.

verses.text:
For I tell you that this which is written must still be fulfilled in me: ‘He was counted with transgressors.’
For that which concerns me has an end.”
verses.explainer: 'For I tell you that this Scripture must be fulfilled in me: And he was numbered with the transgressors. For what is written about me has its fulfillment.' Jesus quotes Isaiah 53:12. He will be treated as a criminal.

verses.text:
They said, “Lord, behold, here are two swords.”
He said to them,
“That is enough.”
verses.explainer: And they say: 'Look, Lord, here are two swords.' And he says: 'It is enough.' The disciples take the sword instruction literally. Jesus' response -- 'It is enough' -- may be resignation or dismissal: 'That is enough of this conversation.'

verses.text: He came out, and went, as his custom was, to the Mount of Olives. His disciples also followed him.
verses.explainer: And he comes out and goes, as was his custom, to the Mount of Olives, and the disciples follow him. The nightly pattern continues, but this night will be different.

verses.text:
When he was at the place, he said to them,
“Pray that you don’t enter into temptation.”
verses.explainer: And when he comes to the place, he says to them: 'Pray that you may not enter into temptation.' He calls them to the spiritual alertness they will fail to maintain.

verses.text: He was withdrawn from them about a stone’s throw, and he knelt down and prayed,
verses.explainer: And he withdraws from them about a stone's throw, and kneels down and prays. Kneeling was unusual for Jewish prayer (standing was standard), indicating the intensity of Jesus' distress.

verses.text:
saying,
“Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done.”
verses.explainer: 'Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done.' The most intimate prayer in the Gospels. Jesus asks for an alternative to the cross while submitting completely to the Father's will. The 'cup' represents the suffering and divine judgment he is about to bear.

thenVsNow.then: Gethsemane reveals that Jesus' obedience was not automatic or costless. He genuinely desired another way. His submission was a real choice made in real agony, not a predetermined script performed without inner conflict.
thenVsNow.now: Gethsemane has been the most important passage in Christian spirituality for understanding prayer in suffering. It teaches that honest expression of anguish is not unfaithful, and that yielding to God's will is the highest form of prayer, especially when it costs everything.

verses.text: An angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him.
verses.explainer: And there appears to him an angel from heaven, strengthening him. This verse, found in some early manuscripts, shows divine comfort in the midst of divine agony. God does not remove the cup but sends strength to endure it.

verses.text: Being in agony he prayed more earnestly. His sweat became like great drops of blood falling down on the ground.
verses.explainer: And being in agony he prays more earnestly; and his sweat becomes like great drops of blood falling down to the ground. Luke the physician records a condition consistent with hematidrosis -- sweating blood under extreme stress. The detail underscores the genuine, physical reality of Jesus' suffering.

verses.text: When he rose up from his prayer, he came to the disciples, and found them sleeping because of grief,
verses.explainer: And when he rises from prayer, he comes to the disciples and finds them sleeping for sorrow. They sleep from grief, not indifference. Their emotional exhaustion overwhelms their spiritual resolve.

verses.text:
and said to them,
“Why do you sleep? Rise and pray that you may not enter into temptation.”
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'Why are you sleeping? Rise and pray that you may not enter into temptation.' The exhortation from verse 40 is repeated. They have failed the first test.

verses.text: While he was still speaking, behold, a multitude, and he who was called Judas, one of the twelve, was leading them. He came near to Jesus to kiss him.
verses.explainer: While he is still speaking, there comes a crowd, and the man called Judas, one of the twelve, is leading them. He draws near to Jesus to kiss him. The betrayal comes from within the intimate circle, signaled by the gesture of friendship.

verses.text:
But Jesus said to him,
“Judas, do you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?”
verses.explainer: But Jesus says to him: 'Judas, would you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?' The question exposes the obscenity of the act: using a sign of love as a weapon of betrayal.

verses.text: When those who were around him saw what was about to happen, they said to him, “Lord, shall we strike with the sword?”
verses.explainer: And when those who are around him see what is going to happen, they ask: 'Lord, shall we strike with the sword?' The disciples recall the two swords from verse 38.

verses.text: A certain one of them struck the servant of the high priest, and cut off his right ear.
verses.explainer: And one of them strikes the servant of the high priest and cuts off his right ear. Peter acts impulsively (John 18:10 identifies him), but Luke withholds the name.

verses.text:
But Jesus answered,
“Let me at least do this”—and he touched his ear, and healed him.
verses.explainer: But Jesus says: 'No more of this!' And he touches his ear and heals him. Jesus' last healing miracle is performed on an enemy, during his own arrest. It is uniquely recorded in Luke and perfectly captures Luke's portrait of Jesus: compassionate even to those who come to destroy him.

verses.text:
Jesus said to the chief priests, captains of the temple, and elders, who had come against him,
“Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs?
verses.explainer: Then Jesus says to the chief priests and officers of the Temple and elders who have come out against him: 'Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs?' They treat a teacher as a terrorist.

verses.text: When I was with you in the temple daily, you didn’t stretch out your hands against me. But this is your hour, and the power of darkness.”
verses.explainer: 'When I was with you day after day in the Temple, you did not lay hands on me. But this is your hour, and the power of darkness.' Jesus acknowledges the temporary triumph of evil. 'The power of darkness' connects back to Satan's entry into Judas (v.3) -- this is a battle in the cosmic war.

verses.text: They seized him, and led him away, and brought him into the high priest’s house. But Peter followed from a distance.
verses.explainer: Then they seize him and lead him away, bringing him into the high priest's house, and Peter is following at a distance. Peter follows -- neither fully with Jesus nor fully separated from him.

verses.text: When they had kindled a fire in the middle of the courtyard, and had sat down together, Peter sat among them.
verses.explainer: And when they have kindled a fire in the middle of the courtyard and sat down together, Peter sits down among them. He warms himself by the enemy's fire.

verses.text: A certain servant girl saw him as he sat in the light, and looking intently at him, said, “This man also was with him.”
verses.explainer: Then a servant girl, seeing him as he sits in the light, looks closely at him and says: 'This man also was with him.' The first challenge comes from a slave girl -- the lowest social position possible.

verses.text: He denied Jesus, saying, “Woman, I don’t know him.”
verses.explainer: But he denies it, saying: 'Woman, I do not know him.' The first denial.

verses.text:
After a little while someone else saw him, and said, “You also are one of them!”
But Peter answered, “Man, I am not!”
verses.explainer: And a little later someone else sees him and says: 'You also are one of them.' But Peter says: 'Man, I am not.' The second denial.

verses.text: After about one hour passed, another confidently affirmed, saying, “Truly this man also was with him, for he is a Galilean!”
verses.explainer: And after an interval of about an hour still another insists, saying: 'Certainly this man also was with him, for he too is a Galilean.' Peter's accent betrays him. The third denial is about to happen.

verses.text: But Peter said, “Man, I don’t know what you are talking about!” Immediately, while he was still speaking, a rooster crowed.
verses.explainer: But Peter says: 'Man, I do not know what you are talking about.' And immediately, while he is still speaking, the rooster crows. The prediction (v.34) is fulfilled to the letter.

verses.text:
The Lord turned, and looked at Peter. Then Peter remembered the Lord’s word, how he said to him,
“Before the rooster crows you will deny me three times.”
verses.explainer: And the Lord turns and looks at Peter. And Peter remembers the saying of the Lord, how he had said to him, 'Before the rooster crows today, you will deny me three times.' Only Luke records this devastating detail: Jesus looks directly at Peter. No words -- just a look that says everything.

thenVsNow.then: The look is not accusation but recognition, sorrow, and perhaps compassion. Jesus had already prayed for Peter (v.32) and predicted his restoration ('when you have turned again'). The look contains both the pain of betrayal and the promise of forgiveness.
thenVsNow.now: Peter's denial and Jesus' look have been the subject of countless paintings, poems, and sermons. They represent the universal human experience of failing in the moment of testing and the equally universal need for the grace that restores.

verses.text: He went out, and wept bitterly.
verses.explainer: And Peter goes out and weeps bitterly. The brave disciple who promised to die for Jesus cannot even acknowledge knowing him. His weeping is the beginning of the repentance Jesus predicted.

verses.text: The men who held Jesus mocked him and beat him.
verses.explainer: Now the men who are holding Jesus in custody mock him and beat him. The abuse begins before any trial.

verses.text: Having blindfolded him, they struck him on the face and asked him, “Prophesy! Who is the one who struck you?”
verses.explainer: They also blindfold him and demand: 'Prophesy! Who is it that struck you?' The irony: they mock his prophetic power while his prophecy about Peter is being fulfilled in the courtyard below.
