verses.text: They spoke many other things against him, insulting him.
verses.explainer: And they speak many other things against him, blaspheming him. The abuse is sustained and varied.

verses.text: As soon as it was day, the assembly of the elders of the people were gathered together, both chief priests and scribes, and they led him away into their council, saying,
verses.explainer: When day comes, the assembly of the elders of the people gather together, both chief priests and scribes. And they lead him away to their council. The Sanhedrin convenes at dawn for a formal proceeding.

verses.text:
“If you are the Christ, tell us.”
But he said to them,
“If I tell you, you won’t believe,
verses.explainer: 'If you are the Christ, tell us.' He says to them: 'If I tell you, you will not believe.' Jesus knows the question is not a genuine inquiry but a trap.

verses.text: and if I ask, you will in no way answer me or let me go.
verses.explainer: 'And if I ask you, you will not answer.' He has already experienced their refusal to engage honestly (20:1-8).

verses.text: From now on, the Son of Man will be seated at the right hand of the power of God.”
verses.explainer: 'But from now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God.' Jesus claims the position described in Psalm 110:1 and Daniel 7:13 -- seated at God's right hand, exercising divine authority.

verses.text:
They all said, “Are you then the Son of God?”
He said to them,
“You say it, because I am.”
verses.explainer: So they all say: 'Are you the Son of God, then?' And he says to them: 'You say that I am.' Jesus neither denies nor qualifies the identification. His response confirms their inference.

verses.text: They said, “Why do we need any more witness? For we ourselves have heard from his own mouth!”
verses.explainer: Then they say: 'What further testimony do we need? We have heard it ourselves from his own lips.' They consider his answer self-incriminating blasphemy -- a capital offense under Jewish law. The trial has produced the verdict the authorities wanted.

explainer.summary: Jesus is brought before Pilate, who declares him innocent but sends him to Herod Antipas. Herod mocks him and sends him back. Pilate again declares Jesus innocent but yields to the crowd's demand for crucifixion, releasing Barabbas instead. On the way to Golgotha, Jesus warns the weeping women of Jerusalem's coming destruction. He is crucified between two criminals, one of whom repents and is promised paradise. Jesus dies with the words 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit.' A centurion declares him righteous, and Joseph of Arimathea buries him in a new tomb.
themes: Jesus' innocence declared repeatedly (three times by Pilate)
The injustice of political expedience
Father, forgive them -- grace from the cross
The repentant criminal and the promise of paradise
Jesus' trust in the Father even in death
The centurion's recognition
explainer.contextInBook: Luke's passion narrative emphasizes Jesus' innocence (declared by Pilate, Herod, and the centurion), his forgiveness of his enemies, and his compassion even while dying. Luke alone records the words to the weeping women, the exchange with the criminal, and the prayer 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit.'

wordStudies.translatedAs: Originally a Persian word for a walled garden or p
wordStudies.meaning: Originally a Persian word for a walled garden or park. In Jewish and Christian thought, paradise represents the presence of God -- the restored Eden. Jesus promises the repentant criminal that 'today' he will be in paradise, indicating immediate conscious fellowship with God after death.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Just, innocent, righteous
wordStudies.meaning: Just, innocent, righteous. The centurion's declaration that Jesus was 'righteous' (23:47) is Luke's substitute for Mark's 'Son of God.' In Luke, Jesus' innocence and righteousness are demonstrated by his conduct on the cross -- forgiving enemies, comforting others, trusting the Father.

verses.text: The whole company of them rose up and brought him before Pilate.
verses.explainer: Then the whole company of them arises and brings him before Pilate. The transfer from Jewish to Roman jurisdiction: only Rome can execute by crucifixion.

verses.text: They began to accuse him, saying, “We found this man perverting the nation, forbidding paying taxes to Caesar, and saying that he himself is Christ, a king.”
verses.explainer: And they begin to accuse him, saying: 'We found this man misleading our nation and forbidding us to give tribute to Caesar, and saying that he himself is Christ, a king.' The charges are political, not religious: sedition, tax revolt, and claiming kingship. These are fabricated from the encounters in chapters 20-22.

verses.text:
Pilate asked him, “Are you the King of the Jews?”
He answered him,
“So you say.”
verses.explainer: And Pilate asks him: 'Are you the King of the Jews?' And he answers: 'You have said so.' An ambiguous confirmation that puts the interpretation back on Pilate.

verses.text: Pilate said to the chief priests and the multitudes, “I find no basis for a charge against this man.”
verses.explainer: Then Pilate says to the chief priests and the crowds: 'I find no guilt in this man.' The first of three declarations of innocence by Pilate.

verses.text: But they insisted, saying, “He stirs up the people, teaching throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee even to this place.”
verses.explainer: But they are urgent, saying: 'He stirs up the people, teaching throughout all Judea, from Galilee even to this place.' They escalate the charges with references to his Galilean origin.

verses.text: But when Pilate heard Galilee mentioned, he asked if the man was a Galilean.
verses.explainer: When Pilate hears this, he asks whether the man is a Galilean. The mention of Galilee gives Pilate an idea.

verses.text: When he found out that he was in Herod’s jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem during those days.
verses.explainer: And when he learns that he belongs to Herod's jurisdiction, he sends him over to Herod, who is himself in Jerusalem at that time. Pilate sees an opportunity to defer the decision -- and to make a political gesture toward Herod.

verses.text: Now when Herod saw Jesus, he was exceedingly glad, for he had wanted to see him for a long time, because he had heard many things about him. He hoped to see some miracle done by him.
verses.explainer: When Herod sees Jesus, he is very glad, for he has long desired to see him, because he has heard about him, and he is hoping to see some sign done by him. Herod's interest is curiosity and entertainment, not justice.

verses.text: He questioned him with many words, but he gave no answers.
verses.explainer: So he questions him at some length, but Jesus makes no answer. Jesus refuses to satisfy Herod's entertainment. Silence before an unserious inquisitor is its own statement.

verses.text: The chief priests and the scribes stood, vehemently accusing him.
verses.explainer: The chief priests and the scribes stand by, vehemently accusing him. The prosecution continues even before this secondary tribunal.

verses.text: Herod with his soldiers humiliated him and mocked him. Dressing him in luxurious clothing, they sent him back to Pilate.
verses.explainer: And Herod with his soldiers treats him with contempt and mocks him. Then, arraying him in splendid clothing, he sends him back to Pilate. The 'splendid clothing' is a mockery of his royal claims.

verses.text: Herod and Pilate became friends with each other that very day, for before that they were enemies with each other.
verses.explainer: And Herod and Pilate become friends with each other that very day, for before this they had been at enmity with each other. The trial of Jesus creates a friendship between two political rivals. Shared hostility toward the innocent bonds the powerful.

thenVsNow.then: The reconciliation of Herod and Pilate through their joint handling of Jesus illustrates how political alliances form around shared interests -- even when those interests involve injustice.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is a sobering reminder that political cooperation is not always moral. Unity in injustice is not peace but complicity.

verses.text: Pilate called together the chief priests, the rulers, and the people,
verses.explainer: Pilate then calls together the chief priests and the rulers and the people. He assembles a public hearing.

verses.text: and said to them, “You brought this man to me as one that perverts the people, and behold, having examined him before you, I found no basis for a charge against this man concerning those things of which you accuse him.
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'You brought me this man as one who was misleading the people. And after examining him before you, behold, I did not find this man guilty of any of your charges against him.' The second declaration of innocence.

verses.text: Neither has Herod, for I sent you to him, and see, nothing worthy of death has been done by him.
verses.explainer: 'Neither did Herod, for he sent him back to us. Look, nothing deserving death has been done by him.' Herod's verdict concurs: not guilty.

verses.text: I will therefore chastise him and release him.”
verses.explainer: 'I will therefore punish and release him.' Pilate offers a compromise: beating instead of execution. Justice is already being sacrificed for political expediency.

verses.text: Now he had to release one prisoner to them at the feast.
verses.explainer: Some manuscripts include: 'Now he was obliged to release one man to them at the festival.' A Passover amnesty custom.

verses.text: But they all cried out together, saying, “Away with this man! Release to us Barabbas!”—
verses.explainer: But they all cry out together: 'Away with this man, and release to us Barabbas!' They choose a convicted insurrectionist and murderer over Jesus.

verses.text: one who was thrown into prison for a certain revolt in the city, and for murder.
verses.explainer: Barabbas had been thrown into prison for an insurrection started in the city and for murder. The irony: Jesus is accused of insurrection and the actual insurrectionist is freed.

verses.text: Then Pilate spoke to them again, wanting to release Jesus,
verses.explainer: Pilate addresses them once more, desiring to release Jesus. He makes a third attempt.

verses.text: but they shouted, saying, “Crucify! Crucify him!”
verses.explainer: But they keep shouting: 'Crucify, crucify him!' The demand for crucifixion -- the most degrading and painful form of execution Rome employed.

verses.text: He said to them the third time, “Why? What evil has this man done? I have found no capital crime in him. I will therefore chastise him and release him.”
verses.explainer: A third time he says to them: 'Why? What evil has he done? I have found in him no guilt deserving death. I will therefore punish and release him.' The third declaration of innocence.

verses.text: But they were urgent with loud voices, asking that he might be crucified. Their voices and the voices of the chief priests prevailed.
verses.explainer: But they are urgent, demanding with loud cries that he should be crucified. And their voices prevail. The mob's volume overwhelms judicial process.

verses.text: Pilate decreed that what they asked for should be done.
verses.explainer: So Pilate decides that their demand should be granted. The Roman governor, who has declared Jesus innocent three times, hands him over for execution. Political pressure defeats truth.

verses.text: He released him who had been thrown into prison for insurrection and murder, for whom they asked, but he delivered Jesus up to their will.
verses.explainer: He releases the man who had been thrown into prison for insurrection and murder, for whom they asked, but he delivers Jesus over to their will. The sentence is stated with devastating irony: the guilty goes free, the innocent goes to death.

verses.text: When they led him away, they grabbed one Simon of Cyrene, coming from the country, and laid on him the cross, to carry it after Jesus.
verses.explainer: And as they lead him away, they seize one Simon of Cyrene, who is coming in from the country, and lay on him the cross, to carry it behind Jesus. Simon is forced to carry the cross -- physically following Jesus on the way to death, fulfilling 14:27 in the most literal way.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Simon of Cyrene', 'explanation': 'Cyrene was a city in modern Libya with a large Jewish community. Simon was likely in Jerusalem for Passover. Roman soldiers could conscript any civilian for forced labor (angareia). His sons Alexander and Rufus (Mark 15:21) apparently became known Christians, suggesting this experience transformed his life.'}

verses.text: A great multitude of the people followed him, including women who also mourned and lamented him.
verses.explainer: And there follows him a great multitude of the people and of women who are mourning and lamenting for him. Professional and spontaneous mourning accompanies the procession.

verses.text:
But Jesus, turning to them, said,
“Daughters of Jerusalem, don’t weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children.
verses.explainer: But turning to them Jesus says: 'Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children.' Even on the way to his death, Jesus is concerned not for himself but for others. This is unique to Luke.

verses.text: For behold, the days are coming in which they will say, ‘Blessed are the barren, the wombs that never bore, and the breasts that never nursed.’
verses.explainer: 'For behold, the days are coming when they will say, Blessed are the barren and the wombs that never bore and the breasts that never nursed!' A reversal of the normal blessing: in the coming catastrophe, childlessness will be an advantage because there will be fewer loved ones to watch suffer.

verses.text: Then they will begin to tell the mountains, ‘Fall on us!’ and tell the hills, ‘Cover us.’
verses.explainer: 'Then they will begin to say to the mountains, Fall on us, and to the hills, Cover us.' People will prefer death to what is coming.

verses.text: For if they do these things in the green tree, what will be done in the dry?”
verses.explainer: 'For if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?' If Rome crucifies an innocent man ('green wood'), what will they do to a guilty nation ('dry wood')?

verses.text: There were also others, two criminals, led with him to be put to death.
verses.explainer: Two others, who are criminals, are led away to be put to death with him. Jesus is literally numbered with the transgressors (Isaiah 53:12), fulfilling the prophecy he quoted at the Last Supper.

verses.text: When they came to the place that is called The Skull, they crucified him there with the criminals, one on the right and the other on the left.
verses.explainer: And when they come to the place that is called The Skull, there they crucify him, and the criminals, one on his right and one on his left. The crucifixion is described in a single, understated clause. Luke does not dwell on the physical horror.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Crucifixion', 'explanation': "The most agonizing and degrading form of execution in the Roman world. The victim was stripped naked, nailed or tied to a cross, and left to die slowly from asphyxiation, exposure, and shock. Death could take hours or days. It was reserved for slaves, rebels, and the lowest criminals. Cicero called it 'the most cruel and disgusting penalty.'"}

verses.text:
Jesus said,
“Father, forgive them, for they don’t know what they are doing.”
Dividing his garments among them, they cast lots.
verses.explainer: And Jesus says: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.' And they cast lots to divide his garments. Found in many early manuscripts, this prayer of forgiveness from the cross is one of the most powerful statements in all of Scripture. Jesus practices what he preached: love your enemies, pray for those who abuse you (6:27-28).

thenVsNow.then: The prayer is radical: Jesus asks forgiveness for the soldiers who are crucifying him, the leaders who condemned him, and the crowd that demanded his death. He attributes their action to ignorance rather than malice.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has inspired countless acts of forgiveness throughout history. It has been quoted by martyrs, civil rights leaders, and ordinary people choosing to forgive the unforgivable. It establishes the principle that Christians are called to respond to the worst human behavior with prayer for the offenders.

verses.text: The people stood watching. The rulers with them also scoffed at him, saying, “He saved others. Let him save himself, if this is the Christ of God, his chosen one!”
verses.explainer: And the people stand by, watching, but the rulers scoff at him, saying: 'He saved others; let him save himself, if he is the Christ of God, his Chosen One!' The mockery uses correct theological language while completely misunderstanding its meaning.

verses.text: The soldiers also mocked him, coming to him and offering him vinegar,
verses.explainer: The soldiers also mock him, coming up and offering him sour wine.

verses.text: and saying, “If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself!”
verses.explainer: They say: 'If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself!' Each group mocks with the same logic: if you have power, use it for yourself.

verses.text: An inscription was also written over him in letters of Greek, Latin, and Hebrew: “THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS.”
verses.explainer: There is also an inscription over him: 'This is the King of the Jews.' The titulus was a placard stating the condemned person's crime. In Jesus' case, the 'crime' is a theological truth the Romans do not understand.

verses.text: One of the criminals who was hanged insulted him, saying, “If you are the Christ, save yourself and us!”
verses.explainer: One of the criminals who is hanged rails at him, saying: 'Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us!' Even a dying criminal joins the mockery.

verses.text: But the other answered, and rebuking him said, “Don’t you even fear God, seeing you are under the same condemnation?
verses.explainer: But the other rebukes him, saying: 'Do you not fear God, since you are under the same sentence of condemnation?' The second criminal recognizes their shared guilt and challenges the first to take his situation seriously.

verses.text: And we indeed justly, for we receive the due reward for our deeds, but this man has done nothing wrong.”
verses.explainer: 'And we indeed justly, for we are receiving the due reward of our deeds; but this man has done nothing wrong.' A confession of guilt combined with a declaration of Jesus' innocence. This criminal sees what Pilate, Herod, and the religious leaders would not.

verses.text: He said to Jesus, “Lord, remember me when you come into your Kingdom.”
verses.explainer: And he says: 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.' A remarkable confession of faith. Hanging on a cross next to a condemned man, he recognizes Jesus as a king with a coming kingdom. This is faith at its most radical -- trusting in a dying man's future reign.

verses.text:
Jesus said to him,
“Assuredly I tell you, today you will be with me in Paradise.”
verses.explainer: And Jesus says to him: 'Truly, I say to you, today you will be with me in paradise.' The promise is immediate ('today'), personal ('with me'), and glorious ('paradise'). The criminal who has nothing to offer -- no good works, no baptism, no years of discipleship -- receives the fullest promise of salvation.

thenVsNow.then: The repentant criminal has no time for any religious acts. He cannot be baptized, attend a synagogue, make restitution, or perform good works. He has only faith and a request. And it is enough.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to the Christian understanding of grace: salvation is received, not earned. It has comforted the dying for centuries and challenges any theology that makes salvation dependent on human performance. The thief on the cross is the ultimate example of salvation by grace through faith.

verses.text: It was now about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour.
verses.explainer: It is now about the sixth hour, and there is darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour. From noon to 3 PM, darkness covers the land -- a sign of cosmic mourning and divine judgment.

verses.text: The sun was darkened, and the veil of the temple was torn in two.
verses.explainer: The sun's light fails. And the curtain of the Temple is torn in two. The Temple veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place tears from top to bottom. The barrier between God and humanity is removed.

verses.text:
Jesus, crying with a loud voice, said,
“Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!” Having said this, he breathed his last.
verses.explainer: Then Jesus, calling out with a loud voice, says: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit!' And having said this he breathes his last. Jesus' final words are from Psalm 31:5 -- a psalm of trust. He dies not with a cry of despair but with an act of surrender to the Father.

verses.text: When the centurion saw what was done, he glorified God, saying, “Certainly this was a righteous man.”
verses.explainer: Now when the centurion sees what has happened, he praises God, saying: 'Certainly this man was innocent!' The Roman officer at the cross recognizes what the religious authorities refused to see: Jesus was righteous. Luke's centurion says 'innocent' (dikaios) where Mark's says 'Son of God' -- both are true.

thenVsNow.then: A Roman centurion -- a representative of the empire that executed Jesus -- declares his innocence. This is the ultimate testimony from the most unexpected source.
thenVsNow.now: The centurion's declaration continues the pattern in Luke of outsiders recognizing what insiders miss. The Roman soldier sees what the Jewish leaders refuse to acknowledge. Faith and perception are not limited to any one group.

verses.text: All the multitudes that came together to see this, when they saw the things that were done, returned home beating their breasts.
verses.explainer: And all the crowds who have assembled for this spectacle, when they see what has taken place, return home beating their breasts. The crowd that demanded crucifixion now mourns. The violence they demanded has produced grief, not satisfaction.

verses.text: All his acquaintances, and the women who followed with him from Galilee, stood at a distance, watching these things.
verses.explainer: And all his acquaintances and the women who had followed him from Galilee stood at a distance watching these things. The women who supported Jesus from the beginning (8:1-3) remain faithful at the end. The male disciples are absent; the women endure.

verses.text: Behold, a man named Joseph, who was a member of the council, a good and righteous man
verses.explainer: Now there is a man named Joseph, from the Jewish town of Arimathea. He is a member of the council, a good and righteous man.

verses.text: (he had not consented to their counsel and deed), from Arimathaea, a city of the Jews, who was also waiting for God’s Kingdom:
verses.explainer: He had not consented to their decision and action; and he was looking for the kingdom of God. A dissenting voice within the council -- not everyone agreed to condemn Jesus.

verses.text: this man went to Pilate, and asked for Jesus’ body.
verses.explainer: This man goes to Pilate and asks for the body of Jesus. A bold act: requesting the body of a condemned criminal from the Roman governor risked suspicion of disloyalty.

verses.text: He took it down, and wrapped it in a linen cloth, and laid him in a tomb that was cut in stone, where no one had ever been laid.
verses.explainer: Then he takes it down and wraps it in a linen shroud and lays him in a tomb cut in stone, where no one had ever yet been laid. The tomb is new -- unused. Jesus' burial fulfills the honor that his execution attempted to deny.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Rock-cut tomb', 'explanation': 'Wealthy families had tombs cut from solid rock outside the city walls. The body was placed on a stone shelf, anointed with spices, and sealed behind a large rolling stone. After decomposition, the bones were collected into an ossuary.'}

verses.text: It was the day of the Preparation, and the Sabbath was drawing near.
verses.explainer: It is the day of Preparation, and the Sabbath is beginning. Time is running out. The Sabbath begins at sundown Friday, and no work (including burial preparation) can be done during it.

verses.text: The women, who had come with him out of Galilee, followed after, and saw the tomb, and how his body was laid.
verses.explainer: The women who have come with him from Galilee follow and see the tomb and how his body is laid. The women observe everything -- they will know exactly where to return.

verses.text: They returned, and prepared spices and ointments. On the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.
verses.explainer: Then they return and prepare spices and ointments. On the Sabbath they rest according to the commandment. Even in their grief, they observe the Sabbath. The irony: they rest on the Sabbath while Jesus rests in the tomb -- the ultimate Sabbath rest before the new creation begins.

explainer.summary: On Sunday morning, women find the tomb empty and receive angelic announcement of the resurrection. Peter confirms the empty tomb. Two disciples encounter the risen Jesus on the road to Emmaus in one of the most beautifully narrated scenes in Scripture -- they recognize him only in the breaking of bread, and he vanishes. They rush back to Jerusalem, where Jesus appears to all the disciples, eats fish to prove his physicality, opens their minds to understand the Scriptures, commissions them as witnesses, and ascends into heaven. The Gospel that began in the Temple ends with the disciples worshiping there with great joy.
themes: The reality of the bodily resurrection
Women as the first witnesses
Christ revealed in Scripture and in the breaking of bread
The Emmaus road as a model for encountering the risen Christ
The Great Commission to all nations
Joy as the final note of the Gospel
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 24 is the climax and resolution of everything Luke has narrated. Every theme converges: the journey reaches its true destination (not the cross but the resurrection), the meals where Jesus was known find their fulfillment (Emmaus), the Scripture he taught is finally understood, and the mission to all nations is launched. Luke's Gospel begins and ends in the Temple, in worship and joy.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A village about seven miles from Jerusalem
wordStudies.meaning: A village about seven miles from Jerusalem. Its exact location is debated. The Emmaus road story is unique to Luke and has become one of the most beloved resurrection narratives in Christianity -- a story about how the risen Christ walks with people who do not yet recognize him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To open up, to open the mind
wordStudies.meaning: To open up, to open the mind. Used twice in this chapter: Jesus 'opened' the Scriptures to the Emmaus disciples (24:32) and 'opened' the minds of the Eleven to understand (24:45). Resurrection understanding requires divine illumination -- the text is insufficient without the Spirit's work.

verses.text: But on the first day of the week, at early dawn, they and some others came to the tomb, bringing the spices which they had prepared.
verses.explainer: But on the first day of the week, at early dawn, they went to the tomb, taking the spices they had prepared. The women resume their interrupted burial preparations at the earliest possible moment after the Sabbath ends.

verses.text: They found the stone rolled away from the tomb.
verses.explainer: And they find the stone rolled away from the tomb. The first surprise: the heavy sealing stone has been moved.

verses.text: They entered in, and didn’t find the Lord Jesus’ body.
verses.explainer: But when they go in they do not find the body of the Lord Jesus. The second surprise: the body is gone. Luke adds the title 'the Lord Jesus' -- a resurrection faith designation.

verses.text: While they were greatly perplexed about this, behold, two men stood by them in dazzling clothing.
verses.explainer: While they are perplexed about this, behold, two men stand by them in dazzling apparel. Angels in human form, as at the Transfiguration. Their appearance is unmistakably supernatural.

verses.text:
Becoming terrified, they bowed their faces down to the earth.
They said to them, “Why do you seek the living among the dead?
verses.explainer: And as they are frightened and bow their faces to the ground, the men say to them: 'Why do you seek the living among the dead?' A question that reframes everything. Jesus is not here because he is alive. You are looking in the wrong place.

thenVsNow.then: The angelic question gently rebukes the women's assumption that Jesus is still dead. They came to anoint a corpse; they are told they are looking for a living person in a graveyard. The resurrection changes the entire category of the search.
thenVsNow.now: The question 'Why do you seek the living among the dead?' has become a signature Easter proclamation in Christian worship. It challenges every attempt to confine Jesus to the past or to treat faith as a memorial exercise rather than a living encounter.

verses.text: He isn’t here, but is risen. Remember what he told you when he was still in Galilee,
verses.explainer: 'He is not here, but has risen. Remember how he told you, while he was still in Galilee...' The angels point back to Jesus' own predictions. He said this would happen.

verses.text: saying that the Son of Man must be delivered up into the hands of sinful men, and be crucified, and the third day rise again?”
verses.explainer: '...that the Son of Man must be delivered into the hands of sinful men and be crucified and on the third day rise.' The passion predictions of 9:22, 9:44, and 18:31-33 are recalled. Everything happened as Jesus said it would.

verses.text: They remembered his words,
verses.explainer: And they remember his words. Memory is activated by the resurrection. What was incomprehensible before (9:45, 18:34) now becomes clear.

verses.text: returned from the tomb, and told all these things to the eleven, and to all the rest.
verses.explainer: And returning from the tomb they tell all these things to the eleven and to all the rest. The women become the first witnesses and proclaimers of the resurrection.

verses.text: Now they were Mary Magdalene, Joanna, and Mary the mother of James. The other women with them told these things to the apostles.
verses.explainer: Now it was Mary Magdalene and Joanna and Mary the mother of James and the other women with them who told these things to the apostles. Luke names the women witnesses -- a detail of historical honesty, since women's testimony was not legally valid in first-century Judaism.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Women as resurrection witnesses', 'explanation': 'Women could not serve as legal witnesses in Jewish courts. If the resurrection story were fabricated, no one would have chosen women as the first witnesses. Their central role is a strong argument for historical authenticity -- the early church reported what happened, even though it was culturally embarrassing.'}

verses.text: These words seemed to them to be nonsense, and they didn’t believe them.
verses.explainer: But these words seem to them an idle tale, and they do not believe them. The male disciples dismiss the women's report as nonsense. The Greek word (leros) means 'delirium' or 'foolish talk.'

verses.text: But Peter got up and ran to the tomb. Stooping and looking in, he saw the strips of linen lying by themselves, and he departed to his home, wondering what had happened.
verses.explainer: But Peter rises and runs to the tomb; stooping and looking in, he sees the linen cloths by themselves; and he goes home marveling at what had happened. Peter sees physical evidence -- the burial wrappings without a body -- but does not yet understand.

verses.text: Behold, two of them were going that very day to a village named Emmaus, which was sixty stadia from Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: That very day two of them are going to a village named Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. The Emmaus road narrative begins -- one of the most exquisite stories in all of Scripture, unique to Luke.

thenVsNow.then: The two disciples are leaving Jerusalem -- walking away from the center of events. They have given up hope. Their journey represents the universal experience of despair after devastating loss.
thenVsNow.now: The Emmaus road has become a metaphor for the Christian life: walking in confusion and grief, unknowingly accompanied by the risen Christ, who reveals himself in Scripture and in the breaking of bread. Countless retreat centers, spiritual programs, and books take their name from this story.

verses.text: They talked with each other about all of these things which had happened.
verses.explainer: And they are talking with each other about all these things that have happened. They are processing their grief through conversation.

verses.text: While they talked and questioned together, Jesus himself came near, and went with them.
verses.explainer: While they are talking and discussing together, Jesus himself draws near and walks with them. The risen Jesus joins them on the road. He comes to them in their confusion, not waiting for them to find him.

verses.text: But their eyes were kept from recognizing him.
verses.explainer: But their eyes are held so that they did not recognize him. They are prevented from recognizing Jesus. The risen body is the same person but different -- recognizable only when he chooses to reveal himself.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“What are you talking about as you walk, and are sad?”
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'What is this conversation that you are holding with each other as you walk?' And they stand still, looking sad. Jesus asks the question that will draw out their pain so he can address it.

verses.text: One of them, named Cleopas, answered him, “Are you the only stranger in Jerusalem who doesn’t know the things which have happened there in these days?”
verses.explainer: Then one of them, named Cleopas, answers him: 'Are you the only visitor to Jerusalem who does not know the things that have happened there in these days?' The irony is devastating: the main character in the story is asked if he has heard about it.

And he says to them: 'What things?' And they say to him: 'Concerning Jesus of Nazareth, a man who was a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people.' They describe Jesus as a prophet but not yet as the risen Lord.

verses.text: and how the chief priests and our rulers delivered him up to be condemned to death, and crucified him.
verses.explainer: 'And how our chief priests and rulers delivered him up to be condemned to death, and crucified him.' The basic facts of the passion, told from the perspective of crushed hope.

verses.text: But we were hoping that it was he who would redeem Israel. Yes, and besides all this, it is now the third day since these things happened.
verses.explainer: 'But we had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel. Yes, and besides all this, it is now the third day since these things happened.' 'We had hoped' -- past tense. Their hope has died with Jesus. The mention of the third day shows they know his prediction but do not believe it has been fulfilled.

verses.text: Also, certain women of our company amazed us, having arrived early at the tomb;
verses.explainer: 'Moreover, some women of our company amazed us. They were at the tomb early in the morning.' They know about the women's report.

verses.text: and when they didn’t find his body, they came saying that they had also seen a vision of angels, who said that he was alive.
verses.explainer: 'And when they did not find his body, they came back saying that they had even seen a vision of angels, who said that he was alive.' They relay the report but interpret it as a 'vision' -- not reality.

verses.text: Some of us went to the tomb, and found it just like the women had said, but they didn’t see him.”
verses.explainer: 'Some of those who were with us went to the tomb and found it just as the women had said, but him they did not see.' The tomb is confirmed empty, but no one has seen Jesus. The evidence is suggestive but not conclusive.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Foolish men, and slow of heart to believe in all that the prophets have spoken!
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'O foolish ones, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken!' Jesus rebukes their failure to believe Scripture. The evidence was in the Bible all along.

verses.text: Didn’t the Christ have to suffer these things and to enter into his glory?”
verses.explainer: 'Was it not necessary that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?' The key theological insight: suffering is not the failure of messiahship but the path to it. The cross is not a detour but the road.

thenVsNow.then: The disciples expected a Messiah who would conquer, not suffer. Jesus insists that the entire Old Testament, rightly read, points to a suffering Messiah who enters glory through death. This was a revolutionary hermeneutical shift.
thenVsNow.now: This verse has shaped Christian biblical interpretation for two millennia. Reading the Old Testament as pointing to a suffering and glorified Christ remains the foundational Christian approach to the Hebrew Scriptures.

verses.text: Beginning from Moses and from all the prophets, he explained to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.
verses.explainer: And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he interprets to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself. The greatest Bible study in history. Jesus walks through the entire Old Testament, showing how every part points to him. Luke does not record the specifics -- the reader is invited to search the Scriptures and discover them.

verses.text: They came near to the village, where they were going, and he acted like he would go further.
verses.explainer: So they draw near to the village to which they are going. He acts as if he is going farther. Jesus does not force his presence. He waits to be invited.

verses.text:
They urged him, saying, “Stay with us, for it is almost evening, and the day is almost over.”
He went in to stay with them.
verses.explainer: But they urge him strongly, saying: 'Stay with us, for it is toward evening and the day is now far spent.' So he goes in to stay with them. Their hospitality becomes the occasion for revelation.

verses.text: When he had sat down at the table with them, he took the bread and gave thanks. Breaking it, he gave it to them.
verses.explainer: When he is at table with them, he takes the bread and blesses and breaks it and gives it to them. The four eucharistic verbs from the feeding of the 5,000 (9:16) and the Last Supper (22:19): took, blessed, broke, gave. In the breaking of bread, recognition comes.

verses.text: Their eyes were opened, and they recognized him, and he vanished out of their sight.
verses.explainer: And their eyes are opened, and they recognize him. And he vanishes from their sight. The moment of recognition and the moment of disappearance are simultaneous. He is known in the breaking of bread and then is no longer physically present. Faith takes over from sight.

thenVsNow.then: The Emmaus disciples recognize Jesus in the same gesture that defined his ministry: breaking bread with others. The meals that Luke has emphasized throughout his Gospel -- with tax collectors, Pharisees, disciples, thousands -- find their culmination here.
thenVsNow.now: The Eucharist (Lord's Supper, Communion) is understood by many Christians as a continuation of this Emmaus experience: Christ present in the breaking of bread, recognized by faith even when not physically visible. The story shapes sacramental theology across traditions.

verses.text: They said to one another, “Weren’t our hearts burning within us, while he spoke to us along the way, and while he opened the Scriptures to us?”
verses.explainer: They say to each other: 'Did not our hearts burn within us while he talked to us on the road, while he opened to us the Scriptures?' The burning heart -- the inner witness that they were in the presence of truth even before their eyes were opened. Scripture and presence together produce this fire.

verses.text: They rose up that very hour, returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, and those who were with them,
verses.explainer: And they rise that same hour and return to Jerusalem. And they find the eleven and those who are with them gathered together. They do not wait until morning. The seven-mile journey back to Jerusalem in the dark is fueled by resurrection joy.

verses.text: saying, “The Lord is risen indeed, and has appeared to Simon!”
verses.explainer: The group says: 'The Lord has risen indeed, and has appeared to Simon!' Before the Emmaus disciples can share their news, they learn that Peter has also seen the risen Jesus. The testimony multiplies.

verses.text: They related the things that happened along the way, and how he was recognized by them in the breaking of the bread.
verses.explainer: Then they tell what happened on the road, and how he was known to them in the breaking of the bread. Two independent resurrection encounters in one day: Peter's and the Emmaus road.

verses.text:
As they said these things, Jesus himself stood among them, and said to them,
“Peace be to you.”
verses.explainer: As they are talking about these things, Jesus himself stands among them, and says to them: 'Peace to you!' Jesus appears to the gathered community. His first word is 'Peace' -- the shalom that encompasses wholeness, well-being, and reconciliation.

verses.text: But they were terrified and filled with fear, and supposed that they had seen a spirit.
verses.explainer: But they are startled and frightened and think they see a spirit. Despite the reports from the women, Peter, and the Emmaus disciples, they are terrified. Resurrection is not what they expected.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Why are you troubled? Why do doubts arise in your hearts?
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'Why are you troubled, and why do doubts arise in your hearts?' Jesus addresses their fear and doubt directly.

verses.text: See my hands and my feet, that it is truly me. Touch me and see, for a spirit doesn’t have flesh and bones, as you see that I have.”
verses.explainer: 'See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself. Touch me, and see. For a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see that I have.' The risen Jesus has a physical body -- hands, feet, flesh, bones. The resurrection is bodily, not merely spiritual.

verses.text: When he had said this, he showed them his hands and his feet.
verses.explainer: And when he has said this, he shows them his hands and his feet. The nail wounds are still visible -- marks of crucifixion on the risen body. The wounds are not erased but transformed.

verses.text:
While they still didn’t believe for joy, and wondered, he said to them,
“Do you have anything here to eat?”
verses.explainer: And while they still disbelieve for joy and are marveling, he says to them: 'Have you anything here to eat?' They 'disbelieve for joy' -- the news is too good to be true. Jesus asks for food to prove his physicality.

verses.text: They gave him a piece of a broiled fish and some honeycomb.
verses.explainer: They give him a piece of broiled fish. Ordinary food for an extraordinary moment.

verses.text: He took them, and ate in front of them.
verses.explainer: And he takes it and eats before them. A ghost does not eat fish. The resurrection body is real, physical, and capable of eating. Jesus' final meal in Luke is not the Last Supper but broiled fish with his disciples.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“This is what I told you, while I was still with you, that all things which are written in the law of Moses, the prophets, and the psalms, concerning me must be fulfilled.”
verses.explainer: Then he says to them: 'These are my words that I spoke to you while I was still with you, that everything written about me in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled.' The three divisions of the Hebrew Bible (Torah, Prophets, Writings/Psalms) all point to Jesus.

thenVsNow.then: By citing all three sections of the Hebrew Bible, Jesus claims that the entire Jewish Scripture finds its fulfillment in his life, death, and resurrection. This is the most comprehensive hermeneutical claim in the Gospels.
thenVsNow.now: This verse grounds the Christian practice of reading the Old Testament christologically -- finding Jesus throughout the Hebrew Scriptures. The three-fold division (Law, Prophets, Psalms) covers everything from Genesis to Chronicles in the Hebrew canonical order.

verses.text: Then he opened their minds, that they might understand the Scriptures.
verses.explainer: Then he opens their minds to understand the Scriptures. Understanding requires divine illumination. The text alone is insufficient without the Spirit's work of opening the mind.

verses.text:
He said to them,
“Thus it is written, and thus it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead the third day,
verses.explainer: And he says to them: 'Thus it is written, that the Christ should suffer and on the third day rise from the dead.' The pattern of suffering and vindication -- the same thing he taught on the Emmaus road -- is stated as the summary of all Scripture.

verses.text: and that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name to all the nations, beginning at Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: 'And that repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem.' The Great Commission in Luke: repentance and forgiveness, proclaimed to all nations, starting from Jerusalem. This is the program for the book of Acts.

thenVsNow.then: The phrase 'all nations' (panta ta ethne) includes every people group -- Gentiles as well as Jews. The mission that begins in Jerusalem will spread to Judea, Samaria, and the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). Luke's two-volume work traces exactly this expansion.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is foundational for the global missionary movement. The commission to proclaim repentance and forgiveness to all nations has driven the geographic expansion of Christianity from a small Palestinian sect to a worldwide faith encompassing every continent and culture.

verses.text: You are witnesses of these things.
verses.explainer: 'You are witnesses of these things.' The disciples' role is defined: they are witnesses. Their authority rests not on theological training but on personal experience of the risen Jesus.

verses.text: Behold, I send out the promise of my Father on you. But wait in the city of Jerusalem until you are clothed with power from on high.”
verses.explainer: 'And behold, I am sending the promise of my Father upon you. But stay in the city until you are clothed with power from on high.' The promise of the Holy Spirit. They must wait in Jerusalem for divine empowerment before beginning their mission. This promise is fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2).

verses.text: He led them out as far as Bethany, and he lifted up his hands, and blessed them.
verses.explainer: And he leads them out as far as Bethany, and lifting up his hands he blesses them. Jesus' final act is blessing. The Gospel ends as it began -- with a priestly figure raising hands in blessing (cf. Zechariah in the Temple, 1:22).

verses.text: While he blessed them, he withdrew from them, and was carried up into heaven.
verses.explainer: While he blesses them, he parts from them and is carried up into heaven. The ascension -- Jesus' departure from visible, physical presence. He leaves while blessing, so the blessing continues indefinitely.

verses.text: They worshiped him, and returned to Jerusalem with great joy,
verses.explainer: And they worship him and return to Jerusalem with great joy. Joy -- Luke's signature theme -- is the final note. They worship and they rejoice. The Gospel that began with an angel announcing joy (1:14, 2:10) ends with the disciples experiencing it.

thenVsNow.then: The disciples' joy seems counterintuitive: Jesus has left them. But they understand now that his departure is not loss but the beginning of a new phase. He has ascended to the Father's right hand, and the Spirit is coming.
thenVsNow.now: The ending of Luke's Gospel establishes the paradox at the heart of Christian experience: Jesus is physically absent but spiritually present. The church lives between the ascension and the return, sustained by the same joy that filled the first disciples.

verses.text: and were continually in the temple, praising and blessing God. Amen.
verses.explainer: And they are continually in the Temple blessing God. The Gospel ends where it began: in the Temple. Zechariah entered the Temple in fear and silence (1:9-22); the disciples fill the Temple with praise and joy. The circle is complete. The story of Jesus has changed everything -- and the story of the church is about to begin.

thenVsNow.then: Luke's Gospel begins in the Temple with Zechariah's priestly service and ends in the Temple with the disciples' continuous worship. This literary inclusio (bookend structure) emphasizes that Jesus is the fulfillment of Temple worship -- what the Temple pointed to has arrived.
thenVsNow.now: The final verse of Luke's Gospel points forward to Acts, where the disciples will move from the Temple to the world. The worship that begins in Jerusalem will extend to every nation, tongue, and people. The story is not over; it is just beginning.
